title: Task 3: Add cannot-cast-spells restriction checking to can-cast?
design:
## Goal
Extend the can-cast? function in rules.cljs to check for :cannot-cast-spells restrictions on the player before allowing any spell to be cast.

## Effort Estimate
3-4 hours

## Context
Completed fizzle-d3y: Player grants system with :player/grants attribute
Completed fizzle-w0x: :add-restriction effect creates :restriction grants on players

Now we need the enforcement point: can-cast? should check if the player has a :cannot-cast-spells restriction grant before allowing casting.

## Implementation

### 1. Study can-cast? function
**VERIFIED**: can-cast? is at rules.cljs:139-157
- Takes: db, player-id, object-id
- Returns: boolean
- Current flow: get-casting-modes → check can-cast-mode? → check targets
- Restriction check should be FIRST (before modes/mana checks)

### 2. Write tests first (TDD)

Add tests to rules_test.cljs. Use existing test helpers or create minimal ones.

**Test helper needed:**
```clojure
(defn add-player-restriction
  "Add a restriction grant to a player for testing.
   Creates grant expiring at turn 99 (effectively permanent for tests)."
  [db player-id restriction-type]
  (let [grant {:grant/id (random-uuid)
               :grant/type :restriction
               :grant/source nil
               :grant/expires {:expires/turn 99 :expires/phase :cleanup}
               :grant/data {:restriction/type restriction-type}}]
    (grants/add-player-grant db player-id grant)))
```

**Tests:**
```clojure
;; Test 1: Player with :cannot-cast-spells restriction cannot cast spells
(deftest cannot-cast-with-restriction-test
  (testing "player with :cannot-cast-spells restriction cannot cast any spell"
    (let [db (-> (init-game-state)
                 (add-player-restriction :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells))
          ;; init-game-state adds Dark Ritual to hand with mana
          hand (q/get-hand db :player-1)
          object-id (:object/id (first hand))]
      (is (not (rules/can-cast? db :player-1 object-id))
          "Should not be able to cast Dark Ritual with restriction"))))

;; Test 2: Player without restriction can cast normally
(deftest can-cast-without-restriction-test
  (testing "player without restriction can cast spells normally"
    (let [db (init-game-state)
          ;; Add mana to cast
          db (mana/add-mana db :player-1 {:black 1})
          hand (q/get-hand db :player-1)
          object-id (:object/id (first hand))]
      (is (rules/can-cast? db :player-1 object-id)
          "Should be able to cast Dark Ritual without restriction"))))

;; Test 3: Restriction on opponent doesn't affect player
(deftest restriction-on-opponent-doesnt-affect-player-test
  (testing "restriction on opponent doesn't prevent player from casting"
    (let [db (-> (init-game-state)
                 (add-opponent)
                 (add-player-restriction :player-2 :cannot-cast-spells))
          db (mana/add-mana db :player-1 {:black 1})
          hand (q/get-hand db :player-1)
          object-id (:object/id (first hand))]
      (is (rules/can-cast? db :player-1 object-id)
          "Player 1 should still be able to cast"))))

;; Test 4: Expired restriction allows casting
(deftest expired-restriction-allows-casting-test
  (testing "after restriction expires, player can cast again"
    (let [db (-> (init-game-state)
                 (add-player-restriction-with-expiry :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells 1 :cleanup))
          db (mana/add-mana db :player-1 {:black 1})
          ;; Expire grants (turn 2, cleanup)
          db' (grants/expire-grants db 2 :cleanup)
          hand (q/get-hand db' :player-1)
          object-id (:object/id (first hand))]
      (is (rules/can-cast? db' :player-1 object-id)
          "Should be able to cast after restriction expires"))))

;; Test 5: has-restriction? returns true for multiple grants
(deftest has-restriction-multiple-grants-test
  (testing "has-restriction? returns true when multiple restriction grants exist"
    (let [db (-> (init-game-state)
                 (add-player-restriction :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells)
                 (add-player-restriction :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells))]
      (is (rules/has-restriction? db :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells)
          "Should find restriction among multiple grants"))))

;; Test 6: has-restriction? returns false for different restriction type
(deftest has-restriction-different-type-test
  (testing "has-restriction? returns false when restriction is different type"
    (let [db (-> (init-game-state)
                 (add-player-restriction :player-1 :cannot-attack))]
      (is (not (rules/has-restriction? db :player-1 :cannot-cast-spells))
          "Should not find :cannot-cast-spells when only :cannot-attack exists"))))
```

### 3. Implementation checklist
- [ ] rules.cljs line 6-11 - Add require for grants namespace:
      ```clojure
      [fizzle.engine.grants :as grants]
      ```
- [ ] rules.cljs - Add has-restriction? function (before can-cast?):
      ```clojure
      (defn has-restriction?
        "Check if a player has an active restriction of the given type.
         Returns true if restriction exists, false otherwise.
         
         Used by can-cast? to enforce player restrictions like 'cannot cast spells'.
         
         Edge cases:
         - Non-existent player: returns false (no grants)
         - Multiple matching grants: returns true (finds first match)
         - Different restriction type: returns false (specific matching)"
        [db player-id restriction-type]
        (let [restriction-grants (grants/get-player-grants-by-type db player-id :restriction)]
          (boolean
            (some #(= restriction-type (get-in % [:grant/data :restriction/type]))
                  restriction-grants))))
      ```
- [ ] rules.cljs:139-157 - Modify can-cast? to check restriction FIRST:
      ```clojure
      (defn can-cast?
        "Check if a player can cast a card.
      
         Checks if ANY valid casting mode is castable.
         A mode is valid if:
         - Player does not have :cannot-cast-spells restriction
         - The card is in the mode's required zone
         - Player can pay the mode's mana cost
         - Player can pay all additional costs
         - Valid targets exist for all required targeting
      
         Returns false if object doesn't exist or no modes are castable."
        [db player-id object-id]
        ;; Check player restrictions FIRST (before any other checks)
        (if (has-restriction? db player-id :cannot-cast-spells)
          false
          (if-let [obj (q/get-object db object-id)]
            (let [card (:object/card obj)
                  modes (get-casting-modes db player-id object-id)
                  has-mode (boolean (some #(can-cast-mode? db player-id object-id %) modes))
                  has-targets (targeting/has-valid-targets? db player-id card)]
              (and has-mode has-targets))
            false)))
      ```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] has-restriction? function added to rules.cljs
- [ ] can-cast? checks for :cannot-cast-spells restriction BEFORE other checks
- [ ] Player with restriction cannot cast any spells (can-cast? returns false)
- [ ] Player without restriction can cast normally
- [ ] Restriction on opponent doesn't affect player's casting
- [ ] Expired restrictions no longer prevent casting
- [ ] Different restriction type doesn't block casting
- [ ] Multiple restrictions still detected
- [ ] All existing tests pass (618+)
- [ ] `make validate` passes (lint, fmt, tests)

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO hardcoding Orim's Chant logic (use generic restriction checking)
- ❌ NO modifying get-castable-cards separately (can-cast? is the enforcement point)
- ❌ NO checking restrictions after mana checks (restriction should be first check - more efficient)
- ❌ NO using count instead of some (multiple grants should work correctly)
- ❌ NO forgetting to require grants namespace

## Key Considerations (Edge Cases)

**Edge Case: Multiple restrictions from different sources**
- Player could have multiple :cannot-cast-spells grants (from different Orim's Chants)
- has-restriction? should return true if ANY matching restriction exists
- Use (some ...) pattern with boolean coercion
- Test: Add 2 restrictions, verify still blocked

**Edge Case: Player doesn't exist**
- get-player-grants-by-type returns [] for non-existent player
- some on [] returns nil (falsy)
- boolean coercion ensures consistent false return
- This is correct behavior - non-existent player has no restrictions

**Edge Case: Different restriction type**
- Player with :cannot-attack should still be able to cast
- has-restriction? checks specific restriction-type
- Test: Add :cannot-attack, verify casting still works

**Edge Case: Lands bypass can-cast?**
- Lands use play-land event, not can-cast?
- :cannot-cast-spells should NOT prevent land plays (correct MTG rules)
- No changes needed - play-land doesn't call can-cast?

**Performance Note: Check restriction first**
- Restriction check is O(n) where n = player grants (typically 0-2)
- Much faster than mana calculations and mode checks
- Check restriction FIRST for early exit on blocked players

**Reference Implementation**
- grants.cljs:113-118 - get-player-grants-by-type (already implemented)
- effects.cljs:530-570 - :add-restriction effect (already implemented)
- rules.cljs:139-157 - Current can-cast? implementation
close_reason: Implemented has-restriction? function and modified can-cast? to check restrictions before allowing casting. All tests pass.

title: Static Abilities: Cost Modifier Cards (Sphere of Resistance, Defense Grid, Chill)
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- Engine supports static abilities on permanent cards via `:card/static-abilities` field (vector of data maps)
- Static ability framework uses query-at-check-time pattern: scan battlefield permanents when a rule needs evaluation, no grant lifecycle management
- Cost modifier static ability type (`:cost-modifier`) modifies spell casting costs
- Sphere of Resistance implemented: {2} Artifact, all spells cost {1} more to cast
- Defense Grid implemented: {2} Artifact, spells cost {3} more except during caster's turn
- Chill implemented: {1}{U} Enchantment, red spells cost {2} more to cast
- Cost modifiers apply to ALL casting costs (normal, flashback, alternate) — they modify the effective cost after base cost is determined
- Cost modifiers stack additively (two Spheres = {2} more)
- Defense Grid checks active player vs caster: applies only when spell is cast NOT during caster's own turn
- Chill checks `:card/colors` on the spell being cast for `:red` membership
- Card spec validates `:card/static-abilities` field and its sub-structures

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] `engine/static_abilities.cljs` exists with query functions for scanning battlefield
- [ ] `compute-effective-mana-cost` / `apply-cost-modifiers` correctly sums all applicable cost increases
- [ ] `can-cast-mode?` uses effective cost (not raw mode cost) when static abilities are present
- [ ] `cast-spell-mode` pays effective cost
- [ ] Mana allocation pre-cast step uses effective cost
- [ ] Sphere of Resistance: card definition, test file with all mandatory categories (A-D + G)
- [ ] Defense Grid: card definition, test file with all mandatory categories (A-D + G), active player condition tests
- [ ] Chill: card definition, test file with all mandatory categories (A-D + G), color check tests
- [ ] Cost modifiers work with flashback casting
- [ ] Multiple cost modifiers stack correctly
- [ ] Card spec validates `:card/static-abilities` field
- [ ] All tests passing (`make validate`)
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO grant-based lifecycle for static abilities (architecture: query-at-check-time pattern chosen to avoid ETB/LTB hooks and bidirectional lifecycle complexity)
- ❌ NO hardcoded card-name checks in engine code (data-over-code: engine interprets `:card/static-abilities` data, never checks card names)
- ❌ NO mutable state for tracking active static abilities (immutability: derive from battlefield state at query time)
- ❌ NO modifying existing card definitions to add static abilities (scope: only new cards get static abilities)
- ❌ NO tautological test assertions like `(is (some? x))` (testing: assert exact values per CLAUDE.md)
- ❌ NO reimplementing production handlers in tests (testing: use th/cast-and-resolve, th/cast-with-target, etc.)
- ❌ NO separate cost-modifier code paths for flashback vs normal casting (simplicity: apply-cost-modifiers takes any base cost from the mode system)

## Approach
Query-at-check-time static ability framework. Cards declare `:card/static-abilities` as EDN data. New `engine/static_abilities.cljs` scans battlefield permanents and evaluates static abilities when rules need checking. For cost modifiers: a central `apply-cost-modifiers` function is called in `can-cast-mode?`, `cast-spell-mode`, `cast-spell-mode-with-allocation`, and the mana allocation pre-cast step.

The framework supports extensible `:static/type` dispatch — only `:cost-modifier` is implemented in this epic. Future types (`:zone-permission`, `:rule-modifier`, `:grant-keyword`, etc.) hook into their own relevant engine check points.

Cost modifiers support:
- `:modifier/amount` — generic mana to add (map, e.g. `{:generic 1}`)
- `:modifier/direction` — `:increase` (future: `:decrease` for Goblin Warchief)
- `:modifier/condition` — game-state conditions evaluated at cast time (e.g. `:not-casters-turn`)
- `:modifier/criteria` — spell-matching criteria (e.g. spell color, spell type)

## Architecture
- `engine/static_abilities.cljs` — NEW: query functions, modifier evaluation, `apply-cost-modifiers`
- `engine/rules.cljs` — MODIFY: `can-cast-mode?`, `cast-spell-mode`, `cast-spell-mode-with-allocation` to use effective costs
- `engine/card_spec.cljs` — MODIFY: add `:card/static-abilities` spec
- `cards/artifacts/sphere_of_resistance.cljs` — NEW card definition
- `cards/artifacts/defense_grid.cljs` — NEW card definition
- `cards/blue/chill.cljs` — NEW card definition
- `cards/registry.cljs` — MODIFY: require new card namespaces
- Pre-cast pipeline mana allocation step — MODIFY: use effective cost

## Design Rationale
### Problem
The engine has no mechanism for permanents with ongoing rule-modifying effects (static abilities). All existing permanents sacrifice themselves. Three key hate piece cards (Sphere of Resistance, Defense Grid, Chill) require cost modification — a permanent on the battlefield making spells cost more.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- `engine/rules.cljs:158-166` — `can-cast-mode?` checks raw `(:mode/mana-cost mode)`, needs effective cost
- `engine/rules.cljs:336-355` — `cast-spell-mode` pays raw mode cost, needs effective cost
- `engine/grants.cljs` — Has `:static-effect` grant type documented but unused; decided against grant approach
- `engine/costs.cljs` — Per-card additional costs (pay-life, exile, etc.) — separate from battlefield cost modifiers
- `engine/effects.cljs:754-786` — `:add-restriction` creates player grants, but restrictions are binary (can/cannot), not additive cost changes
- No permanent ETB/LTB system exists for spell-type permanents (only `play-land` registers triggers)

**External:**
- MTG Rule 601.2f: Total cost = (base cost OR alternate cost) + increases - reductions
- Cost increases stack additively (two Spheres = {2} more)
- Cost increases apply to alternate costs (flashback)
- Defense Grid: 'its controller's turn' means the spell's caster's turn

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Query-at-check-time ✓
**What it is:** Scan battlefield for permanents with `:card/static-abilities` at the moment a rule needs checking. No lifecycle management. Static ability is active iff permanent is on battlefield.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed 5 future static ability types (zone-permission, rule-modifier, grant-keyword, player-attribute, damage-prevention)
- All follow same pattern: query battlefield at the relevant check point
- No bidirectional lifecycle hooks needed for any case

**Pros:**
- One consistent pattern for ALL static ability types
- No lifecycle management (ETB/LTB hooks not needed)
- Naturally handles conditional effects (Defense Grid) — condition evaluated at check time with full context
- Matches actual MTG rules model (continuous effects derived from battlefield state)

**Cons:**
- Scans battlefield on each cast check (trivial for practice tool scale)

**Chosen because:** Simplest, most correct, one pattern everywhere. Scales to all future static ability types without additional infrastructure.

#### 2. Grant-based registration ❌
**What it is:** Register `:static-effect` grants when permanent enters battlefield. Remove when it leaves. Check grants instead of scanning.

**Why we looked at this:** Grants are an existing system; seemed natural to extend.

**Investigation:**
- Simple player-level effects (Exploration, player shroud) work cleanly
- 'Grant keyword to matching permanents' (Sterling Grove, Warchief haste) requires bidirectional lifecycle hooks
- Conditional effects (Defense Grid) still need cast-time evaluation — grants add lifecycle overhead for same work
- Permanent ETB/LTB hooks don't exist; building them adds significant infrastructure

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Bidirectional lifecycle complexity for permanent-targeting effects. Requires ETB/LTB system that doesn't exist. No simpler than query for ANY case, strictly more complex for several.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** We're building a full permanent lifecycle system for other reasons (creatures/combat epic).

#### 3. Hybrid (grants for player-level, query for permanent-level) ❌
**What it is:** Use grants for simple player modifications, queries for permanent-targeting effects.

**Why we looked at this:** Grants are intuitive for simple cases like Exploration.

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Two patterns for same concept adds cognitive overhead. 'How does this card work?' shouldn't require knowing which subsystem it uses.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Query performance becomes measurably problematic (extremely unlikely at practice tool scale).

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Static ability framework (`:card/static-abilities` field, query infrastructure)
- Cost modifier type (`:cost-modifier`) with increase direction
- Three cards: Sphere of Resistance, Defense Grid, Chill
- Card spec validation for static abilities
- Integration into all cast flow check points

**Out of scope (deferred):**
- Cost DECREASE direction (Goblin Warchief) — future card epic
- Other static ability types (:zone-permission, :rule-modifier, :grant-keyword, etc.) — future epics
- Permanent ETB/LTB system — not needed for query approach
- UI display of modified costs — practice tool, player tracks mentally

### Open Questions
- Should `can-cast?` subscriptions in views also reflect modified costs? (Default: yes, via same `can-cast-mode?` path)
- Should bot `choose-action` account for cost modifiers? (Default: yes, via same `can-cast?` path)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Scope: just cost mods or general framework? | General static ability framework | Build extensible `:card/static-abilities` with typed dispatch |
| Defense Grid: full or simplified? | Full active player check | Need `:not-casters-turn` condition evaluation |
| Color check for Chill? | Use `:card/colors` field | Already on every card, check set membership |
| Architecture: query vs grant vs hybrid? | Query-based everywhere | One pattern, no lifecycle management |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### Static Ability Architecture
**Question explored:** Does query-at-check-time scale to all static ability types?
**Sources consulted:** Conceptual analysis of Future Sight, Exploration, Sterling Grove, Goblin Warchief, Solitary Confinement

**Findings:**
- All 5 future types work with query pattern
- Grant approach fails for 'grant to matching permanents' (Sterling Grove) — needs bidirectional hooks
- Grant approach adds lifecycle overhead for conditional effects with no simplification
- Query approach is simpler or equal in ALL cases

**Conclusion:** Query-based everywhere, one pattern for all static ability types

#### MTG Cost Modification Rules
**Question explored:** How do cost increases interact with X spells, alternate costs, reductions?
**Sources consulted:** Scryfall rulings, MTG Wiki, community Q&A

**Findings:**
- Total cost = (base OR alternate) + increases - reductions
- Increases stack additively
- Apply to flashback and other alternate costs
- X is chosen first, then modifications apply
- Defense Grid: 'controller's turn' = caster's turn

### Dead-End Paths

#### Grant-based static effects
**Why explored:** Existing grant system seemed like natural extension
**Investigation:** Analyzed lifecycle for 5 card types, found bidirectional hooks needed
**Why abandoned:** More complex than query for every case, requires non-existent ETB/LTB system

### Open Concerns Raised
- 'What would grants look like for Exploration/Shroud?' → Analyzed in detail; clean for player-level but complex for permanent-targeting
- 'Where are all the rule check points?' → Mapped 4 cast-flow points for cost mods, 6 future check points for other types
close_reason: All 13 success criteria met. Static abilities engine + 3 cards (Sphere of Resistance, Defense Grid, Chill). 2335 tests, 6585 assertions. Commits: 405e87d, a3a81ef, 1f04336.

title: Refactor opponent turn: model as real turn with active-player switching
description:
## Problem

The opponent's turn is currently a hack: a bare `opponent-draw` call wedged into the player's `::start-turn` event handler. This already caused bugs (double draw, infinite loop) and won't scale to the bot system which needs the opponent to take real turns with phases, priority, and decision points.

## Current State

- `opponent-draw` is called manually before `start-turn` in the `::start-turn` event handler
- `:game/active-player` never changes — always points to player-1
- Turn counter only tracks player turns (opponent turns are invisible)
- No phase progression for opponent (no untap, upkeep, draw step, main, etc.)
- History entry for opponent draw is manually constructed in the event handler

## Target State

- Turns alternate between player and opponent via `:game/active-player`
- Opponent gets a real (abbreviated) turn: at minimum draw step, expandable to full phases later
- Turn counter reflects both players' turns (or separate per-player turn tracking)
- `start-turn` works identically for both players — no special-casing
- Bot protocol hooks into opponent turn phases (`:on-turn-start`, `:on-main-phase`, etc.)
- The `opponent-draw` function and its manual orchestration in `::start-turn` are eliminated

## Architecture Already in Place

- Opponent is a real player entity (`:player/id :opponent`) with full schema
- `:game/active-player` exists as `:db.type/ref` in schema (just never switches)
- All effects accept `player-id` — draw, lose-life, deal-damage work for either player
- `get-opponent-id` query exists
- `IBot` protocol designed with turn triggers (`:on-turn-start`, `:on-priority`, etc.)

## Key Design Decisions Needed

1. **Turn numbering**: Increment for both players (MTG-correct) or track per-player?
2. **Goldfish turn phases**: Draw-only? Or untap+draw minimum?
3. **Player's end step → opponent turn → player turn**: How does the UI signal turn transitions?
4. **Bot integration point**: Does the bot get called during opponent's turn phases, or does it subscribe to events?

## Scope

- Refactor `start-turn` / `::start-turn` to alternate `:game/active-player`
- Add opponent turn flow (at minimum: draw step via existing machinery)
- Remove `opponent-draw` hack from `::start-turn` event handler
- Update history to naturally record opponent turn actions
- Update phase-bar UI to reflect whose turn it is
- Prerequisite for bot system (Phase 5 in roadmap)

## What This Unlocks

- Bot system can hook into opponent turn phases
- Burn bot can bolt during its main phase
- Control bot can hold up mana during opponent's turn
- Discard bot can cast Duress during its main phase
- Clean foundation for any future opponent interaction
notes: Detached from architecture epic fizzle-gz0f. This is standalone work tracked as an independent feature. See doc/arch/architecture-overview.md for context and ADR-004 for rationale.
close_reason: Absorbed into fizzle-ip7s (Bot Abstraction + Goldfish Bot epic). Opponent turn refactor is task 1 of the bot epic.

title: Add two-screen routing (setup placeholder vs game)
design:
## Goal
Implement screen routing so the app can switch between a setup screen (placeholder for Epic 2) and the game screen, using a re-frame :active-screen key in app-db.

## Effort Estimate
2-3 hours

## Context
Epic fizzle-8305 requires two-screen routing. Currently core.cljs renders the game screen unconditionally with the new CSS Grid layout. Epic 2 (fizzle-m2sg) will fill in the setup screen content, but the routing shell must exist now. The game currently initializes via ::events/init-game dispatched in core.cljs init function.

## Implementation

### 1. Add :active-screen to app-db initialization
In events/game.cljs, update the ::init-game event handler to include :active-screen :game in the initial db map.

### 2. Create re-frame subscription
In subs/game.cljs, add:
\`\`\`clojure
(rf/reg-sub ::active-screen
  (fn [db] (:active-screen db)))
\`\`\`

### 3. Create re-frame event
In events/game.cljs, add:
\`\`\`clojure
(rf/reg-event-db ::set-active-screen
  (fn [db [_ screen]] (assoc db :active-screen screen)))
\`\`\`

### 4. Update core.cljs app function
Subscribe to ::active-screen. Conditionally render:
- :game → current game-grid layout with all zones
- :setup → placeholder div with centered "Setup Screen (Coming Soon)" text

Extract the game grid into a defn- game-screen component. The app function becomes a router:
\`\`\`clojure
(defn app []
  (let [screen @(rf/subscribe [::subs/active-screen])]
    [:div {:class "min-h-screen bg-surface font-mono text-text"}
     ;; Header with nav
     [:div {:class "flex items-center gap-4 p-3 border-b border-border"}
      [:h1 {:class "text-text"} "Fizzle"]
      [:button {:class (nav-btn-class (= screen :setup))
                :on-click #(rf/dispatch [::events/set-active-screen :setup])}
       "Setup"]
      [:button {:class (nav-btn-class (= screen :game))
                :on-click #(rf/dispatch [::events/set-active-screen :game])}
       "Game"]]
     ;; Screen content
     (case screen
       :setup [setup-screen]
       :game [game-screen])]))
\`\`\`

### 5. Add nav button style helper
\`\`\`clojure
(defn- nav-btn-class [active?]
  (str "px-3 py-1 text-sm rounded cursor-pointer "
       (if active?
         "bg-accent text-surface font-bold"
         "bg-surface-raised text-text-muted")))
\`\`\`

### 6. Add setup screen placeholder
\`\`\`clojure
(defn- setup-screen []
  [:div {:class "flex items-center justify-center h-[80vh] text-text-muted text-lg"}
   "Setup Screen (Coming Soon)"])
\`\`\`

### 7. Update game-screen to be the extracted grid
Move the current game-grid div into a defn- game-screen component. The grid classes stay on this component.

### 8. Add ::subs/active-screen require to core.cljs
Ensure core.cljs has the subs namespace aliased to subscribe.

### 9. Run make validate

## Success Criteria
- [ ] :active-screen key exists in app-db after init, defaults to :game
- [ ] Subscription ::active-screen returns current screen value
- [ ] Event ::set-active-screen switches between :setup and :game
- [ ] Game screen renders the full CSS Grid game UI
- [ ] Setup screen renders placeholder text
- [ ] Nav buttons visible in header, active button highlighted
- [ ] Clicking Setup/Game buttons switches screens
- [ ] make validate passes (0 failures, 0 errors, 0 lint warnings)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO URL-based routing (overkill for two screens, re-frame db is sufficient)
- ❌ NO lazy loading of game screen (game initializes on app start regardless)
- ❌ NO inline :style maps

## Key Considerations

**Game State on Screen Switch**
Game keeps running when switching to setup screen. No teardown/recreation. This is intentional — player can switch back and forth without losing state.

**Init Event Compatibility**
The init-game event must still work. Adding :active-screen to the db map should not break any existing event handlers that destructure or merge db state.

**Subscription Namespace**
core.cljs already requires subs.game — verify the alias name and use it for the new subscription.
close_reason: Implemented two-screen routing with :active-screen in app-db, subscription, event, nav buttons, setup placeholder, and game-screen extraction. All 1000 tests pass, make validate clean.

title: Feature: History entries should identify principal (human vs bot)
description: History make-entry has no principal field, making it impossible to tell who acted in the history log
design:
## Goal
History entries should record and display who caused the action (human or bot). Currently, make-entry has no principal field, making bot turns hard to follow in the history log.

## Root Cause
history/core.cljs make-entry (lines 12-17) only captures: snapshot, event-type, description, turn. No principal field.

The view (history.cljs:78) displays :entry/description with no indication of actor.

## Effort Estimate
3-4 hours

## Success Criteria
- [ ] make-entry has 5-arity version accepting principal (player-id keyword like :player-1 or :player-2)
- [ ] 4-arity make-entry still works (backward compat, defaults principal to nil)
- [ ] History interceptor passes active-player-id as principal when creating entries
- [ ] yield-loop passes active-player-id as principal when creating entries
- [ ] start-turn manual entries pass active-player-id as principal
- [ ] History view shows visual indicator for bot actions (e.g., subtle "Bot" prefix or different text color)
- [ ] Human actions show no prefix (clean display)
- [ ] Entries with nil principal display normally (backward compat)
- [ ] 4+ tests: entry-has-principal-field, bot-entry-principal-is-bot-player, human-entry-principal-is-human-player, nil-principal-displays-normally
- [ ] make validate clean

## Implementation Checklist

### Step 1: Extend make-entry
- [ ] In history/core.cljs, add multi-arity make-entry:
  ```clojure
  (defn make-entry
    ([game-db event-type description turn]
     (make-entry game-db event-type description turn nil))
    ([game-db event-type description turn principal]
     {:entry/snapshot game-db
      :entry/event-type event-type
      :entry/description description
      :entry/turn turn
      :entry/principal principal}))
  ```

### Step 2: Store is-bot flag alongside principal
- [ ] Add :entry/is-bot? boolean to entry map (avoids expensive Datascript query during render):
  ```clojure
  (defn make-entry
    ([game-db event-type description turn]
     (make-entry game-db event-type description turn nil false))
    ([game-db event-type description turn principal is-bot?]
     {:entry/snapshot game-db
      :entry/event-type event-type
      :entry/description description
      :entry/turn turn
      :entry/principal principal
      :entry/is-bot? (boolean is-bot?)}))
  ```

### Step 3: Update history interceptor
- [ ] In interceptor.cljs :after, determine principal and is-bot? from game-db-after:
  ```clojure
  (let [active-pid (try (queries/get-active-player-id snapshot-db) (catch :default _ nil))
        is-bot? (when active-pid
                  (boolean (bot/get-bot-archetype snapshot-db active-pid)))
        entry (history/make-entry snapshot-db event-id description turn active-pid is-bot?)]
    ...)
  ```
- [ ] Import fizzle.bots.protocol if not already imported (may already be present via game-loop)

### Step 4: Update yield-loop
- [ ] In game.cljs yield-loop, pass principal when creating entries:
  ```clojure
  (let [active-pid (queries/get-active-player-id post-db)
        is-bot? (boolean (bot/get-bot-archetype post-db active-pid))
        entry (history/make-entry post-db ::yield desc turn active-pid is-bot?)]
    ...)
  ```

### Step 5: Update start-turn manual entries
- [ ] In game.cljs start-turn function (search for make-entry calls in start-turn):
  - Opponent draw entry: principal = opponent player-id, is-bot? = true (opponent is bot)
  - Turn start entry: principal = new active player id, is-bot? = based on new active player

### Step 6: Update history view
- [ ] In views/history.cljs action-log, use :entry/is-bot? to display indicator:
  ```clojure
  [:div {:class (str "..." (when (:entry/is-bot? entry) " text-text-muted italic"))}
   (when (:entry/is-bot? entry)
     [:span {:class "text-[10px] text-text-dim mr-1"} "Bot"])
   (:entry/description entry)]
  ```
- [ ] Human entries: no prefix, standard styling
- [ ] Bot entries: "Bot" prefix in smaller dimmed text + italic description

## Key Considerations

**Performance**: Storing :entry/is-bot? boolean avoids Datascript queries during render. Each entry already stores a full game-db snapshot; one boolean is negligible overhead.

**Backward compatibility**: 4-arity make-entry defaults to nil/false. Existing tests and code paths work unchanged.

**make-entry callers (exhaustive list)**:
1. history/interceptor.cljs :after — main path (Step 3)
2. game.cljs yield-loop — yield-all/F6 path (Step 4)
3. game.cljs start-turn — manual entries for opponent draw and turn start (Step 5)
4. Tests — existing tests use 4-arity, keep working (backward compat)

## Edge Cases (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)

**start-turn manual entries**:
- game.cljs start-turn creates entries for opponent draw and turn start. These must also pass principal. Without this, the first entries on each turn would lack principal while all other entries have it — inconsistent.

**init-game entry**:
- The "Game started" entry from the interceptor. Active player at init is human. principal = :player-1. is-bot? = false. Displays normally.

**nil principal on old entries**:
- If user loads a saved tactic with old entries (no :entry/principal), is-bot? is nil/falsy -> no prefix. Clean degradation.

## Anti-patterns
- Do NOT store player name strings in the entry (store player-id keyword, derive display)
- Do NOT modify history stepping/forking logic (principal is display-only metadata)
- Do NOT query get-bot-archetype from entry snapshot during render (use stored :entry/is-bot?)
- Do NOT break 4-arity make-entry callers (multi-arity for backward compat)
close_reason: Closed

title: Deploy to GitHub Pages with automated CI
description: Fizzle has no hosted version — only runs locally. Set up automated deployment to GitHub Pages so pushing to main publishes a live site at username.github.io/fizzle. Three changes: fix asset paths in index.html, add make release target, create GitHub Actions deploy workflow.
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- Push to main triggers automated build + deploy to GitHub Pages
- Site is accessible at username.github.io/fizzle (project site)
- Release build produces working static assets (JS + CSS + HTML)
- Both development (shadow-cljs watch) and production (release) paths work with same index.html

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] make release produces deployable artifacts in resources/public/
- [ ] GitHub Actions workflow builds and deploys on push to main
- [ ] Site loads and functions correctly at github.io URL
- [ ] Local dev (make dev) still works after path changes
- [ ] All existing tests passing
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO separate index.html for dev vs production (maintenance burden: one file must work for both)
- ❌ NO gh-pages branch approach (outdated: use modern actions/deploy-pages artifact pattern)
- ❌ NO hardcoded username in workflow or paths (portability: repo should work for any fork)
- ❌ NO skipping CSS build step (correctness: Tailwind must be compiled for production)
- ❌ NO test/lint in deploy workflow yet (scope: CI validation is separate concern, add later)

## Approach
Three targeted changes to enable automated deployment:

1. Fix absolute asset paths in index.html to relative paths (css/app.css, js/main.js) so the same file works for both local dev server and GitHub Pages project site subdirectory.

2. Add a make release target that chains clean -> build-css -> shadow-cljs release app into one command.

3. Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml with two-job pattern: build (setup-java + setup-node + npm ci + make release + upload-pages-artifact) and deploy (deploy-pages).

## Architecture
Files changed:
- resources/public/index.html — relative paths
- Makefile — add release target
- .github/workflows/deploy.yml — new file

Build pipeline: npm ci -> make release (clean + postcss + shadow-cljs release) -> upload resources/public/ -> deploy-pages

CI requirements: Java (shadow-cljs JVM) + Node.js (npm packages)

## Design Rationale
### Problem
Fizzle has no hosted version. Users can only run it locally. Need a live URL for sharing and feedback.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- resources/public/index.html:6,10 — absolute paths /css/app.css and /js/main.js break on project sites
- shadow-cljs.edn:17-18 — output-dir resources/public/js, asset-path /js (irrelevant for single-module release)
- Makefile:37-39 — build-css target exists, no release target
- No .github/workflows/ directory exists

**External:**
- GitHub Pages modern approach uses actions/deploy-pages@v4 + upload-pages-artifact@v3
- shadow-cljs requires Java + Node in CI (JVM-based despite npm launcher)
- setup-node@v4 has built-in npm caching via cache: npm
- Two-job pattern (build + deploy) is the recommended GitHub Pages workflow structure

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. GitHub Pages + GitHub Actions ✓
**What it is:** Single workflow file deploys resources/public/ on push to main using the modern Pages artifact pattern.

**Chosen because:** Free, integrated with existing GitHub setup, user already has GH Pages experience. Pure static SPA needs nothing more.

#### 2. Netlify ❌
**What it is:** Third-party hosting with GitHub integration, PR previews, custom domains.

**Why we looked at this:** Better SPA support, PR deploy previews.

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Overkill for pure static SPA. Adds another service to manage. No features needed that GH Pages doesn't provide.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Need PR deploy previews or backend functions.

#### 3. Cloudflare Pages ❌
**What it is:** Edge-deployed static hosting with GitHub integration.

**Why we looked at this:** Global CDN, generous free tier.

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Same as Netlify — unnecessary complexity for this use case.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Performance at global scale becomes a concern.

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Automated deploy on push to main
- Relative asset paths for project site compatibility
- make release target for local + CI builds

**Out of scope (deferred):**
- CI testing/validation (separate workflow, add after deploy works)
- Custom domain (do after site is live and verified)
- SPA routing / 404.html (Fizzle is single-page, no URL routes)
- Cache busting / asset hashing (shadow-cljs release output is already content-hashed in advanced mode)

### Open Questions
- None — design is straightforward

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Goal: demo or tool? | Both — start as demo, grow | Ship fast, iterate |
| Backend needed? | Pure static SPA, no backend | Simplest hosting possible |
| Domain? | GitHub Pages first, domain later | Project site subdirectory |
| Automated deploys? | Yes, GitHub Actions | Need workflow file |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### Asset Path Strategy
**Question explored:** How to handle paths for project site subdirectory?
**Sources:** GitHub Pages docs, shadow-cljs asset-path behavior
**Findings:** Relative paths (no leading /) work for both dev server root and project site subdirectory. shadow-cljs asset-path irrelevant for single-module release build.
**Conclusion:** Change /js/main.js to js/main.js, /css/app.css to css/app.css

#### CI Environment
**Question explored:** What does shadow-cljs need in CI?
**Sources:** shadow-cljs docs, GitHub Actions best practices
**Findings:** shadow-cljs requires JVM despite npm package being a launcher. Need setup-java + setup-node.
**Conclusion:** Use temurin Java + Node 20 with npm caching
close_reason: Complete — site deployed to GitHub Pages. Commits: f5bc6c6 (asset paths), 44d06a9 (deploy workflow), e6ac6ea (fix Datascript/Closure :simple optimizations). All tests pass.

title: Fizzle Architecture
description: Volatility-based decomposition of the Fizzle MTG practice tool. 6 components organized around independent change axes, with pure engine boundary as the key architectural invariant.
design:
## System Purpose
Fizzle is a ClojureScript MTG combo deck practice tool with fork/replay. The dominant change force is card pool expansion (EDN data), supported by occasional new effect types and interaction patterns. All other subsystems are converging toward stability.

## Lowy Layer Map
- Boundary: Presentation
- Manager: Game Orchestration, Practice Platform
- Engine: Interpretation Core
- Resource Accessor: Card Pool, Data Foundation

## Global Constraints
- Engine layer (Interpretation Core) must remain pure functions with zero re-frame dependencies
- No upward dependencies: Engine cannot call Manager, Resource Accessor cannot contain business logic
- Data-driven extension model: new cards/bots/tactics are EDN data interpreted by their respective engines
- Bot and tactic data live with their engines (Practice Platform), not with Card Pool
- Future axis noted: if data loading moves external, a Data Gateway component at Resource Accessor layer would sit beneath Card Pool and Practice Platform
close_reason: Architecture decomposition complete. All work consolidated into doc/arch/architecture-overview.md. ADRs preserved in doc/arch/adr-*.md. Data flow trace in DATA_FLOW_TRACE.md. Opponent turn refactor (fizzle-gvs7) continues as independent issue.

title: Card Pool
description: Pure EDN card definitions and deck data. Highest volatility component — new cards added regularly.
design:
## Volatility Axis
Card content expansion — new card definitions are added most frequently. Each card is a pure EDN data map defining effects, costs, types, and metadata.

## Layer: Resource Accessor
Provides card data to the Interpretation Core. Contains no business logic — cards are data, not code. The engine interprets them.

## Interface Contract
- IN: None (data source, not called by others)
- OUT: Card entity maps consumed by Interpretation Core via multimethods and db queries

## Responsibility
Defines the card pool as pure EDN data. Does NOT interpret effects, execute game logic, or contain card-specific code paths. If you are writing a case statement on a card name, you have gone wrong.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/cards/*.cljs (9 files, ~58 card definitions)

## Notes
- If data loading moves to an external source, a Data Gateway component would sit beneath this component at the Resource Accessor layer
close_reason: Architecture consolidated into doc/arch/architecture-overview.md. Component definitions, ADRs, data flows, and constraints preserved.

Reason: initial decomposition, not yet audited

title: Interpretation Core
description: Pure-function game rules engine. Interprets card effects, manages game state transitions, handles selection mechanism. Zero re-frame dependencies.
design:
## Volatility Axis
Game interpretation mechanics — changes when new effect types, selection patterns, or rules mechanics are needed. Medium-rare volatility: new effect handlers are just defmethods (variability), but new interaction patterns (iterative selection, opponent choice) require structural changes (true volatility).

## Layer: Engine
Pure business logic. Computes game state transitions, interprets effect data, manages player interaction mechanism. Does NOT coordinate game flow, access external resources, or depend on re-frame.

## Interface Contract
- IN: Pure function calls from Game Orchestration (cast-spell, resolve-stack-item, execute-effect, build-selection, etc.)
- OUT: Returns transformed db state, tagged values ({:db :needs-selection}), or selection builders. Calls into Card Pool (multimethod dispatch on card data) and Data Foundation (Datascript queries).

## Responsibility
Interprets card EDN into game state changes. Manages stack resolution, mana system, zone transitions, targeting, combat, effect execution, and selection mechanism. Does NOT coordinate game lifecycle, dispatch re-frame events, or manage UI state.

## Key Architectural Invariant
This component MUST remain pure functions with zero re-frame dependencies. The purity of engine/ is an explicit architectural boundary, not an implementation detail.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/engine/*.cljs (20 files: rules, resolution, effects, stack, mana, zones, targeting, grants, triggers, trigger-dispatch, trigger-db, combat, costs, abilities, bots)
- src/fizzle/events/selection/*.cljs (selection sub-modules that are pure logic)
close_reason: Architecture consolidated into doc/arch/architecture-overview.md. Component definitions, ADRs, data flows, and constraints preserved.

title: Game Orchestration
description: Re-frame event coordination layer. Manages game lifecycle, dispatches to Interpretation Core, handles side effects.
design:
## Volatility Axis
Game flow coordination — changes when game lifecycle sequencing, event handling, or side-effect management needs to evolve. Medium volatility during active development, stabilizing.

## Layer: Manager
Coordinates workflow between components. Contains NO business logic — delegates all game rule computation to Interpretation Core. Manages re-frame event dispatch, effect handling (fx), and game state lifecycle.

## Interface Contract
- IN: Re-frame event dispatches from UI (Presentation) and Practice Platform
- OUT: Calls Interpretation Core pure functions, writes to Data Foundation (Datascript db via re-frame db effect)

## Responsibility
Coordinates game flow: event handling, dispatching to engine functions, managing re-frame interceptors, handling side effects. Does NOT compute game rules, interpret card effects, or manage selection logic. Thin orchestration layer.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/events/game.cljs (~670 lines, main dispatcher hub)
- src/fizzle/events/abilities.cljs (~273 lines)
- src/fizzle/events/selection.cljs (~1437 lines, re-frame event handlers that wrap selection mechanism)
- src/fizzle/core.cljs (app lifecycle)

title: Practice Platform
description: Fork/replay, tactics, bots, history. Each subsystem owns its data (EDN) and interpretation engine. Stabilizing volatility.
design:
## Volatility Axis
Practice mode evolution — changes when new practice features, bot behaviors, or tactic scenarios are added. Data-driven extension model: new bots/tactics are EDN data interpreted by their respective engines within this component. Stabilizing after initial build.

## Layer: Manager
Orchestrates practice sessions by wrapping Game Orchestration with fork/replay, bot AI, and tactic constraints. Contains bot and tactic data alongside their interpretation logic (domain-split: data lives with its interpreter).

## Interface Contract
- IN: User actions from Presentation (start practice, fork, replay, load tactic)
- OUT: Dispatches to Game Orchestration (re-frame events), reads from Data Foundation (game state queries)

## Responsibility
Manages practice tool features: fork/replay system, history tracking, tactic/puzzle save/load, bot AI opponents, hand sculpting. Each subsystem owns its own data definitions AND interpretation logic. Does NOT compute game rules or interpret card effects.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/history/*.cljs (4 files: interceptor, descriptions, core, fork)
- src/fizzle/tactics/*.cljs (puzzle system)
- src/fizzle/bots/*.cljs (bot AI: goldfish, burn, control, discard)
- src/fizzle/storage/*.cljs (localStorage wrapper)

title: Presentation
description: Reagent views, re-frame subscriptions, Tailwind styling. Stabilizing volatility.
design:
## Volatility Axis
UX/UI changes — visual layout, interaction patterns, card rendering, styling. Stabilizing after core UX is established.

## Layer: Boundary
User-facing boundary where the system meets the outside world. Subscribes to derived state, renders UI, dispatches events. No game logic, no direct state mutation.

## Interface Contract
- IN: Re-frame subscriptions from Data Foundation (stored state) and Interpretation Core (derived/computed state via ADR-003)
- OUT: Re-frame event dispatches to Game Orchestration and Practice Platform (user actions)

## Responsibility
Renders the game UI: battlefield, hand, stack, mana pool, graveyard, phase bar. Collects player input via selection UI. Does NOT compute game state, interpret cards, or manage game lifecycle.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/views/*.cljs (17 files: battlefield, hand, stack, card components, styling)
- src/fizzle/subs/*.cljs (4 files: game, setup, card, selection subscriptions)
close_reason: Architecture consolidated into doc/arch/architecture-overview.md. Component definitions, ADRs, data flows, and constraints preserved.

title: Data Foundation
description: Datascript schema, queries, initialization. Most stable component — the shared data contract.
design:
## Volatility Axis
Data model changes — changes when new entity types or attributes are needed. Most stable component in the system. Changes here ripple everywhere, so stability is critical.

## Layer: Resource Accessor
Defines the shared data contract (Datascript schema) and provides query functions. All game state flows through this component. Contains no business logic — pure data definition and access.

## Interface Contract
- IN: Schema definitions consumed by all components; query functions called by Interpretation Core, Game Orchestration, Presentation
- OUT: Datascript db state, query results

## Responsibility
Defines the 13+ entity types (card, object, player, stack-item, trigger, grant, etc.) that all components share. Provides query functions for accessing game state. Does NOT interpret game rules, coordinate flow, or render UI.

## Current Modules
- src/fizzle/db/schema.cljs (entity type definitions, ~100 lines)
- src/fizzle/db/queries.cljs (Datascript query functions)
- src/fizzle/db/init.cljs (database initialization)
close_reason: Architecture consolidated into doc/arch/architecture-overview.md. Component definitions, ADRs, data flows, and constraints preserved.

title: Move pure query functions from engine/ to db/queries
description:
stack/get-all-stack-items and grants/get-grants are pure Datascript queries that currently live in engine modules. Per ADR-003, Data Foundation should be the single interface for raw data access. Move these functions to db/queries and update all callers.

Functions to move:
- engine/stack.cljs: get-all-stack-items
- engine/grants.cljs: get-grants

Callers to update:
- subs/game.cljs (imports both)
- Any engine/ modules that call these internally (update to use db/queries)
- Any events/ modules that call these

Acceptance criteria:
- Functions relocated to db/queries
- All callers updated
- No new circular dependencies introduced
- make validate passes
design:
## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Add get-all-stack-items to db/queries.cljs

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/db/queries.cljs`

Add after existing stack-related query stack-empty? (around line 110):
```clojure
(defn get-all-stack-items
  "Get all stack-items in LIFO order (highest position first).
   Returns vector, or empty vector if stack is empty."
  [db]
  (->> (d/q '[:find [(pull ?e [*]) ...]
              :where [?e :stack-item/position _]]
            db)
       (sort-by :stack-item/position >)
       (vec)))
```

### Step 2: Add get-grants to db/queries.cljs

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/db/queries.cljs`

Add near other object-related queries:
```clojure
(defn get-grants
  "Get all grants on an object.
   Returns empty vector if object has no grants."
  [db object-id]
  (let [obj (get-object db object-id)]
    (or (:object/grants obj) [])))
```

Note: get-object is already in db/queries.cljs — no new imports needed.

### Step 3: Update production callers of get-all-stack-items

- `src/main/fizzle/subs/game.cljs` — change stack/get-all-stack-items to q/get-all-stack-items (already imports queries as q)
- `src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs` — change stack/get-all-stack-items to q/get-all-stack-items. Add [fizzle.db.queries :as q] to requires if not present.

### Step 4: Update production callers of get-grants

- `src/main/fizzle/engine/rules.cljs` — change grants/get-grants to q/get-grants
- `src/main/fizzle/subs/game.cljs` — change grants/get-grants to q/get-grants
- `src/main/fizzle/engine/effects.cljs` — change grants/get-grants to q/get-grants if called directly
- `src/main/fizzle/events/selection/zone_ops.cljs` — change grants/get-grants to q/get-grants if called directly

### Step 5: Update test callers

Mechanically update all test files (~24 files) that import engine.stack for get-all-stack-items or engine.grants for get-grants:
- Change requires: add [fizzle.db.queries :as q]
- Change stack/get-all-stack-items -> q/get-all-stack-items
- Change grants/get-grants -> q/get-grants
- Keep existing engine requires if other functions from those namespaces are still used

### Step 6: Remove original functions from engine modules

- `src/main/fizzle/engine/stack.cljs:61-69` — delete get-all-stack-items
- `src/main/fizzle/engine/grants.cljs:23-28` — delete get-grants

### Step 7: Resolve stack-empty? duplication

Both engine/stack.cljs and db/queries.cljs have stack-empty?. Verify they are functionally identical. If so, remove the engine version and update callers to use q/stack-empty?. If not identical, document the difference.

### Step 8: Verify

```bash
make validate
```

Expected: All tests pass, no lint errors, no circular dependencies.

title: Refactor resolve-all as recursive dispatch
description:
Replace the internal loop in resolve-all-handler (events/game.cljs:471-515) with a recursive reg-event-fx that resolves one stack item per dispatch, then re-dispatches itself.

## Current State
resolve-all-handler loops through stack items internally, manually creating history entries via history/core and applying them via apply-history-entries. This bypasses the global history interceptor and duplicates its fork/append logic.

## Target State
- ::resolve-all is a reg-event-fx that calls resolve-one-item for the top stack item
- If no pending-selection and stack has more initial items, re-dispatches [::resolve-all initial-ids]
- Each dispatch goes through the history interceptor, getting its own entry automatically
- initial-ids parameter preserves current behavior: stop when newly-created item appears on top

## Code to Remove
- make-resolve-entry helper (lines 443-454)
- apply-history-entries helper (lines 457-468)  
- resolve-all-handler function (lines 471-515)
- history/core and history/descriptions imports from game.cljs (after opponent turns also done)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Resolve All button produces identical game state as before
- Each resolved item gets its own history entry (via interceptor, not manual)
- Resolution stops at pending-selection, new stack items, or empty stack
- Step-back after resolve-all steps through individual resolutions
- make validate passes

## Depends On
Nothing — can be done independently of opponent turns refactor
design:
## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Add ::resolve-all to priority events in history interceptor

**File:** src/main/fizzle/history/interceptor.cljs

Add :fizzle.events.game/resolve-all to the priority-events set. This ensures the interceptor creates history entries when each ::resolve-all dispatch changes :game/db.

### Step 2: Convert ::resolve-all from reg-event-db to reg-event-fx

**File:** src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

Replace current registration (lines 530-533) with recursive version:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx
  ::resolve-all
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ initial-ids]]
    (let [game-db (:game/db db)
          initial-ids (or initial-ids
                         (set (map :db/id (stack/get-all-stack-items game-db))))]
      (if (empty? initial-ids)
        {:db db}
        (let [top (stack/get-top-stack-item game-db)]
          (if (and top (contains? initial-ids (:db/id top)))
            (let [result (resolve-one-item game-db :player-1)]
              (if (:pending-selection result)
                {:db (-> db
                         (assoc :game/db (:db result))
                         (assoc :game/pending-selection (:pending-selection result)))}
                {:db (assoc db :game/db (:db result))
                 :fx [[:dispatch [::resolve-all initial-ids]]]}))
            {:db (-> db
                     (assoc :game/db game-db)
                     (maybe-continue-cleanup))}))))))
```

Key design:
- First dispatch: initial-ids is nil, snapshots current stack IDs
- Subsequent dispatches: initial-ids passed as parameter
- Each dispatch resolves ONE item, goes through history interceptor
- Re-dispatches via :fx for next item
- Stops at: pending-selection, new item on top, empty stack

### Step 3: Handle maybe-continue-cleanup

Verify maybe-continue-cleanup works at app-db level in the new structure. Currently called on app-db in resolve-all-handler. Ensure it returns modified app-db for use in :db effect.

### Step 4: Delete helper functions

**File:** src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

Delete:
- make-resolve-entry (lines 443-454)
- apply-history-entries (lines 457-468)
- resolve-all-handler (lines 471-515)

Note: history/core and history/descriptions imports stay — used by start-turn and other code.

### Step 5: Update tests

**File:** src/test/fizzle/events/resolve_all_test.cljs

Tests currently call game/resolve-all-handler directly. Rewrite to dispatch ::resolve-all through re-frame.

Key test scenarios to preserve:
- Resolves all items on stack
- Stops at pending-selection
- Stops when new item appears on top
- Creates individual history entries per resolution
- Step-back after resolve-all steps through individual resolutions
- Fork creation when resolving from rewound position

### Step 6: Verify history behavior

After implementation, verify:
- Each resolved item creates own history entry (via interceptor, not manual)
- Step-back (undo) after Yield All steps through individual resolutions
- Fork creation works from rewound position

### Step 7: Verify

```bash
make validate
```

Expected: All tests pass. Yield All produces identical game state. History entries appear individually.
close_reason: Recursive resolve-all implemented and passing all tests. History interceptor registration added to test file to verify per-item history entries.

title: Move UI collapse atoms into re-frame app-db
description:
Three global Reagent atoms in core.cljs (stack-collapsed?, gy-collapsed?, history-collapsed?) are mutable state outside the re-frame single-atom model. Move them into app-db under :ui/ keys for consistency.

## Current State

- Add reg-event-db ::toggle-stack-collapsed, ::toggle-gy-collapsed, ::toggle-history-collapsed

design:
## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Add :ui/ keys to app-db initialization

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/events/setup.cljs:39-53`

Add 3 keys to `init-setup-handler` return map:
```clojure
{:setup/selected-deck :iggy-pop
 ;; ... existing keys ...
 :ui/stack-collapsed false
 :ui/gy-collapsed false
 :ui/history-collapsed false}
```

Also add to `init-game-state` in `src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs:137-174` — merge into the return map:
```clojure
:ui/stack-collapsed false
:ui/gy-collapsed false
:ui/history-collapsed false
```

### Step 2: Register 3 subscriptions and 3 toggle events

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/subs/game.cljs` — add near existing UI subs (line 16 area):
```clojure
(rf/reg-sub ::stack-collapsed (fn [db _] (:ui/stack-collapsed db)))
(rf/reg-sub ::gy-collapsed (fn [db _] (:ui/gy-collapsed db)))
(rf/reg-sub ::history-collapsed (fn [db _] (:ui/history-collapsed db)))
```

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs` — add near existing UI events (line 199 area):
```clojure
(rf/reg-event-db ::toggle-stack-collapsed
  (fn [db _] (update db :ui/stack-collapsed not)))

(rf/reg-event-db ::toggle-gy-collapsed
  (fn [db _] (update db :ui/gy-collapsed not)))

(rf/reg-event-db ::toggle-history-collapsed
  (fn [db _] (update db :ui/history-collapsed not)))
```

### Step 3: Convert collapsible-right-column to use subscriptions

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/core.cljs:35-45`

Replace atom-based helper with subscription-based version:
```clojure
(defn- collapsible-right-column
  [title sub-key toggle-event & children]
  (let [collapsed? @(rf/subscribe [sub-key])]
    [:div {:class (str "border-l border-border bg-surface-raised overflow-y-auto "
                       (if collapsed? "right-col-collapsed" "right-col-expanded"))}
     [:div {:class "flex items-center cursor-pointer select-none gap-2 p-2 border-b border-border"
            :on-click #(rf/dispatch [toggle-event])}
      [:span {:class "text-text-label text-xs font-bold uppercase tracking-wider"} title]
      [:span {:class "text-text-dim text-xs"}
       (if collapsed? "\u25B6" "\u25BC")]]
     (when-not collapsed?
       (into [:div {:class "p-2"}] children))]))
```

### Step 4: Update game-screen callers

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/core.cljs:69-71`

Change from atoms to subscription/event keywords:
```clojure
[collapsible-right-column "Stack" ::subs/stack-collapsed ::events/toggle-stack-collapsed [stack/stack-view]]
[collapsible-right-column "Graveyard" ::subs/gy-collapsed ::events/toggle-gy-collapsed [graveyard/graveyard-view]]
[collapsible-right-column "History" ::subs/history-collapsed ::events/toggle-history-collapsed [history/history-sidebar]]
```

Update requires in core.cljs ns form to include the sub/event namespaces if not already present.

### Step 5: Remove the 3 defonce atoms

**File:** `src/main/fizzle/core.cljs:30-32`

Delete:
```clojure
(defonce stack-collapsed? (r/atom false))
(defonce gy-collapsed? (r/atom false))
(defonce history-collapsed? (r/atom false))
```

Remove reagent.core require if no longer needed.

### Step 6: Verify

```bash
make validate
```

Expected: All tests pass, no lint errors. Panel collapse behavior unchanged.

title: Task 3: Write Testing Strategy Documentation
design:
## Goal
Document the card testing strategy at two levels: concise summary in CLAUDE.md and full guide in docs/testing-strategy.md. Establishes the standard for all future card tests.

## Epic Context
Epic: fizzle-8tl0 — Test Infrastructure & Card Testing Strategy
Linear plan step 3 of 6. Builds on completed tasks 1 (shared helpers) and 2 (anti-pattern fixes).

## Implementation

### 1. Study exemplar tests to extract patterns
Read these files:
- src/test/fizzle/engine/effects_test.cljs — engine gold standard
- src/test/fizzle/cards/brain_freeze_test.cljs — complex integration
- src/test/fizzle/cards/recoup_test.cljs — thorough boundary testing
- src/test/fizzle/cards/gemstone_mine_test.cljs — newly created exemplar
- src/test/fizzle/cards/lotus_petal_test.cljs — doseq parameterization example

### 2. Write docs/testing-strategy.md (full guide)
Structure:
- Overview and philosophy (test through real event dispatch)
- Mandatory Test Categories A-D (required for EVERY card):
  A. Card definition — verify ALL fields (id, name, cmc, cost, types, colors, effects with all keys)
  B. Cast-resolve happy path — full cycle through cast/resolve
  C. Cannot-cast guards — insufficient mana, wrong phase, no targets
  D. Storm count — casting increments storm
- Conditional Categories E-I:
  E. Selection/modal tests (player choice cards)
  F. Targeting tests (targeted spells)
  G. Edge cases (2+ per card: empty zones, partial resources)
  H. Flashback tests
  I. Trigger/ability tests
- Minimum test counts per card type with rationale
- Exemplar references with commentary
- Anti-pattern examples (before/after from task 2)
- Test helper usage (fizzle.test-helpers namespace)
- Parameterization patterns (when doseq vs separate tests)

IMPORTANT: Category A must NOT be tautological. Include good vs bad examples:
- BAD: (is (some? (:card/id card)))
- GOOD: (is (= :dark-ritual (:card/id card)))

### 3. Add Card Testing Requirements section to CLAUDE.md
Append to Conventions section (DO NOT modify existing content). Keep concise (15-25 lines):
- Mandatory categories A-D (one line each)
- Conditional categories E-I (trigger + one line)
- Compact minimum test count table
- Link to docs/testing-strategy.md
- Top 3 anti-patterns

### 4. Verify
- make validate passes
- All file references in docs point to real files

## Success Criteria
- [ ] docs/testing-strategy.md exists with all sections above
- [ ] CLAUDE.md has Card Testing Requirements appended to Conventions
- [ ] Strategy references actual exemplar files
- [ ] Anti-pattern examples reference actual code
- [ ] Minimum test counts per card type with rationale
- [ ] Category A warns against tautological tests with examples
- [ ] No modifications to any test files or production code
- [ ] make validate passes
close_reason: Closed

title: Migrate City of Brass trigger to event system and remove dead code
design:
## Goal

Migrate City of Brass :becomes-tapped trigger from scanning-based system to event-based system, then delete the now-dead scanning code.

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours

## Implementation

### 1. Study Existing Patterns

**Event system (use this):**
- trigger_registry.cljs:12-23 - register-trigger! pattern
- trigger_registry.cljs:39-54 - unregister-by-source! on zone leave
- trigger_dispatch.cljs:34-57 - dispatch-event flow
- events.cljs:25-37 - permanent-tapped-event exists

**Old system (delete this):**
- triggers.cljs:220-253 - fire-matching-triggers
- triggers.cljs:274-310 - fire-and-resolve-triggers
- triggers.cljs:74-83 - check-trigger :becomes-tapped
- triggers.cljs:106-117 - resolve-trigger :becomes-tapped
- game.cljs:529 - call to fire-matching-triggers

### 2. Register City of Brass Trigger on ETB

In game.cljs play-land or a new registration helper:

\`\`\`clojure
;; When City of Brass enters battlefield, register its trigger
(defn register-card-triggers!
  [object-id controller-id card]
  (doseq [trigger (:card/triggers card)]
    (registry/register-trigger!
      {:trigger/id (random-uuid)
       :trigger/event-type :permanent-tapped
       :trigger/source object-id
       :trigger/controller controller-id
       :trigger/filter {:event/object-id :self}  ; only when THIS permanent taps
       :trigger/uses-stack? true
       :trigger/effects (:trigger/effects trigger)
       :trigger/description (:trigger/description trigger)})))
\`\`\`

### 3. Dispatch Event on Tap

In activate-mana-ability (game.cljs), after tapping:

\`\`\`clojure
;; Replace fire-matching-triggers with:
(dispatch/dispatch-event db (game-events/permanent-tapped-event object-id player-id))
\`\`\`

### 4. Add resolve-trigger :permanent-tapped

In triggers.cljs:

\`\`\`clojure
(defmethod resolve-trigger :permanent-tapped
  [db trigger]
  ;; Same logic as old :becomes-tapped resolver
  (let [controller (:trigger/controller trigger)
        effects-list (:trigger/effects trigger)]
    (reduce (fn [db' effect]
              (let [resolved-effect (if (= :controller (:effect/target effect))
                                      (assoc effect :effect/target controller)
                                      effect)]
                (effects/execute-effect db' controller resolved-effect)))
            db
            effects-list)))
\`\`\`

### 5. Unregister Triggers on Zone Leave

Ensure triggers are unregistered when permanent leaves battlefield:

\`\`\`clojure
;; In zone change handling (or sacrifice/destroy):
(registry/unregister-by-source! object-id)
\`\`\`

### 6. Delete Dead Code

**triggers.cljs - Remove:**
- Lines 58-71: check-trigger multimethod and :default
- Lines 74-83: check-trigger :becomes-tapped
- Lines 106-117: resolve-trigger :becomes-tapped
- Lines 212-253: fire-matching-triggers and get-battlefield-objects helper
- Lines 274-310: fire-and-resolve-triggers

**game.cljs - Remove:**
- Line 529: call to triggers/fire-matching-triggers
- Remove fire-matching-triggers from require if no other usages

### 7. Update Tests

**Delete tests for removed functions:**
- triggers_test.cljs: test-fire-matching-triggers-* (lines 238-340)

**Add tests for new event-based flow (in triggers_test.cljs or new file):**
- test-cob-trigger-registered-on-etb: Verify registry/get-all-triggers includes CoB trigger after ETB
- test-cob-trigger-unregistered-on-leave: Verify registry empty after sacrifice/destroy
- test-cob-tap-dispatches-event: Verify dispatch-event called with :permanent-tapped event type
- test-cob-trigger-deals-damage: Tap CoB, resolve trigger, assert player life reduced by 1
- test-cob-multiple-taps: Tap CoB twice, verify two triggers fire, life reduced by 2

## Success Criteria

- [ ] City of Brass trigger works via dispatch-event (verify: tap CoB, life decreases by 1)
- [ ] fire-matching-triggers function deleted: \`rg "fire-matching-triggers" src/\` returns 0 results
- [ ] fire-and-resolve-triggers function deleted: \`rg "fire-and-resolve-triggers" src/\` returns 0 results
- [ ] check-trigger multimethod deleted: \`rg "defmulti check-trigger" src/\` returns 0 results
- [ ] No references to deleted functions: \`rg "(fire-matching-triggers|fire-and-resolve-triggers|check-trigger)" src/\` returns 0 results
- [ ] 5+ new tests for event-based trigger flow pass
- [ ] Existing City of Brass tests still pass (if any)
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Trigger Source Leaves Battlefield**
- If City of Brass is sacrificed/destroyed before trigger resolves, trigger should still resolve
- Trigger has controller-id, can still deal damage even if source gone
- Verify: sacrifice CoB with trigger on stack, damage still dealt

**Edge Case: Multiple Permanents with Same Trigger**
- Two City of Brass on battlefield, tap one
- Only the tapped one's trigger should fire (filter by :event/object-id :self)
- Verify: tap one CoB, only 1 damage dealt

**Edge Case: Rapid Multiple Taps**
- Tap same CoB multiple times (if untapped between)
- Each tap should dispatch event and add trigger
- Verify: untap, tap, untap, tap = 2 triggers = 2 damage

**Unregistration on Zone Leave**
- CRITICAL: Must unregister triggers when permanent leaves battlefield
- If not unregistered, ghost triggers remain in registry
- Verify: sacrifice CoB, registry/get-all-triggers no longer contains its trigger

**Reference Implementation**
- Study how turn-based triggers use dispatch-event in turn_based.cljs
- Follow same pattern for card triggers

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)

- ❌ NO leaving check-trigger or fire-matching-triggers code (even commented out)
- ❌ NO registering triggers without corresponding unregister on zone leave
- ❌ NO hardcoded object-ids in tests (use dynamic test fixture IDs)
- ❌ NO skipping unregister verification test (ghost triggers cause bugs)
- ❌ NO modifying City of Brass card definition (card data stays same, only engine changes)

title: Show top stack item inline near Yield button to reduce visual distance
description: Currently the stack is in a collapsible right column, far from the control buttons. When deciding whether to yield (resolve), the player needs to look across the screen to see what's on top of the stack. This breaks gameplay flow. Add an inline preview of the top stack item near the Yield button (or in the controls area) so the player can see what they're resolving without scanning to the right panel. The full stack panel remains for viewing the complete stack. Consider: compact inline format (e.g. 'Resolve: Dark Ritual'), visual connection between preview and Yield button, updating dynamically as stack changes.
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- Yield button label shows top stack item card name: 'Yield: {card-name}'
- Yield All button label shows stack item count: 'Yield All ({count})'
- When stack is empty, buttons show plain 'Yield' and 'Yield All' (both disabled)
- Card name extraction follows same priority logic as stack-item-view: card-name > description > type fallback
- Button width grows naturally to fit card name (no truncation)

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] Yield button shows 'Yield: Dark Ritual' when Dark Ritual is top of stack
- [ ] Yield button shows 'Yield' when stack is empty
- [ ] Yield All button shows 'Yield All (5)' when 5 items on stack
- [ ] Yield All button shows 'Yield All' when 1 or fewer items (disabled)
- [ ] Fallback labels work for stack items without card names (triggers, abilities)
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO new subscriptions (reuse existing ::stack subscription already in controls view)
- ❌ NO new UI elements/components (modify button labels only, not layout)
- ❌ NO card name truncation or ellipsis (let button grow naturally)
- ❌ NO changes to stack-item-view or stack panel (this feature is controls-only)

## Approach
Modify views/controls.cljs to extract the card name from (first stack) and interpolate it into the Yield button label. Add (count stack) to Yield All label. Use same label priority logic as stack-item-view in views/stack.cljs (card-name > description > type fallback). Single-file change plus tests.

## Architecture
- views/controls.cljs — Modify Yield and Yield All button labels
- No new files, subscriptions, or components

## Parallelism Map

### Independent Work Streams
1. **Yield button label** (views/controls.cljs)
   - Extract top item name, update button text
   - Estimated complexity: low

### Parallelism Assessment
- Independent streams: 1
- Recommendation: solo execution
- Rationale: Single file change, no parallelism opportunity

## Design Rationale
### Problem
Players must scan from the control buttons to the stack panel to see what resolves next. During fast storm reps this breaks flow.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- views/controls.cljs:40-47 — Yield/Yield All buttons already subscribe to ::stack
- views/stack.cljs:7-19 — stack-item-view has label priority logic (card-name > description > type)
- subs/game.cljs:42-66 — ::stack subscription enriches items with card names

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Inline in button label ✓
**What:** Button text becomes 'Yield: {name}' dynamically
**Chosen because:** Zero new UI, leverages existing subscription, minimal code change

#### 2. Adjacent text element ❌
**Why explored:** Standard pattern for showing context near buttons
**REJECTED BECAUSE:** Adds layout complexity for no benefit. Button label IS the preview.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Button label approach causes layout issues

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:** Yield and Yield All button label text changes
**Out of scope:** Stack panel changes, new subscriptions, card type icons
