title: Phase 4: Migrate Deep Analysis to cast-time targeting
description:
## Goal
Migrate Deep Analysis card to use the new cast-time targeting system as a stepping stone before implementing Recoup.

## Context
Phases 1-3 complete:
- Grant system implemented (grants.cljs)
- Cleanup system implemented (expire-grants trigger)
- Targeting foundation implemented (targeting.cljs with 5 core functions)

Deep Analysis currently uses :effect/target :any-player in its effects. Need to migrate to :card/targeting for cast-time target declaration.

## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Add :card/targeting to Deep Analysis
Update deep_analysis.cljs to add targeting requirement:
```clojure
:card/targeting [{:target/id :player
                  :target/type :player
                  :target/options [:self :opponent :any-player]
                  :target/required true}]
```

### Step 2: Modify casting flow for target declaration
In events/game.cljs (cast-spell event):
- After mode selection, check if card has targeting
- If so, prompt for target selection BEFORE moving to stack
- Store chosen targets on object via :object/targets

### Step 3: Update resolution to use stored targets
Modify spell resolution to:
- Read targets from :object/targets map
- Pass target to effect execution
- Remove hardcoded target selection at resolution time

### Step 4: Add fizzle logic
Add to spell resolution:
- Call all-targets-legal? before executing effects
- If false, fizzle: move card per cast mode but skip effects
- Log fizzle event for debugging

### Step 5: Update Deep Analysis tests
- Update existing tests to work with new flow
- Add test for target selection at cast time
- Add test for fizzle when player target becomes invalid (edge case)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Deep Analysis has :card/targeting attribute
- [ ] Casting Deep Analysis prompts for player target before stack
- [ ] Target stored on object, used at resolution
- [ ] Existing Deep Analysis tests pass
- [ ] New test confirms cast-time target selection
- [ ] make test passes with no failures

## Anti-Patterns
- Do NOT break existing Deep Analysis tests (migration, not rewrite)
- Do NOT modify targeting.cljs (foundation already complete)
- Do NOT implement Recoup-specific logic yet
design:
## Goal
Migrate Deep Analysis card to use the new cast-time targeting system as a stepping stone before implementing Recoup.

## Effort Estimate
8-10 hours

## Context
Phases 1-3 complete:
- Grant system implemented (grants.cljs)
- Cleanup system implemented (expire-grants trigger)
- Targeting foundation implemented (targeting.cljs with 5 core functions)

Deep Analysis currently uses :effect/target :any-player in its effects (line 19 of deep_analysis.cljs). Need to migrate to :card/targeting for cast-time target declaration.

## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Add :card/targeting to Deep Analysis (TDD)
**File:** src/main/fizzle/cards/deep_analysis.cljs

Add targeting requirement to card definition:
\`\`\`clojure
:card/targeting [{:target/id :player
                  :target/type :player
                  :target/options [:self :opponent :any-player]
                  :target/required true}]
\`\`\`

Keep :effect/target :any-player temporarily for backwards compatibility during migration.

**Test:** Write test that verifies targeting/get-targeting-requirements returns the player target.

### Step 2: Modify casting flow for target declaration
**File:** src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

**Location:** cast-spell-with-mode-on-stack function (around line 800)

After mode selection, BEFORE moving spell to stack:
1. Check if card has :card/targeting via targeting/get-targeting-requirements
2. If targeting exists AND requires selection (not pre-chosen):
   - Call targeting/has-valid-targets? to verify targets exist
   - Build player-target selection state (similar to build-player-target-selection)
   - Return {:db db :pending-selection targeting-selection}
3. When target selection confirmed:
   - Store targets on object via :object/targets map
   - Continue with stack placement

**Key difference from current flow:**
- Currently: Target selected at RESOLUTION time (line 883)
- New: Target selected at CAST time, stored on object, used at resolution

### Step 3: Update resolution to use stored targets
**File:** src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

**Location:** resolve-spell-effects function and execute-effects-with-selection function

Modify to:
1. Read targets from :object/targets map on the spell object
2. Pass stored target to effect execution instead of prompting
3. For :effect/target :any-player effects, look up :player target from stored targets
4. If no stored target (legacy path), fall back to current selection behavior

### Step 4: Add fizzle logic for invalid targets
**File:** src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

**Location:** resolve-stack-top function (around line 750)

Add before executing effects:
\`\`\`clojure
(if (and (seq (targeting/get-targeting-requirements card))
         (not (targeting/all-targets-legal? db object-id)))
  ;; Fizzle: move card to destination without executing effects
  (let [destination (or (get-in obj [:object/cast-mode :mode/on-resolve]) :graveyard)]
    (zones/move-to-zone db object-id destination))
  ;; Normal resolution
  (resolve-spell-effects db player-id object-id))
\`\`\`

### Step 5: Update Deep Analysis tests
**File:** src/test/fizzle/cards/deep_analysis_test.cljs

Update existing tests:
- Tests that cast Deep Analysis need to also provide target selection
- Helper function to simulate target selection at cast time

New tests to add:
1. test_deep_analysis_has_targeting_requirement - verify :card/targeting present
2. test_deep_analysis_target_selection_at_cast_time - verify prompt before stack
3. test_deep_analysis_stored_target_used_at_resolution - verify stored target used
4. test_deep_analysis_player_target_always_legal - player targets don't fizzle (players can't move zones)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Deep Analysis card has :card/targeting attribute with player target
- [ ] targeting/get-targeting-requirements returns 1 requirement for Deep Analysis
- [ ] Casting Deep Analysis prompts for player target BEFORE spell goes on stack
- [ ] :object/targets stores {:player :player-1} (or :player-2) on the spell
- [ ] Resolution reads target from :object/targets, not prompting again
- [ ] all-targets-legal? returns true for player targets (always legal)
- [ ] All 20+ existing Deep Analysis tests pass without modification
- [ ] 4 new tests pass confirming cast-time targeting
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + fmt + tests)

## Key Considerations

**Edge Case: Storm Copies**
- Storm creates copies of spells on the stack
- Copies should inherit targets from original spell
- Verify existing storm copy logic copies :object/targets

**Edge Case: Flashback Deep Analysis**
- Flashback casts from graveyard, not hand
- Targeting should work identically
- Test flashback with both :self and :opponent targets

**Edge Case: Cancelled Target Selection**
- If player dismisses target selection UI
- Spell should NOT go on stack (similar to cancelled mode selection)
- Current UI pattern: return to previous state

**Backwards Compatibility**
- Keep :effect/target :any-player during migration
- Effects module may still check this for legacy behavior
- Remove after Cephalid Coliseum also migrated (Phase 5)

**Reference Implementation**
- Study existing player-target selection: build-player-target-selection (line 883)
- Study mode selection flow: cast-spell-with-mode-on-stack (line 800)
- Study stored target reading: patterns don't exist yet, creating new pattern

## Anti-Patterns
- ❌ Do NOT break existing Deep Analysis tests (migration preserves behavior)
- ❌ Do NOT modify targeting.cljs (foundation already complete in Phase 3)
- ❌ Do NOT implement Recoup-specific logic yet
- ❌ Do NOT remove :effect/target :any-player yet (needed for backwards compat)
- ❌ Do NOT hardcode player IDs - use stored target from :object/targets
- ❌ Do NOT duplicate target selection logic - reuse existing build-player-target-selection pattern

## Specific Test Cases (must implement)
1. test_deep_analysis_has_targeting_requirement
   - Catches: Missing :card/targeting attribute on card definition
   - Verifies: targeting/get-targeting-requirements returns vector with 1 player target

2. test_deep_analysis_target_selection_at_cast_time
   - Catches: Target prompt happening at wrong time (resolution vs cast)
   - Verifies: pending-selection returned after mode selection, before stack

3. test_deep_analysis_stored_target_used_at_resolution
   - Catches: Resolution still prompting instead of using stored target
   - Verifies: :object/targets has {:player target-id}, effect uses it

4. test_deep_analysis_flashback_targeting
   - Catches: Flashback path skipping targeting
   - Verifies: Flashback cast also prompts for target, stores it
close_reason: Implemented cast-time targeting for Deep Analysis: added :card/targeting attribute, cast-spell-with-targeting function, confirm-cast-time-target function, and updated resolve-spell-with-selection to use stored targets with fizzle check. All tests passing (520 tests).

title: Card tests bypass production casting flow (systemic anti-pattern)
description:
## Problem

~18 of ~28 castable card test files bypass the production casting flow by manually constructing selection maps and calling confirm-cast-time-target directly instead of going through cast-spell-with-targeting. This means the cast-time targeting validation path (can-cast? → has-valid-targets? → find-valid-targets) is essentially untested across the entire card pool.

This directly caused both Portent and Stifle shipping with casting bugs that passed all tests (fizzle-d4w2.6, fizzle-d4w2.7).

## Anti-patterns found

1. **Manual selection map construction** (most common): Tests build `{:selection/type :cast-time-targeting ...}` instead of going through `sel-targeting/cast-spell-with-targeting`
2. **Direct selection handler calls**: Tests call `sel-core/execute-confirmed-selection`, `library/execute-peek-selection` directly
3. **Direct effect execution**: Tests call `effects/execute-effect` directly instead of full cast-resolve pipeline
4. **Manual mode assignment**: Tests assign `:object/chosen-mode` via `d/db-with` instead of modal selection system
5. **Custom resolution helpers**: e.g. brain_freeze_test creates custom resolution that bypasses `game/resolve-one-item`

## Affected files

**HIGH (manual selection construction — targeting validation completely bypassed):**
- brain_freeze_test.cljs
- blue_elemental_blast_test.cljs
- hydroblast_test.cljs
- counterspell_test.cljs
- daze_test.cljs
- foil_test.cljs
- chain_of_vapor_test.cljs
- annul_test.cljs
- duress_test.cljs
- mana_leak_test.cljs
- portent_test.cljs
- stifle_test.cljs

**MEDIUM (direct selection/effect execution):**
- careful_study_test.cljs
- flash_of_insight_test.cljs
- impulse_test.cljs
- sleight_of_hand_test.cljs
- opt_test.cljs
- intuition_test.cljs
- merchant_scroll_test.cljs

**LOWER (ability activation bypass):**
- tormods_crypt_test.cljs
- deep_analysis_test.cljs

## Fix

Each affected card's cast-resolve happy path test should go through the actual production flow: cast-spell-with-targeting → selection → confirm, verifying can-cast? returns true as a precondition. Custom helpers that construct selections manually should be replaced or supplemented with production-path tests.

## CLAUDE.md reference

Anti-pattern #1: Reimplementing production handlers in tests (test through rules/cast-spell, not manual copies)
close_reason: All 9 subtasks complete: 5 composable test helpers created, 21 test files fixed across 5 code tasks, testing-strategy.md updated, CLAUDE.md updated, implement-card skill updated, lint enforcement added. 2075 tests, 5922 assertions, all passing.

title: Scaffold card directory structure and registry with sample migration
description: Set up the new cards/ subdirectory structure, create registry.cljs, and migrate a representative sample of cards (one from each subdirectory type) to prove the pattern works end-to-end.
design:
## Goal
Establish the new card directory structure and registry pattern by migrating a representative sample of cards. Prove the full data flow works: card file → registry → engine/cards.cljs → consumers. Coexist with remaining iggy_pop.cljs cards during migration.

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours

## Implementation

### 1. Create subdirectory structure
Create empty directories:
- src/main/fizzle/cards/black/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/blue/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/white/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/red/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/green/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/multicolor/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/lands/
- src/main/fizzle/cards/artifacts/
Mirror in test path:
- src/test/fizzle/cards/black/
- src/test/fizzle/cards/blue/
- etc.

### 2. Establish export convention
Each card file exports a single def named `card` (or `cards` as a vector for cycles).

Example — src/main/fizzle/cards/black/dark_ritual.cljs:
```clojure
(ns fizzle.cards.black.dark-ritual)

(def card
  {:card/id :dark-ritual
   :card/name "Dark Ritual"
   ...})
```

This is a CHANGE from current standalone convention (lightning-bolt/lightning-bolt). The uniform `card` name enables registry to use a consistent pattern: `ns/card` for every card.

For cycles — src/main/fizzle/cards/lands/basic_lands.cljs:
```clojure
(ns fizzle.cards.lands.basic-lands)

(def cards
  [{:card/id :plains ...}
   {:card/id :mountain ...}
   {:card/id :forest ...}
   {:card/id :island ...}
   {:card/id :swamp ...}])
```

### 3. Migrate representative cards (one per occupied category)
Extract from iggy_pop.cljs → individual files:
- black/dark_ritual.cljs (iggy_pop.cljs line 21-30)
- blue/merchant_scroll.cljs (iggy_pop.cljs line 219-234)
- lands/city_of_brass.cljs (iggy_pop.cljs line 71-91)
- artifacts/lotus_petal.cljs (iggy_pop.cljs line 135-146)

Move existing standalone files into subdirectories:
- orims_chant.cljs → white/orims_chant.cljs (update ns, rename var to `card`)
- basic_lands.cljs → lands/basic_lands.cljs (update ns, rename to `cards` vector)
- lightning_bolt.cljs → red/lightning_bolt.cljs (update ns, rename var to `card`)

After extracting cards from iggy_pop.cljs, REMOVE those defs from iggy_pop.cljs. Update iggy_pop.cljs all-cards vector to exclude migrated cards. Update iggy-pop-decklist to reference card IDs only (no var references needed — decklist uses :card/id keywords).

### 4. Create cards/registry.cljs
```clojure
(ns fizzle.cards.registry
  (:require [fizzle.cards.black.dark-ritual :as dark-ritual]
            [fizzle.cards.blue.merchant-scroll :as merchant-scroll]
            [fizzle.cards.white.orims-chant :as orims-chant]
            [fizzle.cards.red.lightning-bolt :as lightning-bolt]
            [fizzle.cards.lands.basic-lands :as basic-lands]
            [fizzle.cards.lands.city-of-brass :as city-of-brass]
            [fizzle.cards.artifacts.lotus-petal :as lotus-petal]
            ;; Remaining cards still in iggy_pop during migration
            [fizzle.cards.iggy-pop :as iggy-pop]))

(def all-cards
  (into (vec (concat [dark-ritual/card
                       merchant-scroll/card
                       orims-chant/card
                       lightning-bolt/card
                       city-of-brass/card
                       lotus-petal/card]
                      basic-lands/cards))
        iggy-pop/all-cards))
```

### 5. Update engine/cards.cljs
Change import from fizzle.cards.iggy-pop to fizzle.cards.registry:
```clojure
(ns fizzle.engine.cards
  (:require [fizzle.cards.registry :as registry]))

(def all-cards registry/all-cards)
(def card-by-id (into {} (map (juxt :card/id identity) all-cards)))
```

### 6. Move and update test files
For each migrated card, move test file to mirrored path and update:
- Namespace declaration (e.g., fizzle.cards.dark-ritual-test → fizzle.cards.black.dark-ritual-test)
- Card import: change `[fizzle.cards.iggy-pop :as cards]` to `[fizzle.cards.black.dark-ritual :as dark-ritual]`
- Card var references: change `cards/dark-ritual` to `dark-ritual/card`

Example — dark_ritual_test.cljs:
Before: `(let [card cards/dark-ritual] ...)`
After: `(let [card dark-ritual/card] ...)`

For standalone cards that already had test files (lightning_bolt_test, orims_chant_test, basic_lands_test):
- Move to mirrored subdirectory
- Update ns declaration
- Update var references from `lightning-bolt/lightning-bolt` to `lightning-bolt/card`

### 7. Update shadow-cljs test runner config
Check shadow-cljs.edn — if test runner config references specific test namespaces, update to include new paths. If it scans test/ directory recursively (likely), no change needed.

### 8. Verify
```bash
make validate  # lint + format + tests
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] 8 subdirectories exist in src/main/fizzle/cards/ (black, blue, white, red, green, multicolor, lands, artifacts)
- [ ] 8 mirrored subdirectories exist in src/test/fizzle/cards/
- [ ] At least 7 cards migrated to individual files (dark-ritual, merchant-scroll, orims-chant, lightning-bolt, basic-lands cycle, city-of-brass, lotus-petal)
- [ ] cards/registry.cljs exports all-cards combining individual files + remaining iggy_pop cards
- [ ] engine/cards.cljs imports from fizzle.cards.registry only (not fizzle.cards.iggy-pop)
- [ ] Migrated card defs removed from iggy_pop.cljs (no duplicate definitions)
- [ ] All card exports use uniform `card` (or `cards` for cycles) var name
- [ ] All 1779+ existing tests pass: `make validate` exits 0
- [ ] card-by-id lookup returns correct card for all 32 cards (verified by existing tests)
- [ ] No direct fizzle.cards.iggy-pop imports in engine/cards.cljs

## Anti-Patterns (task-specific)
- ❌ NO duplicate card definitions (card must be removed from iggy_pop.cljs when migrated — verify all-cards count stays at 32)
- ❌ NO namespace/path mismatches (ns declaration must match file path exactly)
- ❌ NO leaving stale test imports (every cards/iggy-pop reference in migrated tests must be updated)
- ❌ NO updating decklist references (iggy-pop-decklist uses :card/id keywords, not var references — leave it alone)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Duplicate cards in all-cards**
Risk: If a card is extracted to its own file but NOT removed from iggy_pop.cljs all-cards, the registry will contain duplicates. Engine card-by-id map would only keep last occurrence but duplicate processing in card_spec validation could cause issues.
Mitigation: After migration, verify `(count all-cards)` is still exactly 32. Add assertion in registry.cljs if practical.

**Edge Case: Test files with cross-card imports**
Some test files import BOTH iggy-pop (for setup cards like dark-ritual for mana) AND standalone card namespaces. When migrating dark-ritual out of iggy-pop, any test file that uses `cards/dark-ritual` for test setup (not just the dark-ritual test itself) will break.
Mitigation: Grep for `cards/dark-ritual` across ALL test files, not just dark_ritual_test.cljs. Update references in ALL files that use migrated cards.

**Edge Case: shadow-cljs module caching**
After moving files, shadow-cljs may cache stale module paths.
Mitigation: Run `make clean` before `make validate` to clear .shadow-cljs/ cache.

**Edge Case: iggy-pop-decklist var references**
The iggy-pop-decklist uses :card/id keywords (e.g., {:card/id :dark-ritual :count 4}), NOT var references. Verify this before migrating — no decklist changes needed.

**No shared helpers in iggy_pop.cljs**
Confirmed by codebase investigation: iggy_pop.cljs contains only card defs + all-cards vector + decklist. No helper functions shared between cards. Migration is purely extract-and-move.

title: History Interceptor Update (Step Group 8)
description: Update priority-events set and selection-triggers-entry in history/interceptor.cljs for new yield events.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add :becomes-tapped trigger type to engine/triggers.cljs
design:
## Goal
Implement the :becomes-tapped trigger type so that cards like City of Brass can trigger abilities when they become tapped.

## Context
Completed fizzle-31d (costs.cljs) and fizzle-4zd (abilities.cljs). Now need trigger infrastructure so City of Brass can deal damage whenever it becomes tapped.

**Key Insight:** The existing trigger system has resolve-trigger multimethod but NO mechanism to detect trigger conditions. We need:
1. A way to check if a trigger condition is met (check-trigger multimethod)
2. A way to fire triggers based on game state changes

## Effort Estimate
3-4 hours

## Implementation Steps

### 1. RED: Write tests for check-trigger and :becomes-tapped
File: src/test/fizzle/engine/triggers_test.cljs

Add 8 tests:

```clojure
;; === check-trigger tests ===

(deftest test-check-trigger-becomes-tapped-matches
  (testing "check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns true when object transitions untapped->tapped"
    ;; Setup: old-db with untapped permanent, new-db with same permanent tapped
    ;; Trigger: {:trigger/type :becomes-tapped :trigger/source object-id}
    ;; Assert: (triggers/check-trigger old-db new-db trigger) => true
    ;; Catches: Not detecting state transition
    ))

(deftest test-check-trigger-becomes-tapped-no-change
  (testing "check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns false when object was already tapped"
    ;; Setup: old-db with tapped permanent, new-db with same permanent still tapped
    ;; Assert: (triggers/check-trigger old-db new-db trigger) => false
    ;; Catches: Firing on every tapped state, not just transition
    ))

(deftest test-check-trigger-becomes-tapped-untapped-still
  (testing "check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns false when object remains untapped"
    ;; Setup: old-db with untapped permanent, new-db with same permanent still untapped
    ;; Assert: (triggers/check-trigger old-db new-db trigger) => false
    ;; Catches: Firing when no tap occurred
    ))

(deftest test-check-trigger-becomes-tapped-missing-object
  (testing "check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns false when object doesn't exist"
    ;; Setup: old-db with no object, new-db with no object
    ;; Assert: (triggers/check-trigger old-db new-db trigger) => false (not crash)
    ;; Catches: NPE on missing object
    ))

;; === fire-matching-triggers tests ===

(deftest test-fire-matching-triggers-creates-trigger-on-stack
  (testing "fire-matching-triggers adds trigger to stack when condition matches"
    ;; Setup: permanent with :card/triggers [{:trigger/type :becomes-tapped ...}]
    ;; old-db: untapped, new-db: tapped
    ;; Action: (triggers/fire-matching-triggers old-db new-db)
    ;; Assert: trigger entity exists on stack in returned db
    ;; Catches: Not creating trigger objects
    ))

(deftest test-fire-matching-triggers-no-match-unchanged
  (testing "fire-matching-triggers returns db unchanged when no triggers match"
    ;; Setup: permanent with :card/triggers, but condition not met
    ;; Assert: db unchanged, no new triggers on stack
    ;; Catches: Creating spurious triggers
    ))

(deftest test-fire-matching-triggers-multiple-triggers
  (testing "fire-matching-triggers handles multiple triggers on same permanent"
    ;; Setup: permanent with two :becomes-tapped triggers
    ;; old-db: untapped, new-db: tapped
    ;; Assert: both triggers added to stack
    ;; Catches: Only firing first trigger
    ))

(deftest test-fire-matching-triggers-multiple-permanents
  (testing "fire-matching-triggers checks all permanents with triggers"
    ;; Setup: two permanents each with :becomes-tapped trigger
    ;; old-db: both untapped, new-db: first tapped, second still untapped
    ;; Assert: only first permanent's trigger fires
    ;; Catches: Checking all triggers regardless of condition
    ))
```

### 2. GREEN: Implement check-trigger multimethod

File: src/main/fizzle/engine/triggers.cljs

```clojure
(defmulti check-trigger
  "Check if a trigger condition is met.
   
   Arguments:
     old-db - Database state before action
     new-db - Database state after action  
     trigger - Trigger definition from card data
   
   Returns:
     Boolean - true if trigger should fire."
  (fn [_old-db _new-db trigger] (:trigger/type trigger)))

(defmethod check-trigger :default [_old-db _new-db _trigger] false)

(defmethod check-trigger :becomes-tapped
  [old-db new-db trigger]
  (let [source-id (:trigger/source trigger)]
    (if-let [new-obj (q/get-object new-db source-id)]
      (let [old-obj (q/get-object old-db source-id)
            was-tapped (get old-obj :object/tapped false)
            now-tapped (get new-obj :object/tapped false)]
        ;; Trigger fires only on transition: untapped -> tapped
        (and (not was-tapped) now-tapped))
      false)))
```

### 3. GREEN: Implement fire-matching-triggers

```clojure
(defn fire-matching-triggers
  "Check all triggers on battlefield and fire those whose conditions are met.
   
   Arguments:
     old-db - Database state before action
     new-db - Database state after action
   
   Returns:
     New db with matching triggers added to stack."
  [old-db new-db]
  (let [;; Get all permanents on battlefield
        battlefield (q/get-battlefield new-db)
        ;; Collect matching triggers
        matching-triggers
        (for [obj battlefield
              :let [card (q/get-card-data new-db (:object/card obj))
                    triggers (:card/triggers card)]
              trigger triggers
              :let [trigger-with-source (assoc trigger :trigger/source (:object/id obj))]
              :when (check-trigger old-db new-db trigger-with-source)]
          {:trigger trigger-with-source
           :controller (:object/controller obj)})]
    ;; Add all matching triggers to stack
    (reduce (fn [db {:keys [trigger controller]}]
              (let [full-trigger (create-trigger 
                                   (:trigger/type trigger)
                                   (:trigger/source trigger)
                                   controller
                                   {:effects (:trigger/effects trigger)})]
                (add-trigger-to-stack db full-trigger)))
            new-db
            matching-triggers)))
```

### 4. GREEN: Add resolve-trigger for :becomes-tapped

```clojure
(defmethod resolve-trigger :becomes-tapped
  [db trigger]
  (let [controller (:trigger/controller trigger)
        effects (get-in trigger [:trigger/data :effects] [])]
    (reduce (fn [db' effect]
              (effects/execute-effect db' controller effect))
            db
            effects)))
```

### 5. VERIFY
- Run: make validate
- All 8 new tests pass
- All existing 189 tests still pass

## Success Criteria
- [ ] check-trigger multimethod exists with :becomes-tapped implementation
- [ ] check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns true ONLY on untapped->tapped transition
- [ ] check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns false for already-tapped or still-untapped
- [ ] check-trigger :becomes-tapped returns false (not crash) for missing object
- [ ] fire-matching-triggers scans battlefield and fires matching triggers
- [ ] fire-matching-triggers handles multiple triggers per permanent
- [ ] fire-matching-triggers handles multiple permanents
- [ ] resolve-trigger :becomes-tapped executes trigger effects
- [ ] 8 new tests pass in triggers_test.cljs
- [ ] All 189+ existing tests still pass
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**State Transition Detection:**
- Must compare old-db to new-db, not just check current state
- :becomes-tapped fires on transition (untapped->tapped), not on being tapped
- Permanents entering battlefield tapped do NOT trigger :becomes-tapped

**Missing Object Handling:**
- If trigger source no longer exists, check-trigger returns false (not crash)
- Defensive coding: use get with default for :object/tapped

**Controller vs Owner:**
- Triggered abilities are controlled by permanent's controller (may differ from owner)
- Use :object/controller when creating trigger on stack

**Effect Execution:**
- Trigger effects use existing effects/execute-effect multimethod
- Unknown effect types are no-op (graceful degradation)
- Empty effects list is valid (trigger fires but does nothing)

**Integration Point:**
- fire-matching-triggers must be called AFTER any game action that might tap permanents
- Caller (events/game.cljs) responsible for calling this at appropriate times
- This task does NOT wire up the caller - that's a separate integration task

**Reference Implementation:**
- Study resolve-trigger :storm in triggers.cljs for effect execution pattern
- Study costs_test.cljs for add-permanent test helper

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO card ID checks in trigger code (data over code)
- ❌ NO hardcoded trigger logic (use multimethod dispatch)
- ❌ NO triggering on every tap check (only state transitions)
- ❌ NO crashing on missing objects (return false gracefully)
- ❌ NO ignoring multiple triggers on same permanent
close_reason: Closed

title: Engine Test Quality: SBA parity, trigger coverage, resolve chain, weak assertions
description:
Unified engine test quality epic superseding fizzle-2192 and fizzle-jdc7. Consolidates 4 P2 testing tasks (fizzle-6p9p merged with fizzle-rcaw) plus P3 weak assertions (fizzle-3345).

## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- All 6 SBA types (life-zero, empty-library, lethal-damage, zero-toughness, token-cleanup, state-check-trigger) tested for BOTH players
- Cascading SBA scenarios tested (one SBA creates conditions for another)
- Trigger coverage expanded beyond draw-step/untap-step to include ETB, sacrifice, mana-ability triggers
- Resolve-stack-item → reduce-effects → SBA chain tested end-to-end via dispatch-sync
- Interactive effect pause/resume tested within resolution chain
- All some?/contains? assertions in trigger_db_test and priority_test replaced with exact value checks

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] opponent_sba_test.cljs covers all 6 SBA types for opponent
- [ ] New sba_parity_test.cljs (or expanded file) covers all 6 SBA types for human player
- [ ] At least 2 cascading SBA tests (e.g., lethal damage → creature dies → state-check trigger)
- [ ] triggers_test.cljs expanded with ETB, sacrifice, mana-ability trigger tests
- [ ] New engine integration test file covering resolve→effects→SBA chain via dispatch-sync
- [ ] trigger_db_test.cljs has zero some?/contains? assertions
- [ ] priority_test.cljs has zero some?/contains? assertions
- [ ] All tests passing (make validate green)
- [ ] No production code changes required (test-only epic)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO mutable conn pattern in new tests (use d/db-with, not d/conn-from-db + d/transact! — existing opponent_sba_test.cljs clear-library uses conn, don't copy that pattern)
- ❌ NO reimplementing production handlers in tests (use th/ helpers: cast-and-resolve, cast-with-target, resolve-top, confirm-selection)
- ❌ NO some?/string?/contains? assertions (exact value checks with = always)
- ❌ NO hand-built stack items or selections (use production builders)
- ❌ NO production code changes to make tests pass (if a test needs prod changes, that's a separate bug ticket)
- ❌ NO doseq-based test variants that differ only by parameter (use doseq for parameterized, not copy-paste)

## Approach
4 tasks executed iteratively:
1. SBA parity — expand opponent_sba_test.cljs + add human player SBA tests (all 6 types both players + cascading)
2. Trigger expansion — ETB, sacrifice, mana-ability triggers in triggers_test.cljs
3. Resolve→Effects→SBA chain — end-to-end integration tests via db-effect/register! + dispatch-sync
4. Weak assertion cleanup — trigger_db_test + priority_test exact value assertions

## Architecture
Test files affected:
- src/test/fizzle/engine/opponent_sba_test.cljs (expand)
- src/test/fizzle/engine/state_based_test.cljs (may expand for human player SBAs)
- src/test/fizzle/engine/triggers_test.cljs (expand)
- src/test/fizzle/engine/resolution_integration_test.cljs (new)
- src/test/fizzle/engine/trigger_db_test.cljs (assertion fixes)
- src/test/fizzle/engine/priority_test.cljs (assertion fixes)

Production code under test:
- engine/state_based.cljs — 6 SBA types (check-sba + execute-sba multimethods)
- engine/triggers.cljs — resolve-trigger multimethod (draw-step, untap-step, + card triggers)
- engine/resolution.cljs — resolve-stack-item multimethod (9 types)
- engine/effects.cljs — reduce-effects
- events/resolution.cljs — resolve-one-item (event-level integration)

## Design Rationale
### Problem
Engine has 862 tests but critical integration gaps. SBAs only tested for opponent empty-library (1/6 types). Triggers only test 2 turn-based types. The core resolve→effects→SBA pipeline has no end-to-end tests. Weak assertions mask bugs.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- state_based.cljs has 6 SBA types with check/execute multimethods — only empty-library tested for opponent, none for human player
- triggers.cljs has 3 resolve-trigger defmethods (default, draw-step, untap-step) — only latter 2 tested
- resolution.cljs has 9 resolve-stack-item defmethods — only :spell and :storm-copy tested
- opponent_sba_test.cljs uses mutable conn pattern (clear-library fn) — should not be copied
- db-effect/register! + dispatch-sync pattern established in fizzle-umx2 for event-level integration tests

### Approaches Considered
#### 1. Unified epic with iterative tasks ✓
Merge overlapping tasks, execute in dependency order (SBAs → triggers → chain → cleanup)
Chosen because: tasks share patterns and the SBA work is foundational for chain tests

#### 2. Keep separate epics ❌
Why explored: existing epic structure
Rejected because: significant overlap between fizzle-6p9p and fizzle-rcaw, tasks share test patterns
DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS: tasks prove to be genuinely independent in scope

### Scope Boundaries
In scope: engine-layer test coverage and assertion quality
Out of scope: event-layer integration tests (separate epic fizzle-wnqj), production code changes, combat resolution chain tests (P4 fizzle-u07p)

## Design Discovery
### Key Decisions Made
| Question | Answer | Implication |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| Epic structure | Fresh epic supersedes fizzle-2192 + fizzle-jdc7 | Close old epics as superseded |
| Task overlap | Merge fizzle-6p9p + fizzle-rcaw | Single resolve chain task |
| SBA depth | Full parity (both players) | ~25-30 SBA tests needed |
| Weak assertions | Include fizzle-3345 | 4th task for assertion cleanup |
| Task order | SBAs first | Foundational for chain tests |

title: Fix upward import: move activate-mana-ability to engine layer
description:
ADR-008 declares Opponent AI as pure computation with no re-frame dependencies. Currently bots/actions.cljs imports events/abilities.cljs (manager layer) to access the pure function activate-mana-ability. This creates an upward dependency from engine to manager.

Fix: Extract activate-mana-ability from events/abilities.cljs into an engine module (e.g., engine/abilities.cljs or engine/mana.cljs). The re-frame event handler in events/abilities.cljs wraps the relocated function. bots/actions.cljs then imports from engine instead.

Evidence:
- bots/actions.cljs:14 imports [fizzle.events.abilities :as abilities]
- bots/actions.cljs:54 calls (abilities/activate-mana-ability ...)
- events/abilities.cljs:18 defines the pure function
- events/abilities.cljs:121 wraps it in reg-event-db

Acceptance criteria:
- bots/actions.cljs has zero imports from events/ or subs/ or views/
- activate-mana-ability pure function lives in engine/
- events/abilities.cljs re-frame handler imports and wraps the engine function
- All existing tests pass
- ADR-008 drift resolved
close_reason: Implemented in ADR compliance sweep

title: Effects System Test Quality — missing unit tests + integration gap
description: Audit of effects system (39 effect types, 158 tests). Individual test quality is high (97% GREEN). Two gaps: (1) ~12 effect types have no isolated unit tests in effects_test.cljs (tested only through card tests), risk is medium if card tests change. (2) Same systemic impl-level testing pattern as selection — no tests go through resolve-stack-item → reduce-effects → event chain → SBAs end-to-end. Manual sba/check-and-execute-sbas calls partially mitigate but don't catch wiring bugs.
close_reason: Superseded by fizzle-jayk (unified engine test quality epic)

title: Design battlefield UX: layout, grouping, and visual hierarchy
description: Design the battlefield layout and visual hierarchy. Currently lands and non-lands are split into two rows with an hr divider, but the overall UX needs thought: how permanents are grouped and displayed, visual weight of tapped vs untapped, mana ability buttons, the divider between rows, spacing, and whether additional groupings (e.g. creatures in the future) should be planned for. This is a design task — produce a UX spec before implementation.

title: Feature: Yield All - resolve entire stack in one action
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- Yield All resolves all stack items that were present when the button was clicked
- Resolution stops when a selection is needed (tutor, discard, targeting, scry, peek-and-select)
- Resolution stops when the top stack item was NOT present when Yield All was clicked (e.g., storm-created copies)
- Resolution stops when the stack is empty
- Each individual item still logs to history normally
- Cleanup phase handling (maybe-continue-cleanup) runs once at the end
- Existing Yield (single item) behavior is unchanged

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] Yield All button visible and enabled when stack has 2+ items
- [ ] Yield All resolves all original stack items in LIFO order
- [ ] Yield All stops at selections (pending-selection set correctly)
- [ ] Yield All stops when new items appear on stack (storm copies)
- [ ] Existing ::resolve-top tests pass unchanged after refactor
- [ ] New tests cover: empty stack, single item, multiple items, selection interruption, new-item stop
- [ ] History entries created for each resolved item
- [ ] Cleanup phase handled correctly after Yield All
- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] make validate passing (lint + format + tests)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO recursive re-frame dispatch for looping (flickering: multiple React renders between resolutions)
- ❌ NO modifying existing ::resolve-top behavior (refactor only: extract shared function, keep behavior identical)
- ❌ NO auto-resolving selections with defaults (safety: user must always make explicit choices)
- ❌ NO resolving newly-created stack items (scope: only items present at click time)
- ❌ NO infinite loop risk (correctness: loop must always make progress by removing an item or stopping)

## Approach
Extract a pure resolve-one-item function from the existing ::resolve-top event handler. This function takes db and returns {:db new-db} or {:db new-db :pending-selection data}. Refactor ::resolve-top to be a thin wrapper around resolve-one-item. Add new ::resolve-all event handler that snapshots current stack-item IDs, then loops calling resolve-one-item, stopping when: selection needed, stack empty, or top item not in snapshot. Add Yield All button to controls UI.

## Architecture
- events/game.cljs: Extract resolve-one-item, refactor ::resolve-top, add ::resolve-all
- views/controls.cljs: Add Yield All button next to Yield
- Subscription: Reuse existing stack subscription for button enable/disable logic

## Parallelism Map

### Independent Work Streams
1. **Extract + refactor** (events/game.cljs)
   - Extract resolve-one-item, refactor ::resolve-top, add ::resolve-all
   - Estimated complexity: medium
2. **UI button** (views/controls.cljs)
   - Add Yield All button, wire to ::resolve-all
   - Estimated complexity: low

### Stream Dependencies
- Stream 2 depends on Stream 1 (needs ::resolve-all event to exist)

### Suggested Waves
- Wave 1: Stream 1 (extract + refactor + new handler)
- Wave 2: Stream 2 (UI button)

### File Ownership Boundaries
| Stream | Owns (exclusive) | Shared (needs coordination) |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| Extract + refactor | events/game.cljs | — |
| UI button | views/controls.cljs | — |

### Parallelism Assessment
- Independent streams: 1 (sequential dependency)
- Recommendation: solo execution
- Rationale: Only 2 streams with dependency, not worth team coordination overhead

## Design Rationale
### Problem
Storm decks generate stacks of 10+ items. Currently each Yield click resolves one item, requiring 12+ clicks for a single Brain Freeze storm. This makes practice tedious and breaks flow.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- events/game.cljs:399-439 - ::resolve-top has 4 paths: spell, storm-copy, activated-ability, other
- events/game.cljs:269-293 - resolve-stack-item-effects handles storm copy creation
- events/game.cljs:380-396 - maybe-continue-cleanup runs after each resolution
- events/selection.cljs:576-702 - resolve-spell-with-selection handles 5 selection types
- views/controls.cljs:40-43 - Yield button dispatches ::resolve-top
- engine/stack.cljs:50-78 - Stack-item queries (get-top, get-all, remove)

**Selection types that pause resolution:**
1. Player targeting (:effect/target :any-player)
2. Tutor (search library)
3. Discard (player selection)
4. Scry (order top N)
5. Peek-and-select (Flash of Insight)

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Loop in single event handler
**What it is:** New ::resolve-all handler with an internal loop. Simple but duplicates resolution path logic from ::resolve-top.
**Why we looked at this:** Simplest possible implementation.
**Pros:** Simple, atomic state update, no flickering
**Cons:** Duplicates resolution logic from ::resolve-top
**⚠️ NOT CHOSEN BECAUSE:** Code duplication between ::resolve-top and ::resolve-all would diverge over time.

#### 2. Recursive dispatch with flag
**What it is:** Set a flag in app-db, have ::resolve-top auto-dispatch itself when flag is set.
**Why we looked at this:** Reuses existing ::resolve-top directly.
**Pros:** No duplication
**Cons:** Multiple React re-renders (flickering), complex flag management
**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Multiple re-frame dispatches cause flickering. Anti-pattern.
**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** re-frame adds batched dispatch support.

#### 3. Extract shared resolution function ✓
**What it is:** Extract resolve-one-item from ::resolve-top. Both handlers call it. ::resolve-all loops over it.
**Chosen because:** No duplication, clean architecture, atomic state update. Matches codebase pattern of helper functions called by event handlers.

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Extract resolve-one-item function
- Refactor ::resolve-top to use it
- New ::resolve-all event handler with snapshot loop
- Yield All button in controls UI
- Tests for new functionality

**Out of scope (deferred/never):**
- Auto-resolving selections (never - user must choose)
- Keyboard shortcut for Yield All (deferred to fizzle-txiw)
- Resolving newly-created items (never - by design requirement)

### Open Questions
- Should Yield All button be disabled during pending-selection? (likely yes, same as Yield)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| What happens at selections? | Stop at selection | Loop breaks on pending-selection, user completes selection manually |
| Resolve storm-created copies? | No, stop after original items | Snapshot IDs at click time, stop when top item not in snapshot |
| Implementation approach? | Extract shared function | Refactor resolve-top, share resolve-one-item with resolve-all |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### Stack Resolution Flow
**Question explored:** How does the current Yield button resolve one item?
**Sources consulted:**
- events/game.cljs:399-439 - 4-path resolution handler
- events/selection.cljs:576-702 - Selection detection and pausing
- engine/stack.cljs - Stack-item queries

**Findings:**
- Resolution has 4 distinct paths based on stack-item type
- 5 selection types can pause resolution
- Storm resolution creates new stack-items mid-resolution
- maybe-continue-cleanup runs after each resolution for cleanup phase handling

**Conclusion:** All 4 paths can be unified into resolve-one-item returning {:db db} or {:db db :pending-selection data}

### Dead-End Paths
None — approach was straightforward after codebase research.

### Open Concerns Raised
- History logging: Each resolved item should still create individual history entries → Confirmed: resolution functions handle their own logging internally
close_reason: All success criteria met. resolve-one-item extracted, ::resolve-top refactored, ::resolve-all with snapshot loop and per-item history logging, Yield All button added. 12 new tests, make validate passes.

title: Add card definition validation via cljs.spec and decompose init-game-state
description:
## Goal
Implement load-time card definition validation using cljs.spec.alpha and decompose init-game-state into <30 line helpers. These are the final remaining success criteria for the architecture hardening epic.

## Context
All other epic tasks complete. Three success criteria remain:
1. Card spec namespace validates: required fields, valid effect types, valid cost types, mana cost structure
2. Spec validation runs at game init time (invalid card data throws descriptive error)
3. init-game-state decomposed into <30 line helpers

## Implementation Steps
1. Create engine/card_spec.cljs with cljs.spec.alpha definitions for card data shapes
2. Enumerate all valid effect types, cost types, condition types from current card definitions
3. Write specs for required card fields (:card/name, :card/type, :card/mana-cost, etc.)
4. Write specs for effect maps, cost maps, condition maps, mana cost structure
5. Add validation function that runs specs against all card definitions
6. Wire validation into game init flow (init-game-state in events/game.cljs)
7. Decompose init-game-state into helper functions (<30 lines each)
8. Verify all 58 existing card definitions pass validation
9. Verify invalid card data throws descriptive error
10. Run make validate

## Success Criteria
- [ ] engine/card_spec.cljs exists with comprehensive specs
- [ ] All 58 existing card definitions pass spec validation unchanged
- [ ] Invalid card data throws descriptive error at init time
- [ ] init-game-state main function body is <30 lines
- [ ] init-game-state uses extracted helpers for player/opponent/deck creation
- [ ] make validate passes
design:
## Goal
Create engine/card_spec.cljs with cljs.spec.alpha definitions for card data shapes, wire spec validation into game init, and decompose init-game-state into <30 line helpers. These are the final 3 success criteria for the architecture hardening epic.

## Effort Estimate
8-10 hours (single task, tightly coupled components)

## Context
All other epic tasks complete. Remaining success criteria:
1. Card spec namespace validates: required fields, valid effect types, valid cost types, mana cost structure
2. Spec validation runs at game init time (invalid card data throws descriptive error)
3. init-game-state decomposed into <30 line helpers

## Implementation Checklist

### Part A: Card Spec Namespace (engine/card_spec.cljs)

**Step 1: Create engine/card_spec.cljs with effect type enum**

All 19 valid effect types (from effects.cljs defmethods):
:add-mana, :mill, :lose-life, :gain-life, :deal-damage, :add-counters,
:draw, :exile-self, :discard-hand, :return-from-graveyard, :sacrifice,
:destroy, :discard, :tutor, :scry, :peek-and-select, :grant-flashback,
:add-restriction, :storm-copies

**Step 2: Cost type enum**
Valid cost types (from costs.cljs defmethods):
:pay-life, :exile-cards, :tap, :sacrifice-self, :remove-counter, :discard-hand, :mana

**Step 3: Condition type enum**
Valid condition types (from conditions.cljs defmethods):
:threshold, :no-counters

**Step 4: Mana cost spec**
Map of optional color keys -> nat-int, plus optional :x true:
{:white nat-int? :blue nat-int? :black nat-int? :red nat-int? :green nat-int? :colorless nat-int? :x boolean?}
Lands have empty mana cost {} — must be valid.

**Step 5: Color and type enums**
Colors: #{:white :blue :black :red :green}
Card types: #{:land :instant :sorcery :enchantment :artifact :creature}
Keywords: #{:storm :threshold :flashback} (plus any others in use)

**Step 6: Effect map spec**
Required: :effect/type (from enum)
Optional fields vary by type — use s/keys with :opt for all possible effect keys:
:effect/mana, :effect/amount, :effect/target, :effect/count, :effect/selection,
:effect/criteria, :effect/destination, :effect/counter-type, :effect/condition,
:effect/target-ref, :effect/x, :effect/select-count, :effect/selected-zone,
:effect/remainder-zone, :effect/order-remainder?, :restriction/type

**Step 7: Ability map spec**
Required: :ability/type (:mana, :activated, :triggered)
Optional: :ability/cost, :ability/effects, :ability/targeting, :ability/condition,
:ability/produces, :ability/name, :ability/description

**Step 8: Targeting spec**
Required: :target/id, :target/type
Optional: :target/zone, :target/controller, :target/criteria, :target/options, :target/required

**Step 9: Alternate cost spec (flashback)**
Required: :alternate/id, :alternate/zone, :alternate/mana-cost
Optional: :alternate/additional-costs, :alternate/on-resolve

**Step 10: Top-level card spec**
Required keys: :card/id (keyword), :card/name (string), :card/cmc (nat-int), :card/mana-cost, :card/colors (set), :card/types (set), :card/text (string)
Optional keys: :card/effects, :card/abilities, :card/etb-effects, :card/triggers, :card/targeting, :card/alternate-costs, :card/keywords, :card/subtypes, :card/supertypes, :card/conditional-effects, :card/kicker, :card/kicked-effects

**Step 11: Validation function**
(defn validate-cards! [cards] ...) — runs s/valid? on each card.
On failure: throws ex-info with s/explain-data for first invalid card, including card name/id.

### Part B: Wire Validation into Game Init

**Step 12: Call validate-cards! in init-game-state**
After loading cards into conn (line 79 in events/game.cljs), call:
(card-spec/validate-cards! cards/all-cards)
This happens before any game state setup, so invalid cards fail fast.

### Part C: Decompose init-game-state

Current function is 103 lines (lines 67-170 in events/game.cljs). Extract helpers:

**Step 13: Extract create-player-tx helper**
Returns transaction data for a player entity. Takes player-id, name, life, is-opponent? params.
~10 lines.

**Step 14: Extract create-objects-tx helper**
Takes card-ids, zone, player-eid, conn -> returns transaction vector of :object/* entities.
Handles :hand and :library zones (with position indexing for library).
~15 lines.

**Step 15: Extract create-opponent-library-tx helper**
Creates 40 dark-ritual objects for opponent library.
~8 lines.

**Step 16: Extract create-game-state-tx helper**
Returns transaction data for :game/* entity.
Takes player-eid, turn=1, phase=:main1.
~8 lines.

**Step 17: Rewrite init-game-state using helpers**
Main body should be ~25 lines: create conn, validate cards, transact cards, create players, process deck, create objects, create game state, return app-db.

### Part D: Verification

**Step 18: Write tests for card spec validation**
In src/test/fizzle/engine/card_spec_test.cljs:
- test-valid-card-passes: minimal valid card def passes spec
- test-missing-required-field-fails: card without :card/name fails
- test-invalid-effect-type-fails: card with {:effect/type :nonexistent} fails
- test-invalid-mana-cost-fails: card with {:card/mana-cost "bad"} fails
- test-empty-mana-cost-valid: land with {} mana cost passes
- test-all-existing-cards-valid: all 58 cards from cards/all-cards pass
- test-validation-error-descriptive: validate-cards! error includes card name

**Step 19: Write tests for init-game-state decomposition**
Existing tests in events/game_test.cljs likely cover init behavior — verify they still pass.
No new tests needed if existing tests cover init (decomposition is behavior-preserving).

**Step 20: Run make validate**

## Success Criteria
- [ ] engine/card_spec.cljs exists with specs for all 19 effect types, 7 cost types, 2 condition types
- [ ] Spec covers required card fields (:card/id, :card/name, :card/cmc, :card/mana-cost, :card/colors, :card/types, :card/text)
- [ ] Spec covers optional nested structures (effects, abilities, targeting, alternate-costs)
- [ ] All 58 existing card definitions pass spec validation unchanged (test)
- [ ] validate-cards! throws ex-info with card name + explain-data on invalid card (test)
- [ ] Card without required field fails validation (test with missing :card/name)
- [ ] Card with unknown effect type fails validation (test with :effect/type :nonexistent)
- [ ] init-game-state main function body is <30 lines (count after decomposition)
- [ ] Extracted helpers: create-player-tx, create-objects-tx, create-opponent-library-tx, create-game-state-tx
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + all tests)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Lands with Empty Mana Cost**
Lands have :card/mana-cost {} — spec must accept empty map as valid.
Test: Cephalid Coliseum, Gemstone Mine, basic lands all pass.

**Edge Case: X Mana Costs**
Some cards have :x true in mana cost map (e.g., Flash of Insight).
Spec must accept {:colorless N :blue 1 :x true} shape.

**Edge Case: Cards with No Effects**
Some cards may have no :card/effects key (lands with only abilities).
All effect-related keys are optional — spec must not require :card/effects.

**Edge Case: Nested Ability Structures**
Abilities contain nested effects, targeting, conditions, costs.
Spec must recursively validate these nested structures.

**Edge Case: Conditional Effects Map**
:card/conditional-effects has structure {condition-keyword [effects-vec]}.
Spec must handle this map-of-vectors shape.

**Performance: Spec Validation at Init**
58 cards * spec validation = negligible cost. No performance concern.
But validation only runs once at init, so even slow validation is acceptable.

**Backward Compatibility**
Epic anti-pattern: NO breaking changes to card definition data format.
Spec must describe existing shapes, NOT prescribe new requirements.
All 58 existing cards MUST pass without modification.

**Error Message Quality**
validate-cards! must produce actionable error messages.
Bad: "Spec validation failed"
Good: "Invalid card definition for 'Dark Ritual': :effect/type :nonexistent is not a valid effect type"
Use s/explain-str or s/explain-data to get field-level detail.

## Anti-Patterns
- NO breaking changes to existing card definition data format (all 58 cards must pass unchanged)
- NO overly strict specs that reject valid but unusual card shapes
- NO runtime-only validation (must run at init, not lazily)
- NO atoms or mutable state in card_spec.cljs (engine layer purity)
- NO requires from card_spec.cljs to events/ layer (engine -> engine only)
- NO changing init-game-state behavior during decomposition (extract only, preserve semantics)
- NO tautological test assertions (is (some? x)) — assert exact values or specific error content
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement pure hypergeometric probability math module
description: Pure math functions for exact hypergeometric probability calculations — the foundation for the live calculator.
design:
## Goal
Create engine/probability.cljs with pure functions for all three tiers of probability calculation. This is the foundation — everything else (subscriptions, views, persistence) builds on these functions.

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing code patterns
- engine/effects.cljs — example of pure engine module with no re-frame dependency
- engine/mana.cljs — example of data-in data-out functions
- Pattern: functions take plain data (numbers, maps), return plain data

### 2. Write tests first (TDD)

Test file: src/test/fizzle/engine/probability_test.cljs

**Tier 1 — Binomial coefficient:**
- C(0,0) = 1
- C(5,0) = 1, C(5,5) = 1
- C(5,2) = 10
- C(10,3) = 120
- C(60,7) = 386206920 (opening hand from 60-card deck)
- C(n,k) = C(n, n-k) symmetry: C(60,7) = C(60,53)
- C(n,0) = 1 for any n
- C(0,k) = 0 for k > 0

**Tier 2 — Hypergeometric PMF:**
- P(exactly 0 of 4 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.60044 (C(4,0)*C(56,7)/C(60,7) = 231917400/386206920)
- P(exactly 1 of 4 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.33624 (C(4,1)*C(56,6)/C(60,7) = 4*32468436/386206920)
- Sum of P(k=0..4) for K=4, n=7, N=60 = 1.0 (within 1e-10 tolerance)
- Edge: K=0 → P(k=0) = 1.0, P(k>0) = 0.0
- Edge: n=0 → P(k=0) = 1.0
- Edge: K=n → P(k=K) = C(K,K)*C(N-K,0)/C(N,K)

**Tier 3 — At-least CDF:**
- P(>=1 of 4 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.39956 — cross-check: 1 - PMF(k=0)
- P(>=1 of 24 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.99983 — land in opening hand
- P(>=3 of 24 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.74513 — 3+ lands in opener
- Boundary: P(>=0 of K in n from N) = 1.0
- Boundary: P(>=(K+1) of K in n from N) = 0.0
- Boundary: P(>=1 of K in 0 from N) = 0.0

**Tier 4 — Multivariate joint:**
- Single-group equivalence: joint with one group equals at-least CDF
- P(>=1 of 8 rituals AND >=1 of 20 lands in 7 from 60): verify sum over all valid (a>=1, b>=1) combinations of C(8,a)*C(20,b)*C(32,7-a-b)/C(60,7)
- Zero-count edge: any group with count=0 and min>=1 → total probability = 0.0
- Disjoint coverage: if groups cover all N cards and n is large enough, P ≈ 1.0

**Tier 5 — Sequential conditional:**
- Single-step equivalence: sequential with one step equals joint/at-least result
- Two-step with disjoint targets: P(>=1 of 4 rituals in 3 draws THEN >=1 of 2 LED in 5 draws from 60)
  Algorithm: Σ over (r=1..3, l=0..min(2,3-r)) of [C(4,r)*C(2,l)*C(54,3-r-l)/C(60,3)] * P(>=1 of (2-l) in 5 from 57)
  Note: must enumerate LED draws in step 1 even though step 1 doesn't target them
- Step with draw-count=0 → no cards drawn, step trivially succeeds if min-count=0 for all targets, fails if any min-count>0
- Monotonicity: more draws → higher probability (for same targets)

### 3. Implementation checklist

src/main/fizzle/engine/probability.cljs:
- [ ] binomial-coefficient [n k] — iterative C(n,k), avoids overflow, uses k > n-k optimization to minimize multiplications
- [ ] hypergeometric-pmf [N K n k] — P(exactly k successes) = C(K,k)*C(N-K,n-k)/C(N,n)
- [ ] at-least-probability [N K n min-k] — P(>=min-k) by summing PMF from min-k to min(K,n). Returns 1.0 for min-k=0.
- [ ] joint-probability [N groups n] — multivariate hypergeometric for disjoint groups.
      groups = [{:count K :min min-k} ...]
      "other" count = N - sum(group counts)
      Enumerate all valid combinations (k1, k2, ...) where ki >= mini AND ki <= Ki AND sum(ki) <= n AND (n - sum(ki)) <= other-count.
      For each combo: C(K1,k1)*C(K2,k2)*...*C(other, n-sum(ki)) / C(N,n)
      Sum all valid combo probabilities.
- [ ] sequential-probability [N all-groups steps] — conditional chain with cross-step consumption.
      CRITICAL ALGORITHM: Each step's draw must enumerate draws for ALL target groups across ALL steps, not just current step's targets.
      This is because step 1 can "accidentally" draw cards that are targets for step 2, reducing step 2's available copies.
      
      Algorithm:
      1. Collect all unique target groups across all steps → [g1, g2, ..., gM]
      2. For step 1 with draw count n1:
         Enumerate all (k1, k2, ..., kM) where:
         - ki >= 0 for all i
         - ki >= mini for groups targeted by this step (success condition)
         - ki <= Gi (can't draw more than exist)
         - sum(ki) <= n1 (can't draw more than draw count)
         - n1 - sum(ki) <= other-count (enough "other" cards)
      3. For each valid combo:
         p_combo = C(G1,k1)*C(G2,k2)*...*C(other, n1-sum(ki)) / C(N, n1)
         Recursively compute remaining steps with:
         - N' = N - n1
         - Gi' = Gi - ki for each group
         - other' = other - (n1 - sum(ki))
         p_rest = sequential-probability(N', updated-groups, remaining-steps)
         total += p_combo * p_rest
      4. Return total (this IS P(all steps succeed), not conditional)

src/test/fizzle/engine/probability_test.cljs:
- [ ] binomial-coefficient-test — 8 cases: zero, identity, small, large (C(60,7)), symmetry, C(0,k>0)=0
- [ ] hypergeometric-pmf-test — 6 cases with exact expected values (see test specs above), sum-to-one invariant
- [ ] at-least-probability-test — 6 cases including boundaries, cross-check with 1-PMF(0)
- [ ] joint-probability-test — 4 cases: single-group equivalence, two-group with known value, zero-count edge, coverage edge
- [ ] sequential-probability-test — 4 cases: single-step equivalence, two-step with cross-group consumption, zero-draw step, monotonicity check

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All five functions implemented as pure functions (no re-frame, no Datascript, no side effects)
- [ ] At least 28 test assertions covering all tiers and edge cases
- [ ] Concrete MTG probabilities verified: P(>=1 of 4 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.39956, P(>=3 of 24 in 7 from 60) ≈ 0.74513
- [ ] Sum-to-one invariant holds: Σ PMF(k=0..min(K,n)) = 1.0 within 1e-10
- [ ] Equivalence: sequential with 1 step = at-least; joint with 1 group = at-least
- [ ] Cross-step consumption: sequential correctly reduces future step target counts based on earlier draws
- [ ] All floating-point comparisons in tests use tolerance (1e-6), not exact equality
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO re-frame or Datascript imports (purity: this module must be pure functions with zero framework dependency)
- ❌ NO floating-point equality in tests — use (< (abs (- expected actual)) 1e-6) (precision: floating-point accumulation makes exact equality unreliable)
- ❌ NO recursive binomial-coefficient (overflow: iterative approach avoids stack overflow and maintains integer precision)
- ❌ NO card names or identities in probability functions (data-over-code: functions take counts and numbers only)
- ❌ NO mutable state (atoms, vars) in any function (purity: all functions must be referentially transparent)
- ❌ NO ignoring cross-step target consumption in sequential calculation (correctness: step 1 can draw step 2's targets, must enumerate all groups in every step)

## Key Considerations (SRE REVIEW)

**Edge Case: Invalid Inputs**
- K > N (more targets than population): return 0.0 — impossible to have more targets than cards
- n > N (drawing more than available): return 0.0 — impossible draw
- min-k > K (wanting more than exist): return 0.0
- Negative values: treat as 0
- These should be handled gracefully (return 0.0), not throw exceptions

**Edge Case: Overlapping Groups Within a Step**
- Groups within a step MUST be disjoint (a card belongs to exactly one group)
- The UI layer (not this module) is responsible for preventing overlap
- If overlap occurs, results will be mathematically wrong but won't crash
- Document this precondition in function docstring

**Edge Case: Cross-Step Target Consumption (CRITICAL)**
- When step 1 draws n1 cards, some may be targets for step 2 even though step 1 doesn't require them
- Example: step 1 draws 3 cards for rituals. Of those 3, one might be an LED (step 2's target)
- The sequential algorithm MUST enumerate all groups across all steps in each step's draw
- Failing to do this gives WRONG probabilities (over-estimates step 2 odds)

**Floating-Point Precision**
- binomial-coefficient should use integer arithmetic (JavaScript safe integers go up to 2^53)
- C(60,30) ≈ 1.18e17 < 2^53 ≈ 9e15... actually C(60,30) > 2^53, so this IS a concern
- C(60,7) = 386206920, C(60,10) ≈ 7.5e10 — these are fine
- For typical inputs (N<=60, n<=15), intermediate products stay within safe integer range
- If needed, use the iterative approach that divides at each step to keep values smaller
- Test with C(60,30) to verify precision — if it fails, document the N/k limits

**Performance**
- Multivariate enumeration: for M groups with max K copies each, worst case is K1*K2*...*KM iterations
- For typical MTG inputs (2-4 groups, 4-8 copies each): ~100-500 iterations per step — instant
- Sequential with 3 steps and 4 groups: ~100^3 = 1M iterations worst case — still <100ms
- No optimization needed for v1; add memoization only if profiling shows need

**Reference: Multivariate Hypergeometric Formula**
P(k1 of K1, k2 of K2, ..., kr of Kr in n draws from N) =
  C(K1,k1) * C(K2,k2) * ... * C(Kr,kr) * C(N-K1-K2-...-Kr, n-k1-k2-...-kr) / C(N,n)
where all groups are disjoint and K1+K2+...+Kr <= N
notes:
## SRE Refinement Notes

### Concrete joint probability test value (pre-computed):
P(>=1 of 2 AND >=1 of 3 in 5 from 10) = 176/252 ≈ 0.69841
Verified via inclusion-exclusion:
  P(A=0) = C(2,0)*C(8,5)/C(10,5) = 56/252
  P(B=0) = C(3,0)*C(7,5)/C(10,5) = 21/252
  P(A=0 AND B=0) = C(5,5)/C(10,5) = 1/252
  P(A>=1 AND B>=1) = 1 - 56/252 - 21/252 + 1/252 = 176/252

Use this as the "two-group" test case in joint-probability-test instead of the large 60-card case (easier to verify, same algorithmic coverage).

### Test checklist clarification:
- joint-probability-test "two-group with known value" means: use the N=10 case above with expected ≈ 0.69841
- All test expected values should be pre-computed with formulas shown, not "verify against manual calculation"
