title: Goldfish bot skips land play: yield cascade races ahead of bot interceptor
description:
## Bug
Goldfish bot yields through its entire turn without playing a land.

## Root Cause
yield-advance-phase for bot turns always returns :continue-yield? true (line 848-851 of game.cljs). This queues another ::yield which runs BEFORE the interceptor's ::bot-decide dispatch. The yield cascade advances through all phases before ::bot-decide gets to execute.

## Fix
For bot turns at priority-granting phases (upkeep, draw, main1, combat, main2, end), return WITHOUT :continue-yield?. The bot interceptor will fire and dispatch ::bot-decide, which either acts (play land, cast spell) or passes (dispatches ::yield to advance).

For non-priority phases (untap), keep :continue-yield? to zip through them.

## Related
Part of epic fizzle-2kge (Event-Driven Game Loop)
close_reason: Fixed: two bugs in yield system — negotiate-priority auto-pass checked wrong entity during bot turn, and yield-advance-phase used active player instead of priority holder for path selection. Human yield at opponent-turn stop now correctly batch-advances to next stop.

title: Task 2: Wire spell casting to create stack-items and resolve from stack-items
description: Wire cast-spell-mode to create a stack-item alongside moving the card to the stack zone. Update ::resolve-top to resolve from stack-items instead of comparing trigger/object entities.
design:
## Goal
When a spell is cast, create a stack-item entity of type :spell referencing the game object. Update the ::resolve-top handler to check stack-items for spells, falling back to legacy triggers. This establishes the new resolution path for spells while maintaining backward compatibility with triggers.

## Context
Completed Task 1 (fizzle-ndvj): engine/stack.cljs exists with create-stack-item, get-top-stack-item, remove-stack-item, resolve-effect-target. Schema additions in place. 17 tests passing.

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours

## Implementation

### Step 1: Add get-stack-item-by-object-ref to engine/stack.cljs

Add a query function to find a stack-item by its object reference:

\`\`\`clojure
(defn get-stack-item-by-object-ref
  \"Find the stack-item that references a given game object EID.
   Returns entity map or nil.\"
  [db object-eid]
  (d/q '[:find (pull ?e [*]) .
         :in $ ?obj
         :where [?e :stack-item/object-ref ?obj]]
       db object-eid))
\`\`\`

This is needed for cleaning up the stack-item when a spell resolves. The resolution code knows the object-id, not the stack-item EID.

### Step 2: Replace set-stack-order with create-stack-item in cast-spell-mode (engine/rules.cljs)

**Current pipeline** (lines 339-358):
\`\`\`clojure
(-> db
    (mana/pay-mana player-id (:mode/mana-cost mode))
    (pay-additional-costs player-id object-id (:mode/additional-costs mode))
    (zones/move-to-zone object-id :stack)
    (set-stack-order object-id)        ;; <-- REPLACE THIS
    (set-cast-mode object-id mode)
    (increment-storm player-id)
    (maybe-create-storm-trigger player-id object-id))
\`\`\`

**New pipeline:**
\`\`\`clojure
(-> db
    (mana/pay-mana player-id (:mode/mana-cost mode))
    (pay-additional-costs player-id object-id (:mode/additional-costs mode))
    (zones/move-to-zone object-id :stack)
    (create-spell-stack-item player-id object-id)  ;; <-- NEW
    (set-cast-mode object-id mode)
    (increment-storm player-id)
    (maybe-create-storm-trigger player-id object-id))
\`\`\`

**New private helper** \`create-spell-stack-item\`:
\`\`\`clojure
(defn- create-spell-stack-item
  \"Create a stack-item for a spell being cast.
   Also sets object/position to the same value for backward compatibility.\"
  [db player-id object-id]
  (let [obj-eid (d/q '[:find ?e .
                       :in $ ?oid
                       :where [?e :object/id ?oid]]
                     db object-id)
        db-with-item (stack/create-stack-item db
                       {:stack-item/type :spell
                        :stack-item/controller player-id
                        :stack-item/source object-id
                        :stack-item/object-ref obj-eid})
        ;; Get the position that was auto-assigned to the stack-item
        stack-item (stack/get-stack-item-by-object-ref db-with-item obj-eid)
        position (:stack-item/position stack-item)]
    ;; Set object/position to match stack-item for backward compat
    (d/db-with db-with-item [[:db/add obj-eid :object/position position]])))
\`\`\`

**Critical: Same position for both.** The stack-item auto-assigns position via get-next-stack-order. We then set the object's position to the SAME value. This ensures backward-compatible code that reads object/position still works. Tests that check object/position will continue to pass.

**DO NOT store effects on the stack-item yet.** Resolution code (resolve-spell-with-selection, resolve-spell) still reads effects from the object/card at resolution time. Storing effects on the stack-item is deferred to a later task when resolution is unified.

**Require addition:** Add \`[fizzle.engine.stack :as stack]\` to rules.cljs requires.

### Step 3: Update create-spell-copy to also create stack-items (engine/triggers.cljs)

In \`create-spell-copy\` (lines 151-179), after creating the copy object, also create a stack-item.

**Problem:** \`create-spell-copy\` currently uses \`d/db-with\` to create the copy and doesn't return the new entity's EID. We need the EID for \`:stack-item/object-ref\`.

**Solution:** After the d/db-with, query for the newly created entity. The copy has a unique random UUID as \`:object/id\`, so we can find it.

\`\`\`clojure
(defn create-spell-copy
  [db source-object-id controller-id]
  (if-let [source-obj (q/get-object db source-object-id)]
    (let [owner-eid (:db/id (:object/owner source-obj))
          controller-eid (q/get-player-eid db controller-id)
          card-eid (:db/id (:object/card source-obj))
          copy-id (random-uuid)
          ;; Create the copy object (no stack-order — stack-item handles ordering)
          db-with-copy (d/db-with db [{:object/id copy-id
                                       :object/card card-eid
                                       :object/zone :stack
                                       :object/owner owner-eid
                                       :object/controller controller-eid
                                       :object/tapped false
                                       :object/is-copy true}])
          ;; Get the copy's EID for stack-item ref
          copy-eid (d/q '[:find ?e .
                          :in $ ?oid
                          :where [?e :object/id ?oid]]
                        db-with-copy copy-id)
          ;; Create stack-item for the copy
          db-with-item (stack/create-stack-item db-with-copy
                         {:stack-item/type :storm-copy
                          :stack-item/controller controller-id
                          :stack-item/source source-object-id
                          :stack-item/object-ref copy-eid
                          :stack-item/is-copy true})
          ;; Set object/position to match stack-item (backward compat)
          stack-item (stack/get-stack-item-by-object-ref db-with-item copy-eid)
          position (:stack-item/position stack-item)]
      (d/db-with db-with-item [[:db/add copy-eid :object/position position]]))
    db))
\`\`\`

**Note:** Remove the \`stack-order (q/get-next-stack-order db)\` and \`:object/position stack-order\` from the original — the stack-item now handles ordering.

**Require addition:** Add \`[fizzle.engine.stack :as stack]\` to triggers.cljs requires.

### Step 4: Update ::resolve-top to use stack-items for spells (events/game.cljs)

Replace the current dual-entity comparison in ::resolve-top (lines 364-410).

**New logic:**
1. Get top stack-item via \`stack/get-top-stack-item\`
2. Get top legacy trigger via \`queries/get-stack-items\` (first)
3. Compare their positions (stack-item/position vs trigger/stack-order)
4. Resolve the higher one
5. For spell stack-items: get object-id from object-ref, call resolve-spell-with-selection, then remove stack-item
6. For legacy triggers: use existing trigger resolution code unchanged

\`\`\`clojure
(rf/reg-event-db
  ::resolve-top
  (fn [db _]
    (let [game-db (:game/db db)
          ;; Check new stack-items (spells create these now)
          top-stack-item (stack/get-top-stack-item game-db)
          stack-item-pos (when top-stack-item (:stack-item/position top-stack-item))
          ;; Check legacy triggers (still used for triggers until Task 3)
          stack-triggers (queries/get-stack-items game-db)
          top-trigger (first stack-triggers)
          trigger-order (when top-trigger (:trigger/stack-order top-trigger))
          ;; Determine what to resolve (LIFO: highest position first)
          resolve-stack-item? (and stack-item-pos
                                   (or (not trigger-order)
                                       (> stack-item-pos trigger-order)))
          resolve-trigger? (and trigger-order
                                (or (not stack-item-pos)
                                    (> trigger-order stack-item-pos)))]
      (cond
        ;; Resolve spell via stack-item
        (and resolve-stack-item? (= :spell (:stack-item/type top-stack-item)))
        (let [obj-ref (:stack-item/object-ref top-stack-item)
              obj (d/pull game-db [:object/id] obj-ref)
              object-id (:object/id obj)
              stack-item-eid (:db/id top-stack-item)
              result (selection/resolve-spell-with-selection game-db :player-1 object-id)]
          (if (:pending-selection result)
            (-> db
                (assoc :game/db (:db result))
                (assoc :game/pending-selection (:pending-selection result)))
            (maybe-continue-cleanup
              (assoc db :game/db (stack/remove-stack-item (:db result) stack-item-eid)))))

        ;; Resolve storm copy via stack-item
        (and resolve-stack-item? (= :storm-copy (:stack-item/type top-stack-item)))
        (let [obj-ref (:stack-item/object-ref top-stack-item)
              obj (d/pull game-db [:object/id] obj-ref)
              object-id (:object/id obj)
              stack-item-eid (:db/id top-stack-item)
              result (selection/resolve-spell-with-selection game-db :player-1 object-id)]
          (if (:pending-selection result)
            (-> db
                (assoc :game/db (:db result))
                (assoc :game/pending-selection (:pending-selection result)))
            (maybe-continue-cleanup
              (assoc db :game/db (stack/remove-stack-item (:db result) stack-item-eid)))))

        ;; Resolve activated ability trigger with selection handling (legacy)
        (and resolve-trigger? (= :activated-ability (:trigger/type top-trigger)))
        (let [result (selection/resolve-activated-ability-with-selection game-db top-trigger)]
          (if (:pending-selection result)
            (-> db
                (assoc :game/db (:db result))
                (assoc :game/pending-selection (:pending-selection result)))
            (maybe-continue-cleanup
              (assoc db :game/db (:db result)))))

        ;; Resolve other triggers normally (legacy)
        resolve-trigger?
        (maybe-continue-cleanup
          (assoc db :game/db (resolve-top-of-stack game-db :player-1)))

        ;; Empty stack
        :else db))))
\`\`\`

**Key: Stack-item removal.** When a spell resolves (no pending selection), we remove the stack-item from the db. When a spell pauses for selection, we do NOT remove the stack-item yet — it stays on the stack until selection is confirmed. This means we also need to update selection confirmation handlers to remove the stack-item (Step 6).

**Require addition:** Add \`[fizzle.engine.stack :as stack]\` to game.cljs requires.

### Step 5: Update resolve-top-of-stack helper (events/game.cljs)

This pure helper (lines 245-284) is used during cleanup for non-selection resolution. Update to check stack-items:

\`\`\`clojure
(defn resolve-top-of-stack
  [db player-id]
  (let [top-stack-item (stack/get-top-stack-item db)
        stack-item-pos (when top-stack-item (:stack-item/position top-stack-item))
        stack-triggers (queries/get-stack-items db)
        top-trigger (first stack-triggers)
        trigger-order (when top-trigger (:trigger/stack-order top-trigger))]
    (cond
      ;; Stack-item has priority (spell or storm copy)
      (and stack-item-pos
           (or (not trigger-order) (> stack-item-pos trigger-order)))
      (let [obj-ref (:stack-item/object-ref top-stack-item)
            obj (d/pull db [:object/id] obj-ref)
            object-id (:object/id obj)
            db-resolved (rules/resolve-spell db player-id object-id)]
        (stack/remove-stack-item db-resolved (:db/id top-stack-item)))

      ;; Legacy trigger has priority
      (and trigger-order
           (or (not stack-item-pos) (> trigger-order stack-item-pos)))
      (-> db
          (triggers/resolve-trigger top-trigger)
          (triggers/remove-trigger (:trigger/id top-trigger)))

      :else db)))
\`\`\`

### Step 6: Update selection confirmation to remove stack-items

When a spell pauses for selection (::resolve-top doesn't remove the stack-item), the selection confirmation handler needs to remove it. The confirmation handlers are in events/selection.cljs.

**Key handlers to update:**
- \`::confirm-tutor-selection\` — after spell completes
- \`::confirm-player-target-selection\` — after spell completes
- \`::confirm-discard-selection\` — after spell completes
- \`::confirm-scry-selection\` — after spell completes
- \`::confirm-graveyard-selection\` — after spell completes
- \`::confirm-peek-selection\` — after spell completes

**Pattern for each:** After the existing resolution code runs, look up the stack-item by object-ref and remove it:
\`\`\`clojure
;; At end of spell resolution in confirmation handler:
(let [obj-eid (d/q '[:find ?e . :in $ ?oid :where [?e :object/id ?oid]] game-db object-id)
      stack-item (stack/get-stack-item-by-object-ref game-db-after obj-eid)]
  (if stack-item
    (stack/remove-stack-item game-db-after (:db/id stack-item))
    game-db-after))
\`\`\`

**IMPORTANT:** Only remove for spell-originated selections (check \`:selection/spell-id\` exists). Trigger-originated selections (\`:selection/trigger-id\`) still use legacy cleanup. Check \`:selection/source-type\` — if it's \`:trigger\`, don't touch stack-items.

### Step 7: Update queries/stack-empty? (db/queries.cljs)

Add stack-item check to the existing \`stack-empty?\` function (lines 142-146):

\`\`\`clojure
(defn stack-empty?
  \"Check if the stack has no items (no triggers, no spells, and no stack-items).\"
  [db player-id]
  (and (empty? (get-stack-items db))
       (empty? (get-objects-in-zone db player-id :stack))
       (nil? (d/q '[:find ?e .
                     :where [?e :stack-item/position _]]
                   db))))
\`\`\`

**Note:** We inline the query rather than requiring engine/stack to avoid circular dependencies (queries.cljs is used by engine namespaces).

### Step 8: Write/update tests (TDD)

**Test file:** Existing card tests should continue to pass without modification. Write new integration tests in a suitable location.

**New tests to add to stack_test.cljs:**

1. **test-create-spell-stack-item-with-cast** — Use \`rules/cast-spell-mode\` on a test card, verify both object on stack zone AND stack-item entity created, both with same position.
   - Bug caught: Stack-item not created during casting.

2. **test-storm-copy-creates-stack-item** — Cast a spell with :storm keyword, verify storm copies each have a stack-item with :stack-item/type :storm-copy and :stack-item/is-copy true.
   - Bug caught: Storm copies missing stack-items.

3. **test-stack-item-removed-after-spell-resolution** — Cast a spell, resolve via resolve-top-of-stack, verify stack-item is removed from DB.
   - Bug caught: Orphaned stack-items after resolution.

4. **test-legacy-trigger-resolves-alongside-spell-stack-items** — Create a legacy trigger AND cast a spell. Verify ::resolve-top resolves the higher-positioned item correctly.
   - Bug caught: Legacy trigger comparison broken.

5. **test-stack-empty-checks-stack-items** — Create a stack-item, verify queries/stack-empty? returns false. Remove it, verify true.
   - Bug caught: stack-empty? not checking new entity type.

6. **test-get-stack-item-by-object-ref** — Create stack-item with object-ref, query by ref, verify found.
   - Bug caught: Query function returns nil when item exists.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] cast-spell-mode creates a :spell stack-item with :stack-item/object-ref pointing to the object EID
- [ ] Object/position and stack-item/position have the SAME value for the same spell
- [ ] ::resolve-top reads from stack-items for spells and storm copies
- [ ] Stack-items are removed after spell resolution (both direct and via selection confirmation)
- [ ] Storm copies created by create-spell-copy each have a :storm-copy stack-item
- [ ] queries/stack-empty? checks stack-items in addition to triggers and objects
- [ ] All existing 826+ tests still pass (no regressions)
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)
- [ ] Legacy triggers still resolve correctly alongside spell stack-items (LIFO ordering preserved)
- [ ] Selection confirmation handlers clean up stack-items for spell-originated selections only

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO storing effects on the stack-item yet (resolution still reads from object/card; deferred to resolution unification task)
- NO removing set-stack-order completely (set object/position to match stack-item position for backward compat)
- NO modifying trigger creation to use stack-items (that's Task 3)
- NO changing selection builders (that's Task 4+)
- NO removing legacy trigger comparison from ::resolve-top (triggers still use it)
- NO requiring engine/stack from db/queries.cljs (avoid circular deps — inline the Datalog query)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Position Synchronization**
The stack-item and object MUST have the same position value. If they diverge, LIFO ordering breaks for code that still reads object/position. The create-spell-stack-item helper auto-assigns position via stack/create-stack-item, then copies it to the object. This is a single atomic db pipeline step.

**Edge Case: Selection Pause and Stack-Item Lifetime**
When a spell pauses for selection (pending-selection returned), the stack-item must NOT be removed. It stays on the stack until selection is confirmed. Each confirm-*-selection handler must check for and remove the stack-item. Only spell-originated selections (not trigger-originated) should remove stack-items.

**Edge Case: Storm Trigger Ordering**
maybe-create-storm-trigger fires AFTER create-spell-stack-item in the pipeline. The storm trigger uses q/get-next-stack-order which checks all three counter sources. Since create-spell-stack-item already created a stack-item with position N, the storm trigger will get position N+1. This is correct — storm trigger resolves before the spell.

**Edge Case: resolve-top-of-stack During Cleanup**
resolve-top-of-stack is called from maybe-continue-cleanup. It uses rules/resolve-spell directly (no selection handling). After this task, it must also check stack-items and remove them after resolution. The implementation in Step 5 handles this.

**Edge Case: copy EID Lookup After d/db-with**
After d/db-with creates a new entity, the EID is assigned by Datascript. We find it by querying for the unique :object/id (random UUID). This is safe because UUIDs don't collide.

**Migration Note**
During migration, both object/position and stack-item/position coexist for spells. This is intentional. Legacy code reading object/position still works. New code reads stack-item/position. In a later task, object/position for stack objects will be removed.

**Reference Implementation**
- Follow set-stack-order pattern (rules.cljs:318-326) for EID lookup
- Follow create-spell-copy pattern (triggers.cljs:151-179) for object creation + stack-item
- Follow ::resolve-top pattern (game.cljs:364-410) for the updated comparison logic
close_reason: Closed

title: Snapshot sharing: encode game state in URL hash fragment
description:
Capture complete game state at any fork point, encode to compact binary format, base64url-encode into a hash fragment URL. Opening the URL restores the exact game state for immediate play.

## Requirements

### Encoding Format
- Version byte at start determines card index bit width and format structure
- Card registry with explicit stable ordering (append-only, indices never change)
- Binary layout: header (life totals, storm count, phase, turn number, mana pool) → zone-by-zone card lists → battlefield card state (tapped, counters, grants) → stack items
- Library order preserved for idempotent puzzles
- Both players' complete state encoded
- Stack items encoded: card index + mode/targets/pending effects

### Card Index
- Explicit registry order, not alphabetical or hash-based
- New cards always appended, existing indices never shift
- Bit width determined by version byte (e.g., v1 = 6-bit/64 cards, v2 = 8-bit/256 cards)

### URL Structure
- Hash fragment: site/#s=<base64url-encoded-state>
- Pure client-side, no server dependency
- Graceful error if encoded state exceeds browser URL length limit (~2000 chars)

### UX
- Share button available at fork points
- Generates URL and copies to clipboard
- Opening URL with hash fragment restores exact game state
- User dropped into playable state immediately (no read-only preview)

### Architecture
- Foundation for future tactics system (no tactics metadata in v1)
- Versioned format for forward compatibility
- No server storage, QR codes, or alternative share formats in v1

## Anti-patterns
- Do NOT encode card definitions in the snapshot — cards are reference data in the registry
- Do NOT use raw Datascript DB dump — use purpose-built compact format
- Do NOT break existing card registry indices when adding new cards
close_reason: Closed

title: Replace casting cascade with data-driven pre-cast pipeline
description: Replace initiate-cast-with-mode's 7-branch conditional cascade with a data-driven pipeline of pre-cast steps using multimethod dispatch. Each step is a defmethod on evaluate-pre-cast-step. Pipeline ordering declared as keyword vector. Steps: flashback-adjustment, discard-specific-cost, x-mana-cost, targeting, mana-allocation. initiate-cast-with-mode becomes a pipeline loop. ADR-015 reference. Touches events/game.cljs (primary), events/selection/costs.cljs, events/selection/targeting.cljs.
design:
## Goal
Replace initiate-cast-with-mode's 7-branch conditional cascade with a data-driven pipeline of pre-cast steps using multimethod dispatch. Each step is a defmethod on evaluate-pre-cast-step. Pipeline ordering declared as keyword vector. initiate-cast-with-mode becomes a pipeline loop (~15 lines).

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours

## Implementation

### Architecture

The pipeline is a vector of step keywords evaluated in order. Each step is a defmethod that returns:
- nil → skip this step (condition not met, e.g., no X cost)
- {:selection sel} → pause for player input, resume after
- {:db db} → step handled casting, done (terminal)

Pipeline vector:
```clojure
(def pre-cast-pipeline
  [:exile-cards-cost :return-land-cost :discard-specific-cost :x-mana-cost :targeting :mana-allocation])
```

The pipeline loop:
```clojure
(defn- run-pre-cast-pipeline [app-db player-id object-id mode target]
  (let [game-db (:game/db app-db)
        ctx {:game-db game-db :player-id player-id
             :object-id object-id :mode mode :target target}]
    (loop [steps pre-cast-pipeline]
      (if (empty? steps)
        ;; All steps returned nil → cast immediately
        (-> app-db
            (assoc :game/db (rules/cast-spell-mode game-db player-id object-id mode))
            (dissoc :game/selected-card))
        (let [step (first steps)
              result (evaluate-pre-cast-step step ctx)]
          (cond
            (nil? result) (recur (rest steps))
            (:selection result) (-> app-db
                                    (assoc :game/pending-selection (:selection result))
                                    (dissoc :game/selected-card))
            (:db result) (-> app-db
                             (assoc :game/db (:db result))
                             (dissoc :game/selected-card))))))))
```

### Step 1: Define multimethod and pipeline vector in game.cljs

```clojure
(defmulti evaluate-pre-cast-step
  "Evaluate a single pre-cast step. Returns nil (skip), {:selection sel} (pause),
   or {:db db} (terminal — casting complete)."
  (fn [step _ctx] step))
```

### Step 2: Migrate each cond branch to a defmethod

**:exile-cards-cost** — checks has-exile-cards-x-cost?, builds selection
**:return-land-cost** — checks has-return-land-cost?, builds selection
**:discard-specific-cost** — checks has-discard-specific-cost?, builds selection
**:x-mana-cost** — checks has-mana-x-cost?, builds selection
**:targeting** — handles both pre-determined target and interactive targeting, including single-player-target auto-cast and modal spell targeting from chosen-mode
**:mana-allocation** — checks has-generic-mana-cost?, builds selection or casts (0 generic fallback)

### Step 3: Replace initiate-cast-with-mode body

Replace the 107-line cond body with call to run-pre-cast-pipeline.

### Step 4: Verify all tests pass

No test changes expected — this is a pure refactor of internal implementation.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] initiate-cast-with-mode is a pipeline loop (~15 lines), not 110-line cascade
- [ ] Each pre-cast step is independently testable as a defmethod
- [ ] Pre-cast pipeline ordering is a data vector, not branch placement
- [ ] All existing tests pass (no regressions)
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)
- [ ] Zero references to old cond branches in initiate-cast-with-mode (body replaced)

## Key Considerations

**Context map**: The ctx map carries {:game-db :player-id :object-id :mode :target}. Steps read what they need from ctx. Modal spell targeting needs to read :object/chosen-mode from the object, which :targeting step does internally (same as current code).

**Terminal vs selection results**: Most steps return either nil or {:selection sel}. The :targeting step with pre-determined target returns {:db db} (terminal — it calls confirm-cast-time-target directly). The :mana-allocation step returns {:db db} when generic cost is 0 (defensive fallback that casts directly).

**Pre-determined targets**: The :targeting step handles the target parameter from ctx. When target is non-nil and targeting-reqs exist, it auto-assigns and returns {:db db}. This preserves the cast-spell-handler opts flow.

**Single-player auto-target**: The :targeting step preserves the logic where a spell with exactly 1 valid player target auto-casts without showing selection dialog.

**No chaining changes**: The existing chaining (discard-cost → targeting, return-land → targeting, x-mana → mana-allocation, targeting → mana-allocation) remains handled by build-chain-selection methods. The pipeline only handles the FIRST pre-cast step; subsequent steps chain via selection lifecycle.

**Pipeline ordering matters**: exile-cards first because it determines X value before targeting. Targeting before mana-allocation because targeting chains to allocation when needed.

## Anti-Patterns
- NO card definition changes
- NO modifying selection executors or chain builders
- NO changing the selection system (confirm-selection-impl, lifecycle, etc.)
- NO introducing new app-db keys
- NO changing test assertions (pure internal refactor)
- NO functions stored in data structures
close_reason: Phase 3 complete: initiate-cast-with-mode replaced with data-driven pipeline. 6 defmethods on evaluate-pre-cast-step, pre-cast-pipeline data vector, ~17-line loop body replacing 107-line cascade. 2092 tests pass, zero regressions. Commit: cf3e301.

title: Epic: Enrich history entries with spell/land names and phase details
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- History entry descriptions must include card names for cast-spell, resolve-top, play-land, activate-mana-ability, and activate-ability events
- History entry descriptions must include phase names for advance-phase events (e.g. 'Advance to Main 1' not 'Advance phase')
- History entry descriptions must include turn number for start-turn events (e.g. 'Start Turn 3')
- Ability activations must show the source card name and disambiguating info (ability description) when the card has multiple abilities
- Targets must be included for spells and abilities that have them (e.g. 'Cast Tendrils targeting opponent')
- All description logic must be centralized in history/descriptions.cljs
- No changes to event handler signatures or event dispatch payloads
- init-game description remains 'Game started' (unchanged)

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] cast-spell shows card name (e.g. 'Cast Dark Ritual')
- [ ] cast-spell shows target when applicable (e.g. 'Cast Tendrils of Agony targeting opponent')
- [ ] resolve-top shows resolved card/item name (e.g. 'Resolve Dark Ritual')
- [ ] advance-phase shows phase name (e.g. 'Advance to Main 1')
- [ ] start-turn shows turn number (e.g. 'Start Turn 3')
- [ ] play-land shows card name (e.g. 'Play Swamp')
- [ ] activate-mana-ability shows source card name (e.g. 'Tap City of Brass')
- [ ] activate-ability shows source card name + ability info (e.g. 'Activate Cephalid Coliseum')
- [ ] Multi-ability cards show disambiguating info
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] New tests cover all enriched description cases
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO modifying event handler signatures or payloads (isolation: descriptions are a display concern, not a game logic concern)
- ❌ NO scattering description logic across event files (maintainability: centralize in descriptions.cljs)
- ❌ NO hardcoded card name strings in descriptions (correctness: always query from game-db entities)
- ❌ NO breaking existing description fallback behavior (safety: if card lookup fails, fall back to generic description)
- ❌ NO importing game event namespaces from descriptions.cljs (architecture: descriptions depends on db.queries only, not events)

## Approach
Enrich the existing describe-event function in history/descriptions.cljs by changing its signature from (describe-event event) to (describe-event event pre-game-db game-db-after). The interceptor already has both game-db snapshots available in its :after phase — pass them through. Each event case clause will query the appropriate game-db to extract card names, phase info, targets, and ability descriptions. Generic fallback descriptions remain as safety net.

## Architecture
- history/descriptions.cljs — Main changes: accept game-dbs, query for card names/phases/targets per event type
- history/interceptor.cljs — One-line change: pass pre-game-db and game-db-after to describe-event
- db/queries.cljs — May need to use existing query functions (get-object, get-game-state, stack queries)
- engine/stack.cljs — Read-only: use existing stack query functions to get top item info

Data flow: interceptor :after phase → describe-event(event, pre-db, post-db) → query relevant db → return enriched string

## Parallelism Map

### Independent Work Streams
1. **Description enrichment** (history/descriptions.cljs)
   - Core logic: querying game-dbs for card names, phases, targets
   - Estimated complexity: medium
2. **Interceptor wiring** (history/interceptor.cljs)
   - One-line change to pass game-dbs
   - Estimated complexity: low

### Stream Dependencies
- Stream 2 depends on Stream 1 (interceptor needs new function signature)
- Both streams are in the same logical change

### Suggested Waves
- Wave 1: Both streams together (they're tightly coupled, 2 files total)

### File Ownership Boundaries
| Stream | Owns (exclusive) | Shared (needs coordination) |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| Descriptions | history/descriptions.cljs | db/queries.cljs (read-only) |
| Interceptor | history/interceptor.cljs | — |

### Parallelism Assessment
- Independent streams: 0 (tightly coupled, sequential)
- Recommendation: solo execution
- Rationale: Only 2 files changed, tightly coupled, no benefit from parallelization

## Design Rationale
### Problem
History sidebar shows generic event names ('Cast spell', 'Play land', 'Advance phase') making it hard to scan game history. Players can't tell which spell was cast or what phase they advanced to without clicking to rewind. Enriched descriptions make the history log immediately useful.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- history/descriptions.cljs:4-18 — Current describe-event returns static strings, ignores event args entirely
- history/interceptor.cljs:41-64 — Interceptor already has pre-game-db and game-db-after available
- events/game.cljs:263-266 — cast-spell takes [db _] with no args (card comes from :game/selected-card)
- events/game.cljs:399-401 — resolve-top takes [db _] (resolves top of stack)
- events/abilities.cljs:119-122 — activate-mana-ability takes [db [_ object-id mana-color]]
- events/abilities.cljs:257-260 — activate-ability takes [db [_ object-id ability-index]]
- db/schema.cljs:76 — stack-item/targets stores targeting choices as map
- engine/grants.cljs:164-166 — phase-order defines all phases: [:untap :upkeep :draw :main1 :combat :main2 :end :cleanup]

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Enrich describe-event with game-db access ✓

**What it is:** Change describe-event signature to accept pre/post game-dbs. Each case clause queries the appropriate db for card names, targets, and phase info. One-line interceptor change to pass existing variables.

**Investigation:**
- Verified interceptor has both dbs at lines 50-52
- Verified event args for all 8 priority events — most have no useful args
- Confirmed stack items store card names via source object and targets map

**Pros:**
- Centralized: all logic in one file
- Minimal blast radius: 2 files changed (descriptions.cljs + interceptor.cljs)
- No event handler changes needed
- Interceptor already has the data

**Cons:**
- descriptions.cljs gains dependency on db.queries (acceptable for display concern)

**Chosen because:** Minimal changes, centralized logic, no touching of game event handlers

#### 2. Generate descriptions in event handlers ❌

**What it is:** Each event handler computes a rich description string and stores it in app-db. Interceptor reads the pre-computed description.

**Why we looked at this:** Event handlers know exactly what action was taken

**Investigation:**
- Counted 8 priority event handlers across 2 files (game.cljs, abilities.cljs)
- Each handler would need modification to compute and store description

**Pros:**
- Each handler has direct knowledge of what it did

**Cons:**
- Requires modifying all 8 event handlers across 2 files
- Scatters description logic across event files
- Mixes display concerns into game logic

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Violates separation of concerns. Descriptions are a display/history concern, not game logic.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** describe-event needs information not available in game-db snapshots

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Enriching all 8 priority event descriptions with card names, phases, targets
- Fallback to generic descriptions on lookup failure
- Tests for all enriched descriptions

**Out of scope (deferred/never):**
- Changing history entry structure (entry/description remains a string)
- Adding new priority events
- Showing mana costs or mana pool changes in descriptions
- Storm copy detail in cast descriptions

### Open Questions
- Exact target display format: 'targeting opponent' vs 'targeting Player 2' (resolve during implementation)
- For activate-ability with multiple abilities: show ability description text or index? (resolve during implementation — use description text when available)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Detail level for descriptions? | Card name only (clean, scannable) | No mana costs, no pool changes in descriptions |
| Show resolved card name? | Yes, show what was resolved | Need to query pre-game-db stack top |
| Show ability source + info? | Yes, plus disambiguating info and targets | Need ability description/index from event args + card name from game-db |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### Event Arg Analysis
**Question explored:** What data do priority event vectors carry?
**Sources consulted:**
- events/game.cljs registrations for cast-spell, resolve-top, advance-phase, play-land, start-turn
- events/abilities.cljs registrations for activate-mana-ability, activate-ability

**Findings:**
- cast-spell, resolve-top, advance-phase, start-turn: take [db _] — NO useful args
- play-land: takes [db [_ object-id]] — has object-id
- activate-mana-ability: takes [db [_ object-id mana-color]] — has both
- activate-ability: takes [db [_ object-id ability-index]] — has both

**Conclusion:** Must use game-db snapshots for most events, event args only useful for land/ability events

#### Stack Item Data Model
**Question explored:** Where are card names and targets stored?
**Sources consulted:**
- db/schema.cljs:76-78 — stack-item schema
- engine/stack.cljs — stack query functions
- events/selection.cljs:748 — target retrieval pattern

**Findings:**
- Stack items have :stack-item/source (object-id) and :stack-item/targets (map)
- :stack-item/description already exists for stack display (different from history description)
- Card names available via source object → :object/card → :card/name

**Conclusion:** All needed data accessible via existing query patterns

### Open Concerns Raised
- 'What if card lookup fails?' → Fall back to generic description (anti-pattern prevents hard failure)
- 'Target format?' → Defer to implementation, likely 'targeting opponent' for player targets
close_reason: Implemented enriched history descriptions with card names, targets, mana colors. All 1177 tests pass.

title: Bot protocol, basic lands, and goldfish deck
Add interactive mana pool view and allocation subscription
description: Stream 3: View + subs layer for mana allocation. The event layer (costs.cljs) and engine (mana.cljs) are complete. This task adds the visual/interactive layer.
design:
## Goal
Add the visual/interactive layer for mana allocation. The engine (pay-mana-with-allocation) and event layer (build-mana-allocation-selection, allocate-mana-color, reset, cancel, confirm) are complete. This task makes the mana pool view context-sensitive — static normally, clickable during allocation mode.

## Effort Estimate
4-5 hours (subscription: 30min, router: 10min, view: 2-3h, tests: 1h, validation: 15min)

## Context
Completed tasks:
- fizzle-d5ro: pay-mana-with-allocation in engine/mana.cljs
- fizzle-myzg.1: mana-allocation selection type in events/selection/costs.cljs
- fizzle-w6m8: Chain mana allocation into casting/ability flows

The pending-selection during allocation has this shape:
  {:selection/type :mana-allocation
   :selection/generic-remaining N
   :selection/generic-total N
   :selection/allocation {}
   :selection/remaining-pool {color -> amount}
   :selection/original-remaining-pool {color -> amount}
   :selection/colored-cost {color -> amount}
   :selection/auto-confirm? true}

Events to dispatch from the view:
- ::cost-events/allocate-mana-color with color keyword
- ::cost-events/reset-mana-allocation
- ::cost-events/cancel-mana-allocation

## Implementation

### Step 1: Add allocation subscription to subs/game.cljs
Add ::mana-allocation-state subscription that derives from ::pending-selection.
Insert after ::pending-selection (line ~282).

Return nil when no allocation active (no pending-selection, or type != :mana-allocation).
When selection type is :mana-allocation, return the selection directly (no transformation needed — view reads selection keys directly). This avoids creating a redundant wrapper map and keeps the subscription simple.

Pattern: Layer 3 subscription using :<- [::pending-selection]

### Step 2: Update selection router in views/modals.cljs
In selection-modal (line ~605), add :mana-allocation case BEFORE the default :else clause (around line 670).
Return nil — mana pool handles allocation UX, not a modal.

Code:
  (= selection-type :mana-allocation)
  nil

Requires adding [fizzle.events.selection.costs :as cost-events] to the ns require — already present (line 6).

### Step 3: Make mana-pool-view context-sensitive in views/mana_pool.cljs
Add require for [fizzle.events.selection.costs :as cost-events] to ns form.

Modify mana-pool-view to subscribe to ::subs/mana-allocation-state in addition to ::subs/mana-pool.

When allocation NOT active (nil): Render EXACTLY the current static view (unchanged).

When allocation IS active:
a) Header: "ALLOCATE MANA" label (replacing "MANA POOL"), with "N generic remaining" subtext using :selection/generic-remaining
b) Mana color buttons: iterate over :selection/remaining-pool entries.
   IMPORTANT: remaining-pool is derived from (merge-with - pool colored-cost), so colors with 0 remaining ARE included in the map (merge-with preserves all keys from both maps). Colors NOT in the original pool will not appear.
   - For each [color amount] in remaining-pool:
     - If amount > 0: clickable button, cursor-pointer, dispatch [::cost-events/allocate-mana-color color]
     - If amount <= 0: dimmed (opacity-30 cursor-not-allowed), no click handler
     - Show symbol + remaining count using existing mana-symbols map and mana-color-class
     - If allocation for this color > 0, show annotation e.g. "B: 2 (1 used)"
c) Action buttons row:
   - Reset button: dispatches [::cost-events/reset-mana-allocation], enabled when allocation non-empty
   - Cancel button: dispatches [::cost-events/cancel-mana-allocation]
d) Tailwind classes: Use existing patterns from modals.cljs for button styling. Mana buttons: rounded-md px-3 py-2 border-2, with mana-color-class for text color.

storm-count-view is separate and unchanged.

### Step 4: Write subscription tests
Create test/fizzle/subs/game_test.cljs (new file — no existing subscription test file).
Use re-frame.core/subscribe and re-frame.db/app-db for testing.

Tests:
- test-mana-allocation-state-nil-when-no-selection: Set app-db with no :game/pending-selection, subscribe to ::mana-allocation-state, assert nil
- test-mana-allocation-state-nil-for-other-types: Set app-db with pending-selection type :discard, assert nil
- test-mana-allocation-state-returns-selection-for-allocation: Set app-db with full :mana-allocation pending-selection, assert returns the selection map
- test-mana-allocation-state-with-partial-allocation: Set app-db with some allocation already done (generic-remaining < generic-total), verify correct values

### Step 5: Run make validate
Ensure all existing tests pass + new tests pass + lint + format clean.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] ::mana-allocation-state subscription returns nil when no pending-selection
- [ ] ::mana-allocation-state subscription returns nil for non-allocation selection types
- [ ] ::mana-allocation-state subscription returns selection map when type is :mana-allocation
- [ ] Selection router in modals.cljs returns nil for :mana-allocation type (no modal appears)
- [ ] Mana pool renders static (existing behavior) when no allocation active
- [ ] Mana pool renders interactive buttons during allocation mode
- [ ] Each mana color button dispatches ::cost-events/allocate-mana-color with correct color
- [ ] Colors with 0 in remaining-pool are visually dimmed (opacity-30) and not clickable
- [ ] Header shows remaining generic amount
- [ ] Reset button dispatches ::cost-events/reset-mana-allocation
- [ ] Cancel button dispatches ::cost-events/cancel-mana-allocation
- [ ] 4+ subscription tests pass in test/fizzle/subs/game_test.cljs
- [ ] All existing tests pass (1265+ tests)
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO modal/dialog for allocation (epic anti-pattern: breaks flow during storm turns)
- NO confirm button (epic anti-pattern: auto-confirm when allocation complete)
- NO mutable state outside datascript (architecture: all state in pending-selection)
- NO auto-pay shortcut (design: player always manually allocates each mana)
- NO single-color auto-allocate when only one color remains (user explicitly chose no shortcuts)
- NO modifying event handler logic (events complete, this is view-only task)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: All remaining pool colors at 0**
If colored costs consume entire pool, remaining-pool will have all zeroes. All buttons will be dimmed. This CANNOT happen in practice because can-pay? validates before casting, but defensively: render all buttons dimmed. The player cannot proceed and must Cancel.

**Edge Case: Colorless mana in pool**
:colorless is a valid key in mana pools (e.g., from Sol Ring). remaining-pool may contain :colorless. The mana-symbols map already has :colorless -> "C" and mana-color-class handles it. No special-casing needed.

**Edge Case: remaining-pool key ordering**
ClojureScript maps have no guaranteed order. For consistent display, sort remaining-pool keys before iterating. Use the same order as mana-symbols: [:white :blue :black :red :green :colorless].

**Edge Case: Reset when allocation is empty**
Reset button should be disabled (or no-op) when :selection/allocation is empty {}. Dispatching reset with empty allocation is harmless (restores original pool which is already active) but visually misleading. Disable when (empty? allocation).

**View-only task: No engine/event changes**
All event handlers (allocate-mana-color-impl, reset-mana-allocation-impl, cancel) are complete and tested. This task ONLY adds visual rendering. Do not modify any files in events/ or engine/.

**Reference patterns:**
- X mana cost modal (modals.cljs:477-515) for custom event dispatching pattern
- Existing mana-pool-view (mana_pool.cljs:24-34) for base component structure

title: Register custom :db effect handler with SBA and bot checks
description: Create the structural chokepoint: a custom re-frame :db effect handler that runs SBAs and bot checks whenever game-db changes. This replaces both interceptors.
design:
## Goal
Register a custom :db effect handler in core.cljs that runs check-and-execute-sbas and bot-should-act? checks whenever game-db changes. This is the foundation — all other tasks depend on it.

NOTE: This task ONLY creates the new handler and verifies it works alongside the existing interceptors. It does NOT remove the interceptors or inline SBA calls — those are separate tasks. Both mechanisms run in parallel during this task, which is safe (SBAs are idempotent).

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing patterns (DO FIRST)
- core.cljs:140-144 — current init registering interceptors
- re-frame default :db effect handler: `(fn [value] (if-not (identical? @app-db value) (reset! app-db value)))` in re-frame/fx.cljc
- engine/state_based.cljs:66-81 — check-and-execute-sbas (loops until no more SBAs)
- bots/interceptor.cljs:26-38 — bot-should-act? (checks priority holder is bot)
- bots/interceptor.cljs:148-174 — how bot interceptor queues ::bot-decide (adds to :fx)

### 2. Create events/db_effect.cljs (new file)
Dedicated module for the custom :db effect handler. Keeps core.cljs clean.

```clojure
(ns fizzle.events.db-effect
  (:require
    [fizzle.bots.interceptor :as bot-interceptor]
    [fizzle.engine.state-based :as sba]
    [re-frame.core :as rf]
    [re-frame.db :as rf-db]))

(defn game-db-effect-handler
  "Custom :db effect handler. Runs SBAs and bot checks on every game-db mutation.
   Replaces SBA and bot interceptors with a single structural chokepoint."
  [new-app-db]
  (let [old-game-db (:game/db @rf-db/app-db)
        new-game-db (:game/db new-app-db)]
    (if (or (nil? new-game-db)
            (identical? new-game-db old-game-db))
      ;; No game-db change — pass through unchanged
      (when-not (identical? @rf-db/app-db new-app-db)
        (reset! rf-db/app-db new-app-db))
      ;; Game-db changed — run SBA pipeline, then check bot
      (let [sba-db (sba/check-and-execute-sbas new-game-db)
            final-app-db (assoc new-app-db :game/db sba-db)]
        (reset! rf-db/app-db final-app-db)
        ;; Queue bot action if bot should act (after SBAs applied)
        ;; Guards: no pending selection, game not over, bot holds priority
        (when (and (not (:game/pending-selection final-app-db))
                   (bot-interceptor/bot-should-act? sba-db))
          (rf/dispatch [::bot-interceptor/bot-decide]))))))

(defn register!
  "Register the custom :db effect handler. Call once during init."
  []
  (rf/reg-fx :db game-db-effect-handler))
```

### 3. Register in core.cljs init
Add after existing interceptor registrations (keep interceptors for now — parallel operation):
```clojure
(defn init []
  (history-interceptor/register!)
  (bot-interceptor/register!)      ;; keep for now — removed in later task
  (sba-interceptor/register!)      ;; keep for now — removed in later task
  (db-effect/register!)            ;; NEW — :db effect handler
  ...)
```

### 4. Verify bot-should-act? handles game-over
Check bots/interceptor.cljs:26-38 — bot-should-act? checks if priority holder is a bot. When game is over, priority may still be assigned. Verify that ::bot-decide handler (line 193-237) returns a pass action when game is over (loss condition set). If not, add guard.

### 5. Tests (TDD — write failing tests first)
Create src/test/fizzle/events/db_effect_test.cljs:

```clojure
;; Test 1: SBA fires through :db effect handler
;; Setup: game-db with opponent at 0 life, no loss condition set
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler with this app-db
;; Assert: @rf-db/app-db has :game/loss-condition :life-zero
;; Bug caught: SBAs not running on game-db change

;; Test 2: identical game-db skips SBAs
;; Setup: set rf-db/app-db to app-db-with-game
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler with SAME app-db (identical game-db)
;; Assert: check-and-execute-sbas was NOT called (use with-redefs to track)
;; Bug caught: unnecessary SBA runs on non-game events

;; Test 3: nil game-db skips SBAs
;; Setup: app-db without :game/db key (setup screen)
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler
;; Assert: no crash, app-db updated normally
;; Bug caught: NPE on setup/calculator screens

;; Test 4: bot queued after SBA
;; Setup: game-db where bot holds priority, no pending selection
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler
;; Assert: rf/dispatch called with [::bot-interceptor/bot-decide]
;; Bug caught: bot not responding after game-state change

;; Test 5: pending selection blocks bot
;; Setup: game-db where bot holds priority + pending selection exists
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler
;; Assert: rf/dispatch NOT called
;; Bug caught: bot acting during player's selection

;; Test 6: SBA result used for bot check (not pre-SBA state)
;; Setup: game-db where SBA would set loss condition (opponent at 0)
;;         AND bot holds priority
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler
;; Assert: bot-should-act? receives sba-db (with loss condition), not original
;; Bug caught: bot acting on stale pre-SBA state

;; Test 7: first init event (empty old app-db)
;; Setup: rf-db/app-db is {} (fresh init)
;; Action: call game-db-effect-handler with {:game/db some-db}
;; Assert: SBAs run on the new game-db, no crash
;; Bug caught: NPE when old-game-db is nil
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] game-db-effect-handler function exists in events/db_effect.cljs
- [ ] register! called in core.cljs init
- [ ] SBAs fire when game-db changes (test: opponent at 0 life → loss condition set)
- [ ] SBAs skip when game-db unchanged (test: identical? returns true → no SBA call)
- [ ] SBAs skip when game-db is nil (test: setup screen → no crash)
- [ ] Bot queued after SBA when bot holds priority (test: dispatch called)
- [ ] Bot NOT queued when pending selection exists (test: dispatch not called)
- [ ] Bot check uses post-SBA game-db (test: loss condition visible to bot)
- [ ] First init event works (empty old app-db, no crash)
- [ ] All existing tests pass — interceptors still active in parallel (make validate)

## Anti-Patterns
- ❌ DO NOT use dispatch-sync for bot-decide — causes re-entry in the :db effect handler
- ❌ DO NOT add a trigger event set — the whole point is eliminating whitelists
- ❌ DO NOT check game-db equality with = — use identical? for O(1) comparison
- ❌ DO NOT remove interceptors in this task — parallel operation first, removal in later task
- ❌ DO NOT skip the when-not identical? guard on the pass-through path — re-frame's default handler has this guard for Reagent/React efficiency

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: First init dispatch**
On first `dispatch-sync [::init-setup]`, the old app-db is `{}`. `(:game/db {})` returns nil. The `(nil? new-game-db)` check handles this — but `old-game-db` is also nil, and `(identical? nil nil)` is true. So the handler takes the pass-through path. Correct behavior: no SBAs on init (no game yet).

**Edge Case: Setup/calculator screens**
When on setup or calculator screens, :game/db may be nil. The nil guard handles this. But verify that navigating from game → setup → game doesn't leave stale game-db.

**Edge Case: History undo interaction**
History interceptor runs in :after BEFORE do-fx :after. History captures pre-SBA state. When user undoes, restored game-db goes through :db effect handler, SBAs re-run on restored state. This is correct — SBAs are idempotent and the restored state is valid.

**Edge Case: Double SBA execution**
During this task, BOTH the :db effect handler AND the interceptor run SBAs. And reduce-effects has inline SBA calls. This means SBAs run 2-3x per event. This is safe because:
1. check-and-execute-sbas loops until no more SBAs fire
2. The `:life-zero` check has a guard: `(if (:game/loss-condition game) [])`
3. Second/third runs are no-ops (identical? on the result)
Performance impact is negligible for this transitional state.

**Integration: bot-should-act? accessibility**
bot-should-act? is currently a public function in bots/interceptor.cljs. It needs to stay public (or be moved) for the :db effect handler to call it. Do NOT make it private in this task.

**Re-entrant dispatch safety**
The :db effect handler calls `rf/dispatch` (not `dispatch-sync`). `rf/dispatch` pushes to the event queue — it does NOT execute inline. The dispatched ::bot-decide runs AFTER the current event's interceptor chain completes. Verified: re-frame's router processes events sequentially from a queue.

## Effort Estimate
~4 hours
