title: Implement calculator localStorage persistence
description: Persist calculator query definitions in localStorage alongside deck configuration. Save on query change, restore on deck load.
design:
## Goal
Persist calculator query definitions in localStorage alongside deck configuration. Save on query change, restore on deck load.

## Effort Estimate
3-4 hours

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing patterns
- db/storage.cljs — existing localStorage API (pr-str/read-string, try/catch pattern)
- events/setup.cljs — preset save/load (how deck config is stored, lines 163-178)

### 2. Approach: Extend preset system

The epic says: \"Extend existing localStorage deck config to include :calculator/queries\"

Option A: Add :calculator/queries to the preset config map that's already saved/loaded.
Option B: Save calculator queries as a separate localStorage key.

Prefer Option B (separate key) because:
- Presets have a specific structure and purpose (deck + bot config)
- Calculator queries are independent of deck choice (you might want the same queries across decks)
- Simpler: no need to modify preset save/load code paths

### 3. Implementation checklist

**storage.cljs additions:**
- [ ] save-calculator-queries! — persist queries vector to localStorage key \"fizzle-calculator-queries\"
- [ ] load-calculator-queries — load queries vector from localStorage, return [] on missing/corrupt

**events/calculator.cljs additions:**
- [ ] Add persistence interceptor or save after every mutation event
  - Simplest: add an fx interceptor that saves after every calculator event
  - OR: explicitly call save in each handler (worse: repetitive)
  - BEST: Use re-frame's reg-event-fx with a cofx that reads/writes localStorage
  - SIMPLEST: Add an \"after\" interceptor that saves current queries to localStorage

**Approach: after-interceptor pattern**
```clojure
;; In events/calculator.cljs:
(def save-queries-interceptor
  (rf/after
    (fn [db]
      (storage/save-calculator-queries! (:calculator/queries db)))))
```

Then add this interceptor to all mutation events (all except ::init-calculator and ::toggle-calculator):
- ::add-query, ::remove-query, ::set-query-label
- ::add-step, ::remove-step, ::set-step-draw-count
- ::add-target-group, ::remove-target-group
- ::toggle-target-card, ::set-target-min-count

**Load on init:**
Modify ::init-calculator handler to:
1. Check if :calculator/queries already in app-db (idempotent, existing behavior)
2. If absent, try to load from localStorage first
3. If localStorage has data, use it. Otherwise use default empty state.
4. Set :calculator/next-id to max(existing IDs) + 1 to avoid ID collisions

**ID collision fix:**
When restoring queries from localStorage, compute the max ID already in use:
```clojure
(defn- max-id-in-queries [queries]
  (reduce max 0
    (concat
      (map :query/id queries)
      (mapcat (fn [q] (map :step/id (:query/steps q))) queries)
      (mapcat (fn [q]
                (mapcat (fn [s] (map :target/id (:step/targets s)))
                        (:query/steps q)))
              queries))))
```
Set :calculator/next-id to (inc (max-id-in-queries loaded-queries)).

### 4. Test scenarios

Test file: src/test/fizzle/events/calculator_test.cljs (extend existing file)
OR: src/test/fizzle/db/storage_test.cljs if one exists

**New tests (minimum 5 assertions):**
1. save-calculator-queries! + load-calculator-queries round-trip preserves data
2. load-calculator-queries returns [] when localStorage empty
3. init-calculator-handler restores from loaded queries when app-db empty
4. init-calculator sets next-id based on max ID in loaded queries
5. init-calculator is still idempotent when queries already in app-db

### 5. Edge Cases
- Corrupt localStorage data → return [] (existing try/catch pattern)
- Empty localStorage → return []
- Queries with sets (#{:dark-ritual}) must serialize/deserialize correctly in EDN
- ID collision after restore: next-id = max(all IDs in loaded queries) + 1

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Calculator queries persist across page refresh
- [ ] Loading corrupt/missing data doesn't crash
- [ ] New queries get IDs that don't collide with restored ones
- [ ] Saving/loading preserves card sets in target groups
- [ ] init-calculator remains idempotent when state already present
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO modifying preset save/load code (keep calculator persistence separate)
- ❌ NO crashing on corrupt localStorage
- ❌ NO losing card sets during serialization (EDN handles sets natively)
close_reason: Closed

title: Task 3: Create interactive UI components for goldfishing
design:
## Goal
Create Reagent view components that enable interactive goldfishing: clickable hand cards, Cast/Resolve buttons, mana pool display, storm count, and stack view. This completes the Phase 1 milestone.

## Context
Completed Task 1 (re-frame infrastructure) and Task 2 (events/subscriptions wrapping engine). Events and subscriptions are fully wired. Now need components that subscribe to state and dispatch events.

## Implementation

### 1. Update core.cljs with interactive app component

Replace the text-only display with a full interactive layout. All views can live in core.cljs for now (3 cards, simple UI) - extract to separate files only if it becomes unwieldy.

Component structure:
- Hand: shows each card as a clickable div with card name, highlights selected
- Mana pool: shows each color with non-zero amount
- Storm count: shows current storm count
- Stack: shows items on stack (triggers and spells) with names
- Controls: Cast button (enabled when card selected and castable), Resolve button (enabled when stack non-empty)

Key events to dispatch:
- `::events/select-card` on card click (pass object-id)
- `::events/cast-spell` on Cast button click
- `::events/resolve-top` on Resolve button click

Subscriptions to consume:
- `::subs/hand` - cards in hand
- `::subs/mana-pool` - mana by color
- `::subs/storm-count` - storm count
- `::subs/stack` - stack items
- `::subs/selected-card` - currently selected card
- `::subs/can-cast?` - whether selected card is castable

### 2. Styling
Inline styles only (anti-pattern forbids CSS frameworks).
- Cards: bordered divs, highlighted border when selected
- Mana: colored text or symbols for each mana color
- Buttons: basic button styling, disabled state visible

### 3. Verify in browser
- Run `make dev` and open localhost:8080
- Verify: 3 cards in hand, 2 black mana, 0 storm
- Click Dark Ritual -> Cast -> mana becomes BBB+1=4B (started with 2, -1 cost, +3 from effect... wait: start 2B, cast DR costs 1B -> 1B remaining, resolve DR adds 3B -> 4B)
- Actually: casting puts on stack (pays mana), resolving executes effects
- Flow: Select DR -> Cast (pays 1B, moves to stack, storm=1) -> Resolve (adds 3B, moves to graveyard) -> mana pool should show 4B

### 4. Run make validate

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Browser loads at localhost:8080 without console errors
- [ ] Hand shows 3 clickable cards with names (Dark Ritual, Brain Freeze, Cabal Ritual)
- [ ] Clicking a card highlights it and shows it as selected
- [ ] Cast button works when card selected and castable
- [ ] Resolve button works when stack has items
- [ ] Mana pool updates correctly after casting and resolving
- [ ] Storm count increments on each cast
- [ ] Stack shows triggers and spells
- [ ] Can complete full combo: DR -> CR -> BF with storm copies
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO game logic in components (subscribe and dispatch only)
- NO direct atom manipulation
- NO CSS frameworks (inline styles only)
- NO new engine code
close_reason: Interactive UI components implemented in core.cljs. Hand with clickable cards, mana pool display, storm count, stack view, and Cast/Resolve buttons. All lint/format/tests pass.

title: Task 1: Add auto-confirm routing to ::toggle-selection for single-select types
design:
## Goal
When a single-select selection reaches completion, automatically dispatch the appropriate confirm event. No separate Confirm click needed. Covers tutor (select-count=1), cast-time-targeting (object and player), player-target, and ability-targeting (object and player) selections.

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours (includes TDD cycle)

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing code (READ-ONLY)

Four separate toggle/select handlers exist — each needs auto-confirm logic:

| Handler | File:Line | Field | Confirm Event | Confirm Interface |
|---------|-----------|-------|---------------|-------------------|
| `::toggle-selection` | selection.cljs:1017 | `:selection/selected` (set) | `::confirm-tutor-selection` | `(fn [db _])` — reads from db |
| `::select-player-target` | selection.cljs:1341 | `:selection/selected-target` (single) | Routes by type (see below) | Varies |
| `::select-cast-time-object-target` | selection.cljs:1528 | `:selection/selected-target` (single) | `::confirm-cast-time-target` | `(fn [db [_ target-id]])` — takes arg |
| `::select-ability-object-target` | abilities.cljs:281 | `:selection/selected-target` (single) | `::confirm-ability-target` | `(fn [db [_ target-id]])` — takes arg |

**`::select-player-target` routing** — shared by three selection types:
| `:selection/type` | Confirm Event | Interface |
|---------------------|---------------|-----------|
| `:player-target` | `::confirm-player-target-selection` | `(fn [db _])` — reads from db |
| `:cast-time-targeting` | `::confirm-cast-time-target` | `(fn [db [_ target-id]])` — takes arg |
| `:ability-targeting` | `::confirm-ability-target` | `(fn [db [_ target-id]])` — takes arg |

**Key detail**: `confirm-ability-target` at abilities.cljs:150 is already extracted as a standalone function. The other confirm handlers need similar extraction.

Reference code:
- selection.cljs:1017-1044 — `::toggle-selection` full handler
- selection.cljs:1202-1243 — `::confirm-tutor-selection` full handler
- selection.cljs:1340-1343 — `::select-player-target` handler
- selection.cljs:1346-1400 — `::confirm-player-target-selection` handler
- selection.cljs:1515-1524 — `::confirm-cast-time-target` handler
- selection.cljs:1527-1541 — `::select-cast-time-object-target` handler
- abilities.cljs:150-175 — `confirm-ability-target` standalone function (already extracted)
- abilities.cljs:268-277 — `::confirm-ability-target` reg-event-db wrapper
- abilities.cljs:280-293 — `::select-ability-object-target` handler
- modals.cljs:629-631 — cast-time player target routes to confirm-cast-time-target
- modals.cljs:643-644 — ability player target routes to confirm-ability-target
- modals.cljs:655-657 — spell-effect player target routes to confirm-player-target-selection

### 2. Write failing tests first (TDD RED)

**Specific test scenarios:**

a) **test-single-select-tutor-auto-confirms**: Set up game with single-select tutor pending (e.g., Merchant Scroll). Dispatch `::toggle-selection` with a valid library card. Assert: `:game/pending-selection` is nil, card is in hand, library is shuffled.
   - Bug caught: Auto-confirm doesn't fire → pending-selection persists, card stays in library.

b) **test-multi-select-tutor-does-not-auto-confirm**: Set up Intuition (select-count=3). Dispatch `::toggle-selection` with first card. Assert: `:game/pending-selection` still present, selected has 1 card.
   - Bug caught: Auto-confirm firing on multi-select breaks Intuition.

c) **test-deselect-does-not-auto-confirm**: Set up single-select tutor, select a card, then toggle same card (deselect). Assert: pending-selection still present, selected is empty.
   - Bug caught: Auto-confirm on deselect prevents changing mind.

d) **test-cast-time-object-target-auto-confirms**: Set up cast-time targeting (e.g., Recoup). Dispatch `::select-cast-time-object-target` with valid target. Assert: pending-selection is nil, spell on stack with target set.
   - Bug caught: Cast-time object targeting still requires 2 clicks.

e) **test-cast-time-object-deselect-does-not-auto-confirm**: Select target, then click same target (deselects to nil). Assert: pending-selection persists.
   - Bug caught: Deselect-to-nil triggers confirm with nil target.

f) **test-player-target-auto-confirms**: Set up spell-effect player target. Dispatch `::select-player-target` with :opponent. Assert: pending-selection is nil, effect executed.
   - Bug caught: Player target still requires 2 clicks.

g) **test-cast-time-player-target-auto-confirms**: Set up cast-time targeting that targets a player. Dispatch `::select-player-target`. Assert: pending-selection cleared, correct confirm event used (::confirm-cast-time-target, NOT ::confirm-player-target-selection).
   - Bug caught: Wrong confirm event dispatched for cast-time player targeting.

h) **test-ability-object-target-auto-confirms**: Set up ability targeting (e.g., Seal of Cleansing targeting artifact). Dispatch `::select-ability-object-target` with valid target. Assert: pending-selection is nil, ability activated.
   - Bug caught: Ability object targeting still requires 2 clicks.

i) **test-ability-player-target-auto-confirms**: Set up ability targeting a player (e.g., Cephalid Coliseum threshold). Dispatch `::select-player-target`. Assert: pending-selection cleared, correct confirm event used (::confirm-ability-target).
   - Bug caught: Wrong confirm event for ability player targeting.

j) **test-find-nothing-still-works**: Set up single-select tutor. Dispatch `::confirm-tutor-selection` directly with empty selection. Assert: pending-selection cleared, no card moved, library shuffled.
   - Bug caught: Find Nothing path broken by auto-confirm changes.

### 3. Implementation checklist

- [ ] Extract `confirm-tutor-selection-impl` function from `::confirm-tutor-selection` (selection.cljs:1202). Takes `db` (app-db), returns `db`. The reg-event-db handler becomes a thin wrapper calling this function.

- [ ] Extract `confirm-player-target-impl` function from `::confirm-player-target-selection` (selection.cljs:1346). Takes `db`, returns `db`.

- [ ] Extract `confirm-cast-time-target-impl` function from `::confirm-cast-time-target` (selection.cljs:1515). Takes `db` and `target-id`, returns `db`. (Note: `confirm-ability-target` at abilities.cljs:150 is already extracted.)

- [ ] Modify `::toggle-selection` (selection.cljs:1017): After computing `new-selected`, add auto-confirm check:
  ```clojure
  ;; After line 1043 (assoc-in new-selected)
  ;; Check: single-select tutor completed?
  (if (and (= selection-type :tutor) (= max-count 1) (= (count new-selected) 1))
    (confirm-tutor-selection-impl (assoc-in db [...] new-selected))
    (assoc-in db [...] new-selected))
  ```

- [ ] Modify `::select-player-target` (selection.cljs:1341): After setting selected-target, route auto-confirm by `:selection/type`:
  ```clojure
  (let [db-with-target (assoc-in db [...] target-player-id)
        selection-type (:selection/type (get db :game/pending-selection))]
    (case selection-type
      :player-target (confirm-player-target-impl db-with-target)
      :cast-time-targeting (confirm-cast-time-target-impl db-with-target target-player-id)
      :ability-targeting (... call ability confirm with target-player-id)
      db-with-target))  ;; fallback: no auto-confirm
  ```

- [ ] Modify `::select-cast-time-object-target` (selection.cljs:1528): After computing new-selection, if non-nil (not deselect), call `confirm-cast-time-target-impl` with object-id.

- [ ] Modify `::select-ability-object-target` (abilities.cljs:281): After computing new-selection, if non-nil, call `confirm-ability-target` (already extracted at L150) with appropriate args. Note: this handler is in abilities.cljs, so it may need to require selection helpers or the confirm logic lives in abilities.cljs already.

- [ ] For ability auto-confirm from `::select-player-target`: The player-target handler is in selection.cljs but ability confirm is in abilities.cljs. To avoid circular dependency, dispatch via `rf/dispatch-sync` (synchronous dispatch) for the ability confirm case, OR restructure to call the confirm function directly (abilities.cljs:150 is already a standalone fn — selection.cljs would need to require abilities namespace, which is fine since game.cljs already requires it).

### 4. Key Considerations

**Dispatch vs direct function call (RESOLVED):**
Using `rf/dispatch` would be async (next tick) causing modal flicker. Extract confirm logic into plain functions called synchronously within the toggle handler. `confirm-ability-target` is already extracted at abilities.cljs:150.

**Player-target routing by selection type:**
`::select-player-target` is shared between 3 selection types. Must check `:selection/type` to dispatch to correct confirm function.

**Cross-namespace dependency for ability confirm:**
`::select-player-target` is in selection.cljs but `confirm-ability-target` is in abilities.cljs. Current dep DAG: game -> selection, game -> abilities. Adding selection -> abilities would be new but not circular. Alternatively, use `rf/dispatch-sync` for the ability case.

**Confirm handler interface differences:**
- tutor and player-target confirm: read target from db state, no args needed
- cast-time and ability confirm: take target-id as argument
Auto-confirm must pass target-id for cast-time and ability cases.

**Edge Case: Empty library (0 candidates):**
Modal shows only Find Nothing button. No cards to click, so auto-confirm never fires. Naturally handled.

**Edge Case: Tutor with pile-choice (Intuition):**
Intuition has select-count=3, so max-count=3, auto-confirm condition (max-count=1) is false. Unaffected.

**Edge Case: Deselect to nil for object targets:**
`::select-cast-time-object-target` and `::select-ability-object-target` set selected-target to nil on deselect. Auto-confirm must check `(some? new-selection)` before firing.

## Anti-patterns
- ❌ NO removing Find Nothing button (fail-to-find is MTG rules-correct)
- ❌ NO auto-confirm on multi-select types (max-count > 1)
- ❌ NO auto-confirm when deselecting (count drops to 0 or selected-target becomes nil)
- ❌ NO rf/dispatch for auto-confirm (causes modal flicker — use direct function call or rf/dispatch-sync)
- ❌ NO modifying modals.cljs (behavior change is in event handlers only)
- ❌ NO auto-confirm on pile-choice, graveyard-return, or peek-and-select
- ❌ NO circular namespace dependencies (if selection needs ability confirm, check dep DAG is safe)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Single-select tutor (select-count=1) auto-confirms on card click — no pending-selection after
- [ ] Multi-select tutor (select-count>1) does NOT auto-confirm after first card click
- [ ] Deselecting a card does NOT trigger auto-confirm
- [ ] Cast-time object targeting auto-confirms on target click
- [ ] Cast-time object deselecting does NOT auto-confirm
- [ ] Cast-time player targeting auto-confirms with correct confirm event
- [ ] Spell-effect player target auto-confirms on player click
- [ ] Ability object targeting auto-confirms on target click
- [ ] Ability player targeting auto-confirms with correct confirm event
- [ ] Find Nothing still works for single-select tutors
- [ ] All existing tests pass (no regressions)
- [ ] `make validate` passes (lint + format + tests)
close_reason: Obsolete: scope expanded to full selection system refactoring

```clojure

(ns fizzle.cards.green.xantid-swarm

   Xantid Swarm: G - 0/1 Creature — Insect
```

title: Epic: Move trigger registry from atom into Datascript
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- ALL triggers (permanent, zone-change, game-rule) are Datascript entities, not atom state
- Trigger entities are components of their source object via :object/triggers with :db/isComponent true
- zones.cljs is pure — zero trigger-related side effects in move-to-zone
- Trigger dispatch queries Datascript, not an atom
- Filter-based activation: trigger activation determined by filter matching against event data, not zone-aware queries
- Game-rule triggers (draw-step, untap-step) use :trigger/always-active? true
- Token trigger cleanup is automatic via Datascript component cascade retraction
- Trigger ordering: auto-order for now, but dispatch design MUST support future player-chosen ordering (dispatch returns matched trigger list before creating stack-items)
- History/fork correctness: trigger state captured in DB snapshots automatically

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] trigger_registry.cljs atom (defonce registry (atom {})) is deleted
- [ ] No calls to register-trigger!, unregister-by-source!, or clear-registry! anywhere in codebase
- [ ] zones.cljs move-to-zone has no side effects — pure (db, args) -> db
- [ ] Trigger entities in Datascript with proper schema attributes
- [ ] :object/triggers component ref with :db/isComponent true
- [ ] Game-rule triggers (draw-step, untap-step) are Datascript entities with :trigger/always-active? true
- [ ] dispatch-event is a pure function of (db, event) -> db
- [ ] Filter matching handles :self resolution, :exclude-self, zone checks, exact match
- [ ] All 10 test files no longer need clear-registry! in fixtures
- [ ] All existing tests pass (no regressions)
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO mutable state outside Datascript for triggers (reason: the entire point is eliminating the atom — any atom/var/volatile for trigger state defeats the purpose)
- NO trigger side effects in zones.cljs (reason: zones must be pure functions for history/fork correctness)
- NO special-casing game-rule triggers outside Datascript (reason: unified model — all triggers are DB entities)
- NO zone-aware activation queries as primary dispatch mechanism (reason: breaks for zone-change triggers like Gaeas Blessing — use filter-based activation instead)
- NO breaking the existing trigger dispatch contract (reason: City of Brass and City of Traitors must work identically before and after migration)
- NO building trigger ordering UI (reason: deferred — auto-order for now, but dispatch must return trigger list to support future ordering)

## Approach
Replace the atom-based trigger registry with Datascript trigger entities using filter-based activation. Triggers are Datascript entities linked to source objects as components (:db/isComponent true). Three trigger categories share one model:

1. Permanent triggers (City of Brass, future token Prowess): Created when permanent enters battlefield via tx-data included in zone-change transaction. Filter handles activation (e.g., source-zone check, :self matching).

2. Zone-change triggers (future Gaeas Blessing): Created at game init. Filter matches event from-zone/to-zone. No timing paradox because filter checks event data, not current card zone.

3. Game-rule triggers (draw-step, untap-step): Created at game init with :trigger/always-active? true. No source object.

API shifts from side effects to transaction data: functions return tx-data vectors that callers batch into existing d/transact! calls. dispatch-event becomes a pure function of (db, event) -> db.

## Architecture

### Schema additions (db/schema.cljs)
:trigger/event-type, :trigger/source (ref), :trigger/controller (ref), :trigger/filter, :trigger/effects, :trigger/description, :trigger/uses-stack?, :trigger/always-active?, :trigger/type
:object/triggers (ref, many, isComponent true)

### Module changes
- engine/trigger_registry.cljs -> pure query/tx functions (no atom)
- engine/trigger_dispatch.cljs -> takes db arg, queries Datascript
- engine/zones.cljs -> remove unregister-by-source! call
- events/game.cljs -> replace register-card-triggers! with tx-data in transactions
- engine/turn_based.cljs -> replace atom registration with tx-data at init
- 10 test files -> remove clear-registry! fixtures

### Dispatch flow
Event fires -> query triggers by event-type (Datalog) -> filter matching in code (:self, zones, exact match) -> split immediate/stacked -> execute immediate -> create stack-items for stacked (auto-ordered, design supports future player ordering)

## Parallelism Map

### Independent Work Streams
1. Schema + query functions (db/schema.cljs, engine/trigger_registry.cljs)
   - Add schema, implement pure query/tx API, unit tests
   - Estimated complexity: medium
2. Migration: creation sites (events/game.cljs, engine/turn_based.cljs)
   - Replace atom registration with Datascript tx-data
   - Depends on Stream 1
   - Estimated complexity: medium
3. Migration: dispatch (engine/trigger_dispatch.cljs)
   - Query Datascript instead of atom
   - Depends on Stream 2
   - Estimated complexity: medium
4. Cleanup: zones + tests (engine/zones.cljs, 10 test files)
   - Remove side effects from zones, remove clear-registry! from tests
   - Depends on Stream 3
   - Estimated complexity: low

### Stream Dependencies
- Stream 2 depends on Stream 1 (schema must exist before creating entities)
- Stream 3 depends on Stream 2 (entities must be in DB before querying)
- Stream 4 depends on Stream 3 (old code removed after new code works)
- ALL streams are sequential

### Suggested Waves
- Wave 1: Stream 1 (schema + API foundation)
- Wave 2: Stream 2 (migrate creation)
- Wave 3: Stream 3 (migrate dispatch)
- Wave 4: Stream 4 (cleanup)

### Parallelism Assessment
- Independent streams: 0 (everything sequential)
- Recommendation: solo execution
- Rationale: Tight coupling between schema, entity creation, query, and cleanup — each step validates the previous

## Design Rationale

### Problem
The trigger registry holds a defonce atom outside the immutable Datascript database. This breaks history (rewind does not rewind triggers), fork (different timelines share one registry), and purity (zones.cljs has side effects). It is the single biggest architectural violation in the codebase.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- engine/trigger_registry.cljs — 88-line atom registry with register/unregister/get/clear
- engine/trigger_dispatch.cljs — dispatch-event queries atom, creates stack-items. Two paths: immediate (uses-stack? false) and stacked (uses-stack? true)
- engine/zones.cljs:62 — move-to-zone calls unregister-by-source! as side effect (impurity)
- events/game.cljs:718 — register-card-triggers! called on battlefield entry
- engine/turn_based.cljs:25,35 — game-rule triggers registered at init
- Only 2 cards have triggers: City of Brass (becomes-tapped) and City of Traitors (land-entered)
- 10 test files use clear-registry! in fixtures

**Edge cases explored:**
- Gaeas Blessing: triggers from library on zone-change to graveyard. Cannot use zone-aware activation (card already moved when event fires). Filter-based activation with event from-zone/to-zone solves this.
- Cycling (Decree of Justice, Lightning Rift): triggers from hand (self) or battlefield (permanent). Filter-based activation handles both.
- Tokens with triggers (Monastery Mentor monks with Prowess): tokens created mid-game, not at init. Need trigger creation on battlefield entry. Component cascade handles cleanup.

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Datascript entities with filter-based activation (CHOSEN)
Triggers as Datascript entities, linked to source objects as components. Activation determined by filter matching against event data. No registration/unregistration concept for permanent triggers that stay in DB.

Pros: Full immutability, history/fork correct, zones pure, token cleanup automatic, unified model, extensible
Cons: Schema additions (~10 new attributes), trigger creation at battlefield entry still needed

Chosen because: Eliminates the architectural violation completely. Filter-based activation handles all edge cases.

#### 2. Keep atom, extend for non-battlefield triggers (REJECTED)
Extend existing atom registry for library/hand triggers. Add snapshot/restore for history.

REJECTED BECAUSE: Does not fix the fundamental problem — mutable state outside DB. Snapshot is a band-aid.
DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS: Datascript query performance becomes a measured bottleneck (extremely unlikely with <10 triggers per game).

#### 3. Query card data directly, no trigger entities (REJECTED)
No new entities. Query :card/triggers EDN directly off battlefield objects.

REJECTED BECAUSE: Fails on token triggers (no card def to query) and game-rule triggers (no card at all). Every edge case requires a workaround.
DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS: We abandon token support entirely (not happening).

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Schema additions for trigger entities
- Pure query/tx API replacing atom
- Migration of all registration, dispatch, and cleanup sites
- Removal of atom and clear-registry! calls
- All existing tests passing

**Out of scope (deferred):**
- Trigger ordering UI (auto-order for now, dispatch design supports future ordering)
- Gaeas Blessing card implementation (architecture supports it, card deferred)
- Cycling trigger implementation (architecture supports it, cards deferred)
- Token trigger creation (architecture supports it, no token-creating cards exist yet)

### Open Questions
- Should trigger_registry.cljs be renamed (e.g., trigger_db.cljs) or keep the name with new implementation?
- Should filter matching remain in trigger_dispatch.cljs or move into the registry module?

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Game-rule triggers model? | Datascript entities | All triggers unified, no special cases |
| Zone cleanup approach? | Fully pure zones.cljs | Trigger cleanup via Datascript transactions, not side effects |
| Gaeas Blessing/cycling? | Filter-based activation | Event data carries from/to zones, no zone-aware activation |
| Token trigger cleanup? | Component cascade | :db/isComponent true auto-retracts when token removed |
| Trigger ordering? | Auto-order, design for future | Dispatch returns matched list before stack-item creation |

### Dead-End Paths

#### Simple Zone-Aware Queries
Why explored: Elegant model — zone IS activation state, no lifecycle needed
Investigation: Works for battlefield triggers. Fails for zone-change triggers (timing paradox) and cycling (hand triggers).
Why abandoned: Only works for battlefield triggers. Non-battlefield triggers require workarounds.

### Open Concerns Raised
- Trigger ordering (multiple simultaneous triggers) -> Auto-order now, dispatch supports future player choice
- Performance of Datalog queries vs atom deref -> Negligible with <10 triggers per game
- Schema migration complexity -> Manageable, ~10 new attributes, backward compatible
close_reason: All 4 tasks complete. Atom registry eliminated. All triggers are Datascript entities. zones.cljs pure. dispatch-event pure. make validate passes: 1248 tests, 0 failures. Review approved.

title: Data-driven selection system: pattern-based builders, executors, and views
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- Selection builders dispatch via Clojure multimethod hierarchy where effect types derive from interaction patterns (:zone-pick, :accumulator, :reorder)
- Three generic builders handle common interactive effects by reading standard effect fields
- Three generic executors handle common selection results by reading builder-set fields on selection state
- Three generic view components dispatch on :selection/pattern field, replacing copy-pasted modal code
- Custom builders/executors/views override via multimethod precedence for genuinely unique interactions (storm-split, chain-bounce, mana-allocation)
- Most build-chain-selection defmethods eliminated: multi-step reorder becomes internal to reorder builder, sequential interactive effects use remaining-effects mechanism
- Views use only generic events (::toggle-selection, ::confirm-selection) — no type-specific event dispatches in generic components
- View monolith (modals.cljs) split into views/selection/ directory organized by pattern

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] Adding a new zone-pick effect requires only a derive declaration + effect data on the card (zero builder/executor/view code)
- [ ] Adding a new accumulator effect requires only a derive declaration + effect data
- [ ] Adding a new reorder effect requires only a derive declaration + effect data
- [ ] Selection hierarchy is the single source of truth for routing — no parallel routing tables
- [ ] All existing selection tests pass without modification (behavior-preserving refactor)
- [ ] View code total < 500 lines (down from 1089)
- [ ] build-chain-selection defmethod count reduced by at least 50%
- [ ] All tests passing (make validate)
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO new selection types for effects that fit zone-pick/accumulator/reorder patterns (growth: derive declarations only, not new defmethods)
- ❌ NO separate routing table alongside the multimethod hierarchy (complection: one dispatch mechanism, not two)
- ❌ NO type-specific event dispatches in generic view components (coupling: views dispatch generic toggle/confirm events only)
- ❌ NO obnoxious data DSL on effects (simplicity: effects keep current field shapes, builders read existing fields)
- ❌ NO breaking hierarchy precedence by registering defmethods that shadow derive declarations silently (clarity: custom overrides are intentional and documented)
- ❌ NO mixing UX redesign into this structural refactor (scope: this epic fixes architecture, UX changes are a separate effort)

## Approach
Use Clojure's multimethod hierarchy (derive) to route effect types to interaction patterns. Three generic builders read standard effect fields to construct selection state. Three generic executors read builder-set fields to apply selections. Three generic view components dispatch on :selection/pattern. Custom handlers for genuinely unique interactions (storm-split, chain-bounce) register on specific keywords and take precedence automatically. Chaining is simplified by making multi-step reorder internal and using remaining-effects for sequential interactive effects.

## Architecture
- selection/core.cljs: hierarchy definition (derive declarations), build-selection-for-effect multimethod with hierarchy, three generic builder functions, three generic executor functions
- selection/library.cljs: custom builders/executors only (Intuition pile-choice, unique reorder logic)
- selection/zone_ops.cljs: custom builders/executors only (chain-bounce)
- selection/costs.cljs: custom builders/executors only (mana-allocation)
- selection/storm.cljs: custom builder/executor only (storm-split)
- views/selection/zone_pick.cljs: generic card-grid modal component
- views/selection/reorder.cljs: generic pile-assignment modal component
- views/selection/accumulator.cljs: generic stepper modal component
- views/selection/common.cljs: shared helpers (confirm-button, cancel-button, modal-wrapper)
- views/selection/custom.cljs: genuinely unique modals (player-target, mode-selector, custom overrides)

## Design Rationale
### Problem
The selection system grows linearly with effect count. 21 selection types exist but only 3 interaction patterns. ~80% of selection code (~1350/1700 lines) is structural repetition. Each new interactive effect requires ~120 lines of builder + executor + view boilerplate even when the player interaction is identical to existing effects. View layer is a 1089-line monolith with card grid layout copy-pasted 7 times.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- events/selection/ (6 modules, ~1700 lines) — 21 selection types, 11 build-selection-for-effect defmethods, 9 build-chain-selection defmethods
- views/modals.cljs (1089 lines) — 21 render-selection-modal defmethods, card grid pattern copy-pasted 7 times
- Only ~350 lines are genuinely unique logic across the entire selection system
- Three interaction patterns identified: zone-pick (pick N from zone X with filter), accumulator (distribute N across targets), reorder (sort cards into piles)
- Validation is already fully data-driven via :selection/validation field
- Effect data already contains sufficient fields for generic builders (no new fields needed)
- build-selection-for-effect :default already exists and does basic translation

**External:**
- Clojure multimethod hierarchies (derive) provide exactly the dispatch mechanism needed — parent/child routing with automatic precedence for more specific keywords

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Pattern-based routing via multimethod hierarchy ✓

**What it is:** Use Clojure derive to create effect-type → interaction-pattern hierarchy. Three generic builders/executors/views handle common cases. Custom defmethods override for complex cases. One dispatch mechanism.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed all 21 selection types — they map to 3 patterns
- Verified effect data already has fields needed by generic builders
- Confirmed Clojure derive gives automatic precedence to specific keywords over parents

**Pros:**
- One dispatch mechanism (no two systems fighting)
- Zero code for new common-pattern effects
- Existing escape hatch (multimethod) handles complex cases
- Effect data unchanged (no obnoxious DSL)

**Cons:**
- Hierarchy needs maintenance (one derive line per effect type)
- Migration touches multiple files

**Chosen because:** Simplest approach that solves the linear growth problem. Uses existing Clojure mechanisms rather than inventing new ones.

#### 2. Convention-based inference ❌

**What it is:** Default builder infers interaction pattern from effect fields (has :effect/zone + :effect/count → zone-pick).

**Why we looked at this:** Fewer artifacts to maintain than a routing table.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed effect field patterns — overlap exists but edge cases are ambiguous
- Some effects have fields that could match multiple patterns

**Pros:**
- No routing table or hierarchy to maintain

**Cons:**
- Implicit — hard to debug when inference is wrong
- Edge cases require special handling
- Feels magical

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Implicit convention fights the 'make protocols explicit' design principle. When inference is wrong, failure is silent.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Effect field shapes become fully standardized with no ambiguity.

#### 3. Declared interaction on effects ❌

**What it is:** Effects explicitly add :effect/interaction :zone-pick. Most explicit and self-documenting.

**Why we looked at this:** Maximum explicitness per effect.

**Investigation:**
- Would require adding field to every interactive effect definition across all card files
- User explicitly flagged concern about 'obnoxious data patterns'

**Pros:**
- Self-documenting at the effect level
- No central registry to maintain

**Cons:**
- Adds boilerplate to every card's effect definitions
- Central hierarchy is actually easier to maintain than per-effect annotations

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Trades one form of per-effect boilerplate for another. The hierarchy is a better single source of truth.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Card definitions become auto-generated from an external source where the annotation cost is zero.

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:**
- Event layer: hierarchy, generic builders, generic executors, chaining simplification
- View layer: generic components, monolith split, event decoupling
- Selection system structural refactoring only

**Out of scope (deferred/never):**
- UX redesign (separate effort that benefits from this foundation)
- Effect system changes (effect data shapes stay as-is)
- Pre-cast pipeline changes (ADR-015 already handles that)
- New card implementations (this epic doesn't add cards)

### Open Questions
- Exact boundary for which effects stay custom vs. go generic (resolve during implementation by testing each conversion)
- Whether mana-allocation fits accumulator pattern or stays fully custom (likely custom due to color-bucket semantics)
- Subscription layer consolidation (may fall out naturally or need explicit work)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Where does friction show up? | Discover effects dont work, build new methods, system expanding linearly | Problem is linear growth of selection types, not inherent complexity |
| Are interaction patterns genuinely different? | Mostly same or similar patterns | Confirms consolidation into 3 generic patterns is viable |
| Data-driven effects resonate? | Directionally right, worried about escape hatch | Need custom tier alongside data-driven tier |
| Two dispatch layers concern? | Yes, two systems fighting | Must use single dispatch mechanism (hierarchy) |
| Executor side same hierarchy? | Makes sense | One hierarchy drives both builder and executor dispatch |
| Eliminate most chaining? | Right approach | Multi-step reorder internal, sequential effects use remaining-effects |
| View layer generic components? | Looks right | 3 generic components, split monolith, decouple events |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### Selection Type Taxonomy
**Question explored:** What are all 21 selection types and how do they cluster?
**Sources consulted:**
- events/selection/ (6 modules) — full enumeration of defmethods
- views/modals.cljs — full enumeration of render defmethods

**Findings:**
- 21 types cluster into 3 patterns: zone-pick (7), accumulator (3), reorder (3), plus genuinely unique (8)
- Of the 8 unique: 2 are invisible (unless-pay, mana-allocation), 2 are player/mode choice, 4 are truly custom
- ~80% of code is structural repetition

**Conclusion:** Three generic builders + executors + views handle the majority of selection types

#### Chaining Analysis
**Question explored:** Can chaining be eliminated?
**Sources consulted:**
- 9 build-chain-selection defmethods across selection modules

**Findings:**
- Reorder chains (scry→order-bottom, peek→order-bottom) are internal to reorder pattern
- Effect pipeline chains (tutor→pile-choice) can use remaining-effects
- Pre-cast chains handled by ADR-015

**Conclusion:** Most chaining is unnecessary with proper pattern design

### Dead-End Paths

#### Obnoxious Data DSL
**Why explored:** Initial instinct to make effects declare selection needs as rich data
**Investigation:** User flagged concern about complex data patterns being just as bad as complex code
**Why abandoned:** Effect data already has the right shape — the builder translation is the waste, not missing data

### Open Concerns Raised
- 'Worried about mixing UX redesign with structural fix' → Scoped out explicitly, separate effort
- 'Obnoxious data pattern concern' → Resolved by using existing effect fields, no new DSL
- 'Two dispatch layers fighting' → Resolved by using single multimethod hierarchy

Selection hierarchy with derive-based routing, 3 interaction patterns (zone-pick, accumulator, reorder), chain reduction 8→4, view monolith split 1089→755 lines. 2124 tests passing. View line target (500) accepted at 755 — irreducible floor given anti-pattern constraints.

title: Migrate :graveyard-return to zone-pick hierarchy
description:
## Goal
Migrate :graveyard-return from a custom builder to the generic zone-pick builder via hierarchy derive. This is the second zone-pick type (after :discard), proving the pattern works for different zone directions (graveyard to hand vs hand to graveyard).

## SRE Refinement Analysis

### Types Evaluated for Zone-Pick Migration
Of the five types mentioned in the original proposal, only :graveyard-return fits zone-pick cleanly:

| Type | Fits Zone-Pick? | Reason |
|------|----------------|--------|
| :graveyard-return | YES | Standard effect selection, moves cards between zones |
| :discard-specific-cost | NO | Pre-cast pipeline cost, different invocation path (direct call, not build-selection-for-effect), executor calls rules/cast-spell-mode, group validation |
| :return-land-cost | NO | Pre-cast pipeline cost, executor calls rules/cast-spell-mode, chaining to targeting |
| :exile-cards-cost | NO | Pre-cast pipeline cost, executor calls rules/cast-spell-mode, stores X value |
| :hand-reveal-discard | NO | Genuinely different UX (caster chooses from opponent revealed hand), criteria filtering, exact-or-zero validation, auto-confirm on empty |

The three cost types flow through evaluate-pre-cast-step in game.cljs, NOT through build-selection-for-effect. They take (game-db, player-id, object-id, mode, cost-map) - a completely different signature. Their executors embed casting logic (rules/cast-spell-mode). They are NOT selection-for-effect types and should NOT derive from :zone-pick.

:hand-reveal-discard is an effect selection type but its interaction pattern is genuinely different: opponent hand reveal + criteria filtering + zero-or-exact. It may warrant its own pattern or stay custom.

### :graveyard-return Zone-Pick Differences
The generic zone-pick builder needs field overrides for :graveyard-return:
- Source zone: :graveyard (not default :hand)
- Target zone: :hand (not default :graveyard)
- Validation: :at-most (not default :exact) - player can choose 0 to N
- Has :selection/candidate-ids - computed from graveyard contents
- Resolves :effect/target for opponent targeting (self/opponent/player-id)

### Approach: Parameterize Generic Builder
The generic zone-pick builder reads a config map keyed by effect type to override defaults. This keeps card effect data clean (no new DSL fields) while allowing per-type zone semantics.

zone-pick-config in core.cljs:
- Default: {:source-zone :hand :target-zone :graveyard :validation :exact :card-source :hand}
- :graveyard-return: {:source-zone :graveyard :target-zone :hand :validation :at-most :card-source :zone :min-count 0}

## Effort Estimate
3-4 hours

## Implementation

1. Write tests first (RED phase):
   - Test zone-pick-config lookup for :graveyard-return
   - Test generic zone-pick builder with :graveyard-return effect: correct source-zone, target-zone, validation, candidate-ids
   - Test opponent targeting resolution in generic builder
   - Test generic executor handles graveyard-return (cards move graveyard to hand)
   - Test hierarchy routing: :graveyard-return routes to zone-pick builder
   - Integration test: Ill-Gotten Gains graveyard-return through full production path

2. Add zone-pick-config map to core.cljs

3. Enhance generic zone-pick builder:
   - Read config for effect type
   - Resolve target player from :effect/target
   - Query candidates from source zone
   - Set :selection/candidate-ids
   - Set :selection/min-count from config

4. Add (derive :graveyard-return :zone-pick) to hierarchy

5. Remove custom builder and executor from zone_ops.cljs

6. Run make validate

## Success Criteria
- [ ] :graveyard-return derives from :zone-pick in hierarchy
- [ ] Generic zone-pick builder handles :graveyard-return with correct zones, validation, candidates
- [ ] Generic zone-pick executor handles :graveyard-return (no custom executor)
- [ ] zone-pick-config data map in core.cljs (extensible for future types)
- [ ] Custom build-graveyard-selection and execute :graveyard-return removed from zone_ops.cljs
- [ ] All existing graveyard-return tests pass unchanged
- [ ] Ill-Gotten Gains integration test passes
- [ ] No new fields added to card effect definitions
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-Patterns
- NO adding :effect/source-zone or :effect/target-zone to card definitions (compute from config)
- NO modifying the three cost types (pre-cast pipeline, different invocation path)
- NO migrating :hand-reveal-discard (genuinely different pattern)
- NO breaking the sync test (exclude :zone-pick from dispatch enumeration - already done)
close_reason: Closed

title: Bit-level encoding primitives (BitWriter/BitReader)
description:
Create snapshot/encoding.cljs with bit-level binary encoding utilities for compact game state serialization.

## What to build
- BitWriter: accumulates bits into a JS Uint8Array. API: write-bits(writer, value, bit-count), to-bytes(writer) → Uint8Array
- BitReader: reads bits from a Uint8Array. API: read-bits(reader, bit-count) → value, remaining?(reader) → boolean
- base64url-encode: Uint8Array → URL-safe base64 string (A-Z a-z 0-9 - _ no padding)
- base64url-decode: string → Uint8Array
- Both writer/reader use mutable JS state internally (atom wrapping {buffer, bit-position}) for performance
- No external dependencies — pure JS interop (Uint8Array, DataView, btoa/atob)

## Implementation notes
- ClojureScript interop with JS typed arrays: (js/Uint8Array. size)
- For base64url: use js/btoa on String.fromCharCode for encode, js/atob for decode, then replace +/= with -_
- BitWriter should auto-grow its buffer (double when full)
- Bit order: MSB first within each byte (network byte order)

## Verification
- Round-trip: write-bits then read-bits recovers exact values
- Handles values at bit boundaries (e.g., 6-bit value of 63, 8-bit value of 255)
- base64url round-trip preserves bytes exactly
- Empty writes produce empty output
close_reason: Closed

title: Priority System: Real Priority Passing
description:
Replace the current 'next phase' button with a real MTG priority system. Both players must pass priority simultaneously for phases to advance.

## Core Mechanics
- **Yield** (empty stack): Pass priority. If both players pass, advance to next phase.
- **Yield All** (non-empty stack): Resolve everything on the stack, retain priority in current phase.
- **Hold Priority**: Set stops on specific phases (like MTGO stops). E.g., 'hold priority at end of opponent turn.'
- Bot priority: Bots have their own stops (burn bot holds at end of player turn to cast bolts).

## Current State
- Active player/priority exist in schema (:game/active-player, :game/priority) but are hardwired to :player-1
- Phase progression via next-phase/advance-phase in events/game.cljs (lines 534-563)
- 8 phases: untap → upkeep → draw → main1 → combat → main2 → end → cleanup
- Phase advance blocked when stack non-empty (already correct behavior)
- Mana pool cleared on every phase change

## What Changes
- Remove 'next phase' button, replace with yield/yield-all controls
- Priority passing logic: both players must pass for phase advance
- Hold priority feature (phase stops)
- UI awareness of priority state
- Foundation that bots plug into for their own priority decisions

## Notes
- This is the foundation everything else in the bot tranche builds on
- Validate with existing goldfish experience before adding bot complexity
- Clearest scope of the 5 epics in this tranche
design:
# Priority System Implementation Plan

- 0 test refs to ::advance-phase, ::resolve-top, ::resolve-all, ::start-turn

## Step Group 1: Schema Additions

## Step Group 2: Priority Engine (TDD)

Create: src/main/fizzle/engine/priority.cljs
Create: src/test/fizzle/engine/priority_test.cljs

Pure functions operating on game-db (Datascript):
- yield-priority [db player-eid] — add to :game/passed set via [:db/add]
- both-passed? [db] — count passed >= 2
- transfer-priority [db current-eid] — set :game/priority to other player
- reset-passes [db] — retract all :game/passed values
- get-priority-holder-eid [db] — query :game/priority ref
- get-passed-eids [db] — query :game/passed refs as set
- check-stop [db player-eid phase] — check :player/stops contains phase
- get-auto-mode [db] — query :game/auto-mode
- set-auto-mode [db mode] — set :game/auto-mode
- clear-auto-mode [db] — retract :game/auto-mode

Test cases:

## Step Group 3: Bot Protocol (TDD)

Create: src/test/fizzle/bots/protocol_test.cljs

- :goldfish method returns :pass
- :default method returns :pass

Test cases:

- :goldfish archetype returns :pass
- unknown archetype defaults to :pass

## Step Group 4: Phase Stops Storage (TDD)

Modify: src/main/fizzle/storage.cljs
Create: src/test/fizzle/storage_test.cljs

- default-stops [] — returns {:player #{:main1 :main2} :opponent #{}}

Test cases:

- toggle adds missing stop, removes existing stop

## Step Group 5: Game Init + Test Helpers

Modify: src/test/fizzle/test_helpers.cljs

test_helpers changes:
- add-opponent: add opts arity with :bot-archetype and :stops support
  Keep 0-arity backward compat

events/game.cljs init-game-state changes:
- Add fizzle.storage to requires

- After line 165: load stops from localStorage, apply to both players

## Step Group 6: ::yield Event (TDD) — CORE

Create: src/test/fizzle/events/priority_test.cljs
Modify: src/main/fizzle/events/game.cljs

Add requires: fizzle.engine.priority, fizzle.bots.protocol, fizzle.storage

Helper: advance-with-stops [game-db app-db]
- At cleanup: opponent-draw + start-turn, clear auto-mode (turn boundary)
- Advancing to cleanup: advance-phase + begin-cleanup, handle pending-selection
- Normal advance: advance-phase, check stop + stack-triggered
  - stop hit (and not F6): give priority
  - stack triggered: give priority, clear auto-mode
  - no stop: return :continue-yield? true

Core: yield-impl [app-db]
1. Get priority holder, add to passed set
2. If not both-passed and opponent is bot: auto-pass for bot
3. If both-passed:
   a. Reset passes
   b. Stack non-empty: resolve-one-item
      - pending-selection: clear auto-mode, return selection

4. If not both-passed: transfer-priority

Event: ::yield (reg-event-fx)
- Block on pending-selection
- Call yield-impl
- If :continue-yield? → {:fx [[:dispatch [::yield]]]}

Test cases:

- yield at main2: advances through end → cleanup → new turn → main1
- yield with selection-needed spell: returns pending-selection, breaks auto-mode
- cast spell retains priority (no auto-yield)

## Step Group 10: Integration Tests + Cleanup

Integration tests:
- Full cast → yield → resolve → mana added flow
- F6 through full turn cycle
- Stop toggle updates game state

- Verify no remaining UI dispatches of old events in views/

## Success Criteria

2. Phase pills clickable to toggle stops with dot indicator
3. Yield passes priority → advances phase when both pass on empty stack
4. Yield passes priority → resolves top when both pass on non-empty stack

title: Unify cost systems: route spell additional costs through costs.cljs multimethods
design:
## Goal
Route spell additional cost checking/payment (currently case-based in rules.cljs) through the costs.cljs multimethod system. After this task, can-pay-additional-cost? and pay-additional-cost in rules.cljs delegate to costs.cljs multimethods instead of duplicating cost logic.

## Effort Estimate
3-5 hours

## Current State (2 parallel systems)

### System 1: Multimethod (costs.cljs) - THE RIGHT PATTERN
- can-pay? multimethod dispatching on (first (keys cost))
- pay-cost multimethod dispatching on (first (keys cost))
- 6 types: :tap, :remove-counter, :sacrifice-self, :discard-hand, :pay-life, :mana
- Data shape: {:pay-life N} or {:tap true}
- Used by: abilities.cljs (activated ability costs)

### System 2: Case-based (rules.cljs:111-177) - DUPLICATE
- can-pay-additional-cost? with case on :cost/type
- pay-additional-cost with case on :cost/type
- 2 types: :pay-life, :exile-cards
- Data shape: {:cost/type :pay-life :cost/amount N} or {:cost/type :exile-cards :cost/zone Z ...}
- Used by: can-cast-mode? (spell casting validation)

### Key Difference: Data Shapes
- costs.cljs uses {:pay-life 10} (cost-type as key, value as value)
- rules.cljs uses {:cost/type :pay-life :cost/amount 10} (namespaced map)

### CRITICAL: :exile-cards is special
pay-additional-cost for :exile-cards returns db unchanged. Actual exile is deferred to event layer selection system. The multimethod just needs to handle can-pay? (validate targets exist) and pay-cost (no-op/return db).

## Implementation

### Step 1: Study existing code (READ ONLY)
- engine/costs.cljs full file, understand multimethod dispatch pattern
- engine/rules.cljs:111-177 can-pay-additional-cost?, pay-additional-cost
- engine/rules.cljs:188-196 can-cast-mode? (caller)
- engine/rules.cljs:355-395 pay-additional-costs (caller)
- Card defs using :mode/additional-costs to understand data shapes

### Step 2: Write failing tests (RED)
Add tests to costs_test.cljs:
- can-pay? :exile-cards with enough targets returns true
- can-pay? :exile-cards with insufficient targets returns false
- pay-cost :exile-cards returns db unchanged (deferred to selection layer)

### Step 3: Add :exile-cards defmethods to costs.cljs (GREEN)
- can-pay? checks if enough matching objects exist in the specified zone
- pay-cost returns db unchanged (actual exile deferred to event layer)

### Step 4: Update rules.cljs to delegate
Replace can-pay-additional-cost? and pay-additional-cost with calls to costs.cljs multimethods.
Need adapter: convert {:cost/type :pay-life :cost/amount N} to {:pay-life N} format.

### Step 5: Verify (make validate)
All existing tests must pass.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] costs.cljs has can-pay? and pay-cost defmethods for :exile-cards
- [ ] can-pay-additional-cost? in rules.cljs delegates to costs.cljs (no case dispatch)
- [ ] pay-additional-cost in rules.cljs delegates to costs.cljs (no case dispatch)
- [ ] New tests for :exile-cards cost type in costs_test.cljs
- [ ] All existing tests pass (make test)
- [ ] make validate passes

## Anti-patterns
- NO changing card definition data shapes (:mode/additional-costs format stays as-is)
- NO case/cond dispatch on cost types in rules.cljs (delegate to multimethods)
- NO breaking the :exile-cards deferred payment pattern (pay-cost must return db unchanged)
close_reason: Unified cost systems: added :exile-cards defmethods to costs.cljs, rules.cljs now delegates can-pay-additional-cost? and pay-additional-cost through costs.cljs multimethods via adapter function. 7 new tests, all 1442 tests pass, make validate clean.

title: Storm copy target inheritance and cleanup
design:
## Goal
Complete the remaining epic criteria: propagate targets through storm copies, simplify descriptions.cljs, and remove :object/targets from schema.

## Context
From fizzle-e2em: targets now stored on :stack-item/targets. Bridge pattern copies targets to :object/targets for execute-effect. Storm copies need targets propagated. Schema cleanup depends on all consumers migrated.

## Implementation

### 1. Storm copy target inheritance (engine/triggers.cljs)
- In create-spell-copy (triggers.cljs:35-74):
  a. Find the original spell's stack-item to get its :stack-item/targets
  b. Query for stack-item by :stack-item/source matching source-object-id
  c. Copy :stack-item/targets from original to the new copy's stack-item
  d. Need to add the targets AFTER create-stack-item (line 70-74)
- Note: currently Brain Freeze uses fixed :effect/target :opponent so this is forward-looking

### 2. History descriptions cleanup (history/descriptions.cljs)
- Simplify get-stack-item-targets (descriptions.cljs:40-47)
- Remove the object-targets fallback — just read :stack-item/targets directly
- This was the original workaround that caused the bug

### 3. Schema cleanup (db/schema.cljs)
- Remove :object/targets from schema
- IMPORTANT: The bridge in resolution.cljs still writes :object/targets
- The bridge is needed because effects/execute-effect reads :object/targets for :targeted-player
- With schema entry removed, d/db-with will still work (Datascript allows unschema'd attributes)
- BUT: removing from schema is a signal that it's deprecated
- Check if any tests manually set :object/targets — they need updating

### 4. Update tests that manually set :object/targets
- orims_chant_test.cljs:217 — manually stores on object for rules/resolve-spell path
- targeting_test.cljs — tests all-targets-legal? with old signature using :object/targets
- deep_analysis_test.cljs:492 — calls all-targets-legal? with object-id

## Success Criteria
- [ ] create-spell-copy propagates :stack-item/targets from original to copy
- [ ] get-stack-item-targets reads only from :stack-item/targets (no fallback)
- [ ] :object/targets removed from db/schema.cljs
- [ ] Tests that manually set :object/targets updated to use stack-item
- [ ] make validate passes
close_reason: Storm copy target inheritance implemented: targets propagated from original to copy in create-spell-copy. descriptions.cljs simplified (no object fallback). all-targets-legal? 2-arity removed (3-arity only). :object/targets removed from schema. Tests updated to use stack-item targets. All 1270 tests pass, make validate clean.

title: ::yield event + history update
description: Core priority passing event. ::yield: add priority holder to :game/passed, check if both passed. If yes: stack non-empty → resolve-one-item, stack empty → advance-phase. If no: transfer priority, if opponent is bot dispatch ::bot-priority-decision. ::bot-priority-decision: call bot-priority-decision multimethod, dispatch ::yield on behalf of bot if :pass. Update history interceptor: replace priority-events set with principled rule (entry when priority returns to active player after being elsewhere).
close_reason: Premature decomposition

title: Extract remaining core.cljs zone components (battlefield, graveyard, stack, controls, turn-bar)
design:
## Goal
Extract ALL remaining view components from core.cljs into dedicated view files under views/. Migrate every inline :style map to Tailwind utility classes with @theme tokens. After this task, core.cljs contains only the app shell (requires + composition), no rendering logic.

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours

## Context
Three extractions completed (opponent.cljs, hand.cljs, mana_pool.cljs) established a clear pattern:
- Namespace with re-frame requires
- Tailwind classes via :class strings (no :style maps)
- @theme tokens for all colors
- Pure functions extracted and tested separately
- Layout-agnostic components (no grid/position knowledge)

## Implementation

### 1. Add new @theme tokens to src/css/app.css

Add these tokens to the @theme block:

```css

--color-mana-bg-blue: #2a4a6a;

/* Disabled/dim UI states */
--color-btn-disabled-bg: #222;
--color-surface-dim: #333;
--color-perm-text: #ccc;
```

Total: 13 new tokens. Token reuse for existing colors:
- #555 -> border (existing)
- #666 -> perm-text-tapped (existing)

- #999 -> text-label (existing)
- #eee -> text (existing)
- #aaa -> text-muted (existing)
- #1a1a2a -> surface-raised (existing)
- #2a2a3a -> surface-hover (existing, exact match for flashback-castable bg)
- #4A9BD9 -> accent (existing, for active phase bg)
- #FFD700 -> border-selected (existing)
- #2a6a2a -> btn-enabled-bg (existing)
- #444 -> perm-border-tapped (existing)

### 2. Create views/battlefield.cljs

## Success Criteria
- [ ] views/battlefield.cljs exists with battlefield-view, permanent-view, mana-button, ability-button, get-producible-colors, get-activated-abilities, mana-bg-class
- [ ] views/graveyard.cljs exists with graveyard-view and graveyard-card-view
- [ ] views/stack.cljs exists with stack-view and stack-item-view
- [ ] views/phase_bar.cljs exists with phase-bar-view and phase-indicator
- [ ] views/controls.cljs exists with controls-view
- [ ] 13 new @theme tokens added to app.css (mana-bg-*, gy-*, btn-disabled-bg, surface-dim, perm-text, flashback-badge-bg)
- [ ] core.cljs reduced to app shell: only app, mount-root, init functions remain (no game rendering logic)
- [ ] Zero inline :style maps in ALL new view files
- [ ] Zero inline :style maps in core.cljs
- [ ] No hardcoded hex colors in any new view file (all @theme tokens)
- [ ] All components layout-agnostic (no grid/position styling)
- [ ] battlefield_test.cljs has tests for get-producible-colors (5+ cases), get-activated-abilities (4+ cases), mana-bg-class (6+ cases)
- [ ] No changes to event/subscription contracts
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + tests)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO inline :style maps (use Tailwind utility classes)
- NO hardcoded hex colors (use @theme tokens)
- NO layout positioning in components (grid parent handles placement)
- NO game logic in views (subscribe and dispatch only)
- NO breaking existing subscription/event contracts
- NO dynamic Tailwind class construction via str+name (e.g., (str "bg-mana-bg-" (name color))) — Tailwind cannot detect dynamically generated class names at build time; use case/cond with literal strings
- NO Tailwind v3 patterns (tailwind.config.js, @tailwind directives)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Tailwind Class Detection:**
Tailwind v4 scans compiled JS for class name string literals. All class names must appear as complete strings in source code. The mana-bg-class function MUST use case with literal strings like "bg-mana-bg-blue", NOT (str "bg-mana-bg-" (name color)). This is the same pattern used successfully in mana_pool.cljs mana-color-class.

**mana-color-display Constant Scope:**
The mana-color-display map (W/U/B/R/G symbols + hex colors) is only used by mana-button in core.cljs. When moved to battlefield.cljs, the hex color values become unnecessary (replaced by Tailwind classes). Only the symbol characters are still needed. Simplify to just symbols if :color and :bg are eliminated. BUT: mana_pool.cljs already has its own mana-symbols map. battlefield.cljs can define its own or import from mana_pool. Since the battlefield needs symbol + bg-class pairing, a local mana-button-config or just using the mana-bg-class function with a separate symbol lookup is cleaner.

**events/phases Reference:**
turn-bar (line 283) references events/phases which is a def in fizzle.events.game. phase_bar.cljs must require [fizzle.events.game :as events] and use events/phases. Verify this var is public (not defn-).

**Private vs Public Functions:**
get-producible-colors and get-activated-abilities are defn- in core.cljs. When moved to battlefield.cljs they can remain defn- since only battlefield-view calls them. However, to test them, either:
1. Make them public (defn) — preferred for testability, follows opponent.cljs pattern
2. Test indirectly via battlefield-view — requires re-frame test infrastructure

Recommendation: Make get-producible-colors, get-activated-abilities, and mana-bg-class public (defn) for direct testing. Keep mana-button, ability-button, permanent-view as private (defn-).

**Graveyard Flashback Background Color:**
graveyard-card-view line 164 uses #2a2a3a for flashback-castable bg. This is an EXACT match for the existing surface-hover token (#2a2a3a). Reuse bg-surface-hover instead of creating a new token. Only the non-flashback graveyard card bg (#2a1a2a) needs a new gy-card-bg token.

**rotate Transform for Tapped Permanents:**
permanent-view line 111 uses :transform "rotate(6deg)" for tapped state. Tailwind arbitrary value: rotate-[6deg]. This works in Tailwind v4. Verify compiled CSS includes it.

**Bare Requires After Extraction:**
core.cljs currently has bare require [fizzle.events.selection] and [fizzle.events.abilities]. After extraction, battlefield.cljs requires abilities directly. But the bare require in core.cljs ensures the namespace is loaded for re-frame event registration. Keep bare requires in core.cljs for any event namespaces not directly required by view files.
Check: events.selection is required bare — no view file dispatches selection events directly (modals.cljs handles that). Keep it bare in core.cljs.
Check: events.abilities is now required by battlefield.cljs. Can remove bare require from core.cljs IF battlefield.cljs is loaded. But since core.cljs requires battlefield, the transitive require covers it. Safe to remove bare [fizzle.events.abilities] from core.cljs.

**font-family monospace in app:**
core.cljs line 339 has :font-family "monospace". Tailwind class: font-mono. This goes in the app root div.
close_reason: All success criteria met. 5 new view files created (battlefield, graveyard, stack, phase_bar, controls). core.cljs reduced to 53-line app shell. 13 new @theme tokens. Zero inline :style maps in new files or core.cljs. 16 new battlefield tests. make validate passes (996 tests, 2262 assertions).

title: Task 2: Implement Ray of Revelation card
design:
## Goal
Implement Ray of Revelation card with Scryfall-accurate oracle text, type-based enchantment targeting, and flashback.

## Effort Estimate
2-4 hours

## Context
Completed task fizzle-0uj: :destroy effect type now exists and handles target-ref resolution.

## Implementation

### 1. Use implement-card skill
REQUIRED: Use the implement-card skill for Scryfall oracle validation.
The skill will fetch oracle text and validate card definition.

### 2. Card definition structure
File: src/fizzle/cards/ray_of_revelation.cljs (new file)

Following deep_analysis.cljs and recoup.cljs patterns:

```clojure
(ns fizzle.cards.ray-of-revelation)

(def ray-of-revelation
  {:card/id :ray-of-revelation
   :card/name "Ray of Revelation"
   :card/cmc 2
   :card/mana-cost {:colorless 1 :white 1}
   :card/colors #{:white}
   :card/types #{:instant}
   :card/text "Destroy target enchantment.\nFlashback {G}"
   
   ;; Type-based targeting for enchantments on battlefield
   :card/targeting 
   [{:target/id :target-enchantment
     :target/type :object
     :target/zone :battlefield
     :target/controller :any
     :target/criteria {:card/types #{:enchantment}}
     :target/required true}]
   
   ;; Destroy effect using target-ref
   :card/effects 
   [{:effect/type :destroy
     :effect/target-ref :target-enchantment}]
   
   ;; Flashback {G} from graveyard, exile on resolve
   :card/alternate-costs 
   [{:alternate/id :flashback
     :alternate/zone :graveyard
     :alternate/mana-cost {:green 1}
     :alternate/on-resolve :exile}]})
```

### 3. Register card
Add to src/fizzle/cards/iggy_pop.cljs card pool (or appropriate registry).

### 4. Write integration tests
File: src/test/fizzle/cards/ray_of_revelation_test.cljs (new file)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] implement-card skill validates oracle text matches Scryfall
- [ ] test-ray-can-cast-targeting-enchantment passes
- [ ] test-ray-cannot-cast-without-enchantment-on-battlefield passes
- [ ] test-ray-flashback-casts-from-graveyard passes
- [ ] test-ray-exiles-after-flashback-resolution passes
- [ ] test-ray-rejects-non-enchantment-targets passes
- [ ] make validate passes (lint + format + all tests)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: No enchantments on battlefield**
- Card should NOT be castable when no valid targets exist
- Test: has-valid-targets? returns false with empty battlefield
- Reference: targeting.cljs:80 has-valid-targets?

**Edge Case: Target removed before resolution**
- If enchantment destroyed/exiled in response, effect fizzles
- :destroy effect already handles this (returns db unchanged if target missing)
- Test: Verify spell resolves gracefully when target gone

**Edge Case: Flashback with no targets**
- Same as regular cast - cannot cast flashback if no valid targets
- Flashback changes cost and zone, not targeting requirements

**Oracle Text Accuracy**
- MUST use implement-card skill to validate against Scryfall
- Card text must match exactly: "Destroy target enchantment.\nFlashback {G}"
- Anti-pattern: NO hardcoding text without Scryfall validation

**Reference Implementations**
- src/fizzle/cards/recoup.cljs - Flashback pattern
- src/fizzle/cards/deep_analysis.cljs - Alternate costs with exile
- src/test/fizzle/engine/targeting_test.cljs - Type-based targeting tests

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO hardcoding oracle text without Scryfall validation
- ❌ NO skipping implement-card skill
- ❌ NO targeting artifacts (Ray only targets enchantments)
- ❌ NO :effect/target (must use :effect/target-ref with stored targets)
- ❌ NO manual card text that doesn't match Scryfall exactly
